title: Patience Isn't Waiting... It's Trusting
excerpt: Patience-either you have it or you don't.  Which are you?
content:
Patience has never been one of my greatest strengths. If I'm being completely honest, it still isn't. I have always been someone who likes to solve problems, make decisions, and move forward. Waiting has always felt like wasted time. Whether it was waiting for test results, waiting for an answer to a prayer, waiting for an opportunity, or simply sitting in traffic, patience has always been something I have had to work on rather than something that came naturally.

Years ago, I remember sitting with a priest during a conversation about faith. Somewhere along the way I mentioned that I should probably start praying for patience. He smiled, paused for a moment, and said something I have never forgotten.

"Be careful what you pray for. Every time people pray for patience, God usually gives them opportunities to practice it."

At the time I laughed because I thought he was joking.

Now, after nearly five decades of life, I think he knew exactly what he was talking about.

Growing up, my mom had a phrase that became part of our daily conversations. Whenever I wanted something immediately or became frustrated because life wasn't moving as quickly as I thought it should, she would simply say, "Nick, be patient."

As a child, those words were frustrating. As a teenager, they sounded like another lecture from a parent who just didn't understand. After all, when you're young, everything feels urgent. Summer vacation can't come fast enough. You can't wait to turn sixteen so you can drive. Then it's eighteen so you can graduate. Then twenty-one. Then your first job. Your first apartment. Your first house. Your first real relationship. Life feels like one long countdown to the next milestone.

Tell an eighteen-year-old to have patience, and chances are you'll get a look that says, "Easy for you to say."

The truth is, I probably reacted the same way.

When we're young, we believe life is supposed to happen on our schedule. We expect doors to open when we knock. We expect dreams to become reality simply because we work hard enough. We don't yet understand that some of life's greatest lessons can only be learned through waiting.

As I've grown older, I've started to appreciate what my mom was really trying to teach me. Patience isn't about sitting still and doing nothing. It isn't about giving up on your dreams or pretending you don't care. Patience is trusting that not everything worth having arrives on your timetable. Sometimes life needs time to prepare us before it delivers the very thing we've been asking for.

Looking back over my own life, I can see countless moments where I desperately wanted God to move faster. I wanted answers immediately. I wanted healing immediately. I wanted clarity immediately. Instead, there were seasons where all I could do was wait.

Cancer certainly taught me that lesson.

Healing doesn't happen overnight. Scans don't come back instantly. Treatments don't magically erase fear. There were days when I wanted the future to arrive simply so I could stop wondering what came next. Yet during those seasons of waiting, I discovered something unexpected. God often does His deepest work in the quiet moments between the prayers and the answers.

Loss has taught me patience as well. Losing people we love changes us forever, and grief refuses to follow our schedule. You cannot rush healing after losing a parent, a grandparent, a friend, or someone who helped shape your life. There is no finish line where someone hands you a certificate declaring that you've grieved enough. Healing unfolds slowly, often when we least expect it, one small step at a time.

Even becoming a father figure later in life has reminded me that patience is one of the greatest gifts we can give another person. Watching an eighteen-year-old navigate adulthood has caused me to reflect on who I was at that age. I wanted everything figured out immediately. I thought I knew far more than I actually did. Looking back, I realize I was still becoming the person God intended me to be, even though I thought I had already arrived.

The same is true for all of us.

We live in a culture that celebrates speed. We have instant messages, overnight shipping, streaming movies, same-day grocery delivery, and answers to almost any question within seconds. We have become so accustomed to immediate results that waiting almost feels unnatural. Unfortunately, faith doesn't usually operate at internet speed.

God has never been in a hurry.

Throughout Scripture, we see story after story of people who waited. Abraham waited decades for God's promise. Joseph waited years before seeing God's plan unfold. Moses spent forty years in the wilderness before leading Israel. David was anointed king long before he ever wore the crown. Even the disciples spent years walking beside Jesus before they fully understood His purpose.

Waiting has always been part of God's classroom.

Perhaps that is why patience is listed as one of the fruits of the Spirit. It isn't simply a personality trait that some people are lucky enough to possess. It is something God develops within us over time. Every delay, every unanswered prayer, every closed door, and every season of uncertainty becomes another opportunity for Him to shape our character.

That doesn't mean waiting is easy.

There are still moments when I catch myself wanting life to move a little faster. I still find myself asking God, "Why not now?" I still struggle when answers don't come as quickly as I would like. But experience has taught me that many of the blessings I enjoy today would not have meant nearly as much if I had received them years earlier.

I simply wasn't ready.

Sometimes God isn't delaying the blessing because He is withholding something from us. Sometimes He is preparing us so we can fully appreciate what He has planned.

I often think back to my mom's gentle reminder to "be patient." Those two simple words carried far more wisdom than I understood as a child. She wasn't trying to slow my life down. She was teaching me that maturity often grows during the moments we spend waiting rather than during the moments we spend achieving.

So maybe my priest was right all those years ago. Maybe praying for patience isn't dangerous because God enjoys testing us. Maybe He answers that prayer by giving us opportunities to become stronger, wiser, more compassionate, and more trusting than we were before.

I'm still learning that lesson.

I imagine I always will.

But if there's one thing life has taught me, it's this: patience isn't about waiting because we have no other choice. Patience is choosing to trust that even when we cannot see what God is doing, He is still working behind the scenes, preparing something far better than we could have imagined.

Sometimes the greatest miracle isn't that God changes our circumstances quickly. Sometimes the greatest miracle is that He changes us while we wait.

Until next time, remember this: God's timing has never been late. It has simply never been ours.

title: Fear and Discipleship
excerpt: Follow me.  Do Not Be Afraid.
content:
Dr. Nick's Matter of Perspective
Fear: The Greatest Obstacle to Discipleship

Fear is a powerful emotion. It can protect us from danger, but it can also imprison us. More dreams have died because of fear than because of failure. More opportunities have been missed because of fear than because of a lack of ability. And perhaps nowhere is that more evident than in our walk with Christ.

I believe fear is one of the greatest obstacles to discipleship.

When Jesus called ordinary fishermen to leave their nets, they had every reason to be afraid. They were leaving behind security, income, familiarity, and the only life they had ever known. Yet they followed anyway. Throughout Scripture, God repeatedly called ordinary people to do extraordinary things, and almost every one of them initially responded with fear.

Moses feared he wasn't a good speaker.

Joshua feared leading after Moses.

Gideon feared he wasn't strong enough.

Esther feared approaching the king.

Peter feared stepping out of the boat.

Even after witnessing the resurrection, the disciples often found themselves hiding behind locked doors because they were afraid.

Yet God never allowed fear to have the final word.

Many people have heard it said that the phrase "Do not be afraid" appears 365 times in the Bible, one reminder for every day of the year. Whether the exact count varies depending on the Bible translation, the message is unmistakable. God continually tells His people not to fear because He knows fear will constantly try to keep us from trusting Him.

Think about that for a moment.

God knew we would wrestle with fear every single day. Fear of rejection. Fear of failure. Fear of what others might think. Fear of not having enough. Fear of getting hurt again. Fear of stepping into the unknown. So He continually reminds us that His presence is greater than our anxiety.

Fear whispers, "You're not good enough."

Faith replies, "God doesn't call the qualified. He qualifies the called."

Fear says, "What if I fail?"

Faith asks, "What if God uses me anyway?"

Fear tells us to stay comfortable. Jesus tells us to take up our cross.

Being a disciple of Christ has never been about comfort. It has always been about surrender.

Sometimes we imagine discipleship means preaching to thousands or becoming a missionary halfway around the world. While God certainly calls some people to those ministries, discipleship usually begins much closer to home. It starts by forgiving someone who hurt you. It means praying with a friend who's struggling. It means standing up for truth when it isn't popular. It means serving without expecting recognition. It means choosing kindness when anger feels easier. It means trusting God when the future is uncertain.

Every one of those moments requires courage.

The enemy loves fear because fear keeps us silent. A silent Christian shares no testimony. A fearful believer takes no risks. A worried heart spends more time imagining worst-case scenarios than trusting God's promises.

But fear and faith cannot sit on the throne of your heart at the same time.

That doesn't mean courageous Christians never feel afraid. It means they choose obedience even while fear is knocking at the door. Courage isn't the absence of fear. Courage is deciding that Jesus is worthy of our trust despite our fear.

I have experienced fear in my own life more times than I can count. Cancer. Loss. Uncertainty. Career changes. Standing beside people in their darkest moments. There have been countless times I didn't know what tomorrow would hold. But looking back, I can honestly say that God was faithful in every chapter. Not because life was easy, but because He never abandoned me.

Fear focuses on the size of the storm.

Faith focuses on the One who commands the wind and the waves.

If God is calling you to forgive, forgive.

If He's calling you to serve, serve.

If He's calling you to speak, speak.

If He's calling you to step out in faith, take the first step.

You don't have to know every detail of the journey before you begin walking. God rarely gives us the entire roadmap. Instead, He asks us to trust Him with the next step.

Perhaps today your greatest act of discipleship isn't doing something spectacular. Maybe it's simply refusing to let fear make your decisions any longer.

After all, Jesus never said, "Follow Me when you're comfortable."

He simply said, "Follow Me."

And that invitation still echoes today.

I want to talk to you! If this message spoke to your heart, or if fear has been holding you back from the life God is calling you to live, I'd love to hear your story. Reach out to me through Inspire with Dr. Nick. Remember, fear may knock on your door, but it doesn't have to be the one who answers it.

title: The Voice.
excerpt: We all have a voice.  How do you use it?
content:
The Voice

There is a voice that follows each of us every day. It doesn't come from a microphone or a loudspeaker. It doesn't have a recognizable face or a familiar accent. Yet it may be the most influential voice we will ever hear because it lives inside our own minds.

From the moment we wake until we close our eyes at night, that voice is speaking. Sometimes it reminds us of our accomplishments and encourages us to keep going. Other times, it drags us backward, replaying old failures, reminding us of painful memories, and convincing us that yesterday's mistakes somehow define tomorrow's possibilities. The greatest conversations we will ever have are often the ones no one else hears.

The older I become, the more convinced I am that our lives are shaped not only by what happens to us, but by the voice we choose to believe about what has happened. Two people can experience the same disappointment and walk away with completely different futures. One hears the voice that says, "This is the end." The other hears the voice that says, "This is just another chapter." The circumstances may be identical, but the perspective is entirely different.

Life has a way of introducing us to voices we never invited. Sometimes those voices come from childhood. Perhaps it was a classmate who made fun of the way you looked. Maybe it was a teacher who underestimated your potential or a coach who made you feel like you would never measure up. For others, the voice belongs to someone much closer. It may have been a parent who only pointed out what you did wrong, a spouse who constantly criticized, or a supervisor who never recognized your efforts. Those words may have been spoken years ago, but they continue echoing in the hallways of our minds long after the people who spoke them have moved on.

If we're not careful, we begin repeating those words to ourselves until they become our own internal dialogue. We stop seeing ourselves through the eyes of possibility and begin viewing ourselves through the lens of every criticism we've ever received. Before long, we aren't simply remembering what someone once said. We are believing it.

I know those voices well.

As someone who has walked through cancer multiple times, there have been days when the voice of fear tried to convince me that hope was foolish. There were moments when uncertainty seemed louder than faith, when exhaustion whispered that it would be easier to quit fighting than to keep moving forward. There have been seasons when rejection made me question my worth, loss made me question God's plan, and disappointment tempted me to believe that my best days were already behind me.

Those voices can become convincing because they usually contain just enough truth to make the lie believable. Yes, life can be difficult. Yes, people can disappoint us. Yes, dreams sometimes fall apart. But the lie is that those experiences become our identity.

They do not.

As Christians, we have another voice calling to us. Throughout Scripture, God repeatedly reminds His people not to fear. He doesn't promise that life will always be easy, but He promises that we will never face it alone. God never introduces us by our failures. He introduces us as His children. He never defines us by our worst day. He defines us by His unfailing love, His grace, and His purpose for our lives.

I've often wondered why God chose to speak to the prophet Elijah not through the wind, the earthquake, or the fire, but through a gentle whisper. Perhaps it's because God's voice rarely competes with the noise of the world. It patiently waits for us to become quiet enough to hear it. In a culture filled with endless notifications, breaking news, social media opinions, and constant comparison, we often fill every silent moment with more noise. Yet the voice we most need to hear is usually the quietest one.

The challenge for each of us is deciding which voice deserves our attention. Every day we have a choice. We can believe the voice that tells us we'll never be enough, or we can believe the One who created us with purpose before we ever took our first breath. We can continue carrying labels that other people placed on us, or we can embrace the identity that God has already given us. We can allow fear to direct our future, or we can allow faith to guide our next step.

There is another perspective we often overlook. While we spend so much time thinking about the voices that influence us, we rarely stop to consider that we are becoming the voice inside someone else's story. The words we choose today may become the words another person remembers for the rest of their life. A teacher's encouragement can inspire a lifelong career. A parent's affirmation can build confidence that lasts for decades. A friend's simple reminder that "you matter" can become the reason someone chooses to keep going.

Unfortunately, the opposite is equally true. Careless words have remarkable staying power. A sarcastic comment, a cruel joke, a harsh criticism spoken in anger, or a sentence uttered without thinking can linger in someone's heart for years. We often forget what we said five minutes later, while the person who heard it may carry it for the next fifty years.

That realization should make us slower to speak and quicker to encourage. Every conversation gives us an opportunity to either build someone up or quietly tear them down. Our words cost us nothing, yet they possess incredible power. They can plant seeds of hope or seeds of doubt. They can restore confidence or destroy it. They can point someone toward Christ or further into despair.

Today, I want to leave you with two questions that are worth asking.

First, what voice are you listening to? Is it the voice of fear, shame, regret, and insecurity, or is it the voice of God's promises reminding you that you are loved, forgiven, and never alone?

Second, what voice are you becoming for others? When your family hears your words, do they hear encouragement? When your coworkers leave a conversation with you, do they feel valued? When a stranger crosses your path, do they encounter kindness? Every day, someone is deciding what to believe about themselves based on the words they hear from the people around them.

Choose to be a voice that heals instead of hurts. Choose to speak hope instead of hopelessness, grace instead of judgment, and compassion instead of criticism. The world already has enough voices telling people they aren't enough. It desperately needs more voices reminding people they are loved.

Because in the end, the voice that shapes your life will be the one you choose to believe.

I want to talk to you! If this column spoke to your heart, I would love to hear your story. We all wrestle with the voices around us and the voice within us. Sometimes the beginning of healing is simply hearing one person remind you that you are seen, you are valued, and you are never beyond God's reach.

title: Gossip: The Poison We Often Call Conversation
excerpt: Gossip: The Poison We Often Call Conversation
content:
Gossip: The Poison We Often Call Conversation

There is an old saying that words can build bridges or burn them down. If that is true, then gossip may be one of the most destructive fires we allow to spread in our daily lives.

Most people would never consider themselves a gossip. We tend to think of gossip as something mean-spirited, malicious, or intentionally harmful. Yet gossip often disguises itself as concern, curiosity, entertainment, or even friendship. It slips into conversations disguised as, "Did you hear about..." or "I'm only telling you because I'm worried about them." Before long, stories are exchanged, details are embellished, and reputations are damaged.

The reality is that gossip rarely helps the person being discussed. Instead, it often serves the needs of the people sharing it. It provides a momentary sense of importance, a feeling of being "in the know," or a temporary bond between people. Unfortunately, those brief moments often come at someone else's expense.

I have witnessed gossip in workplaces, churches, schools, families, and community organizations. It does not discriminate. In fact, some of the places where kindness should flourish are often where gossip can do the most damage. A rumor whispered in a hallway can grow into a story that follows someone for years. A careless comment can permanently alter how others view a person. Sometimes the individual being discussed never even gets the opportunity to defend themselves.

What makes gossip especially dangerous is that it often contains just enough truth to sound believable. A small fact becomes mixed with assumptions, opinions, and speculation. By the time the story reaches its final destination, it barely resembles reality. Yet people continue repeating it as though it were absolute truth.

As someone who has faced challenges throughout life, I know firsthand how painful it can be to become the subject of conversations you never knew were taking place. Whether it was during my battle with cancer, difficult seasons in my career, or personal struggles, there were times when others created narratives without ever asking me for the truth. Many of us have experienced that feeling. It hurts because it reminds us that people often judge our story after reading only a single page.

One of the simplest tests I try to apply is this: Would I say these same words if the person were standing right beside me?

If the answer is no, perhaps those words should never be spoken at all.

Another question worth asking is whether what we are about to say is helpful. Is it necessary? Is it kind? Is it true? Too often, conversations fail all three tests. We repeat information that is neither helpful nor necessary simply because we have the opportunity to do so.

Our world already has enough division, negativity, and criticism. Social media has only amplified the problem. Rumors travel faster than facts. Opinions are treated as evidence. Entire reputations can be damaged in a matter of minutes by people who have never met the individual they are discussing.

As Christians, we are called to something higher. Scripture repeatedly warns us about the power of our words. Proverbs reminds us that "death and life are in the power of the tongue." Think about that for a moment. The same mouth that offers encouragement can also destroy confidence. The same words that can heal can also wound.

Imagine how different our communities would be if we became known for protecting people instead of talking about them. What if we chose encouragement over criticism? What if we defended someone when others were tearing them down? What if we simply refused to participate when gossip began to circulate?

That choice may not make us the most popular person in the room, but it may make us the most respected.

The next time a conversation turns toward gossip, consider changing the direction. Offer grace. Offer understanding. Offer silence if necessary. Sometimes the most powerful words are the ones we choose not to say.

After all, every person you meet is carrying a story you know nothing about. Every person is fighting battles you cannot see. Every person deserves the same compassion and understanding that you would want if your name became the topic of someone else's conversation.

Words matter.

Use them carefully.

Use them kindly.

Use them to build people up rather than tear them down.

Because long after a conversation ends, the impact of our words often remains.

Until next time, this is Dr. Nick reminding you that sometimes the greatest measure of character is not what we say about others, but what we refuse to say when they are not in the room.

I want to talk to you! Reach out to me through Inspire With Dr. Nick and share your thoughts, your story, or a perspective that has changed your life.

title: 86,400 seconds.
excerpt: We are gifted 86,400 seconds each day... How do you use them?
content:
86,400 Seconds

Every day, God gives each of us an incredible gift. It arrives quietly and without recognition, yet it is more valuable than any paycheck, investment, or earthly possession we could ever receive. That gift is time. Specifically, God grants each of us 86,400 seconds every single day.

What makes this gift so remarkable is that it is distributed equally. Regardless of our age, occupation, social status, education, or financial situation, every person begins the day with the same number of seconds available to them. The successful business executive receives 86,400 seconds. The retiree receives 86,400 seconds. The student, the farmer, the nurse, the factory worker, and the stay-at-home parent all receive exactly the same amount. In a world where so much is unequal, time remains one of the few gifts given equally to everyone.

The challenge is not how many seconds we receive. The challenge is how we choose to spend them.

Most people are very careful with money. We budget it, save it, invest it, and often worry about it. We become frustrated when we waste it because we understand its value. Yet many of us fail to treat time with the same level of importance, even though time is infinitely more valuable than money. If you lose money, there is a chance you can earn it back. If you lose a possession, you may be able to replace it. If you lose an opportunity, another one may eventually come along. However, once time has passed, it is gone forever. There is no way to recover yesterday's hours, last week's missed opportunities, or the years that have slipped quietly into the past.

Imagine for a moment that every morning a bank deposited $86,400 into your account with one condition. Whatever portion remained at the end of the day would disappear forever. Most of us would spend that money carefully. We would think about how to maximize its value and make wise decisions regarding its use. Yet God gives us something far more precious than money. He gives us 86,400 seconds, and many of us spend them without a second thought.

We live in a culture that constantly encourages us to move faster. We rush from one appointment to another. We hurry through meals. We multitask during conversations. We glance at our phones while sitting with people we care about. We become so focused on preparing for tomorrow that we often fail to experience today. In the process, we unintentionally allow some of life's most meaningful moments to pass us by.

The truth is that life is ultimately made up of moments. It is found in conversations around the dinner table, visits with aging parents, laughter shared with friends, and quiet evenings spent with those we love. It is found in simple acts of kindness, unexpected opportunities to help someone in need, and moments when we pause long enough to appreciate the blessings surrounding us. These experiences may seem ordinary at the time, but they often become the memories we treasure most.

As I have grown older, I have come to understand that some of the most valuable moments in life cannot be scheduled, purchased, or recreated. They simply happen when we make ourselves available to them. A conversation with a friend who needs encouragement. A chance encounter with someone who is struggling. A sunset viewed from the front porch. A grandparent sharing stories from decades ago. These moments often seem small in the moment, but years later they become some of our most cherished memories.

My own experiences with cancer have taught me lessons about time that I wish I had understood earlier in life. When you are faced with uncertainty about your health, your perspective changes. Things that once seemed urgent suddenly seem less important. The frustrations that once consumed your attention begin to lose their significance. You become more aware that each day is a gift and not a guarantee. You start to realize that life should not merely be measured by its length, but by how it is lived.

Far too often we postpone what matters most. We assume there will always be another holiday, another family gathering, another opportunity to visit someone, or another chance to say the words that need to be said. We put off phone calls, delay reconciliation, and postpone pursuing dreams because we convince ourselves that there will be plenty of time later. The reality is that none of us knows how much later we have.

That is why it is so important to use today's seconds wisely. Spend time with people you love. Offer forgiveness where it is needed. Invest in relationships. Strengthen your faith. Volunteer. Encourage someone who is struggling. Pursue the goals and dreams that God has placed on your heart. Make memories. Share laughter. Create moments that will matter long after the day itself has passed.

One of the greatest tragedies in life is not running out of time. It is reaching the end of our lives and realizing we spent too much of our time focused on things that never truly mattered. Arguments, grudges, worries, and distractions have a way of consuming enormous amounts of our lives while offering very little in return. Meanwhile, the opportunities to love, serve, encourage, and make a difference quietly pass by unnoticed.

Scripture reminds us in Psalm 90:12, "Teach us to number our days, that we may gain a heart of wisdom." That verse is not intended to make us fearful of the future. Rather, it encourages us to appreciate the value of the present. Wisdom comes when we recognize that every day is a gift from God and every moment presents an opportunity to honor Him through the way we live.

As you go about your day today, remember that God has entrusted you with 86,400 seconds. They are yours to invest, yours to spend, and yours to use for good. However, unlike money, they cannot be saved for tomorrow. When midnight arrives, whatever remains unused disappears forever. A new day may bring another deposit, but today's balance will never return.

Perhaps that is why time is such a sacred gift. It reminds us that life is not measured by what we accumulate, but by what we do with the moments God gives us. The question each of us must answer is not how many seconds we have received, but how faithfully we have used them.

Today, God has blessed you with 86,400 seconds. Use them well.

title: Father's Day
excerpt: Father's Day.
content:
Father's Day

Father's Day is one of those holidays that can stir up a wide range of emotions.

For some people, it is a joyful day filled with family gatherings, laughter, cookouts, and phone calls from children and grandchildren. It is a day of celebration, gratitude, and reflection on the men who helped shape our lives. For others, however, Father's Day can be a difficult reminder of loss, distance, strained relationships, or dreams that never quite became reality.

The truth is that Father's Day means something different to each of us because our stories are different.

Some people are blessed with fathers who were present every step of the way. Some grew up with fathers who worked long hours to provide for their families. Others were raised by single mothers, grandparents, stepfathers, mentors, coaches, teachers, or family friends who stepped into the role when someone else could not. Some people spend Father's Day remembering a father who has passed away, while others spend the day wishing they had experienced a relationship they never had.

Life rarely fits neatly inside the picture-perfect scenes we see in greeting cards and television commercials.

As I have gotten older, I have come to understand that fatherhood is about far more than biology. Biology may make someone a parent, but it is love, sacrifice, commitment, and presence that make someone a father. Fatherhood is not measured by DNA. It is measured by the countless moments when someone chooses to put another person's needs ahead of their own.

It is measured by showing up.

Showing up for the ball games.

Showing up for graduations.

Showing up during difficult conversations.

Showing up when life becomes messy and complicated.

Showing up when it would be easier to walk away.

The older I get, the more I appreciate the importance of simply being there.

When I was younger, I thought being a father meant having all the answers. I imagined fathers as people who always knew exactly what to do and exactly what to say. As life has taught me repeatedly, that is rarely the case. Most fathers are simply doing the best they can with the knowledge, experiences, and resources they have at the time.

Many are figuring things out as they go.

They make mistakes.

They second-guess decisions.

They worry more than they admit.

They lose sleep over their children, whether those children are five years old or fifty years old.

And yet they keep trying.

That persistence is one of the greatest expressions of love.

This Father's Day carries a different perspective for me than it once did.

If you had told me years ago that I would find myself stepping into a fatherly role later in life, I probably would not have believed you. Life has a way of taking us down roads we never expected to travel. Sometimes the greatest blessings arrive through circumstances we never planned and opportunities we never imagined.

Over the past year, I have learned lessons about fatherhood that I never expected to learn.

I have learned that being a father figure has very little to do with having the right answers and everything to do with being available. It is about listening when someone needs to talk. It is about offering guidance while also allowing someone the freedom to learn from their own experiences. It is about celebrating victories, no matter how small they may seem, and providing support when things do not go according to plan.

There is no instruction manual.

There is no perfect formula.

Every person is different, and every relationship develops in its own unique way.

What remains constant, however, is the need for encouragement, support, accountability, and love.

As I reflect on Father's Day, I also find myself thinking about the father figures who influenced my own life. Some were relatives. Some were mentors. Some were supervisors, teachers, coaches, clergy members, or friends. Looking back, I realize that many of the lessons that shaped me came from people who may never fully understand the impact they had on my life.

A few words of encouragement.

A lesson about integrity.

An example of kindness.

A conversation at exactly the right moment.

These things matter.

In fact, they often matter far more than we realize.

One of the greatest gifts any father can give is not wealth, status, or material possessions. It is character. It is teaching honesty when dishonesty would be easier. It is demonstrating compassion in a world that often feels unkind. It is showing resilience when circumstances become difficult. It is helping someone understand that success is not measured solely by what we accomplish, but by how we treat other people along the way.

The world needs more fathers who model those values.

The world needs more men who understand that strength is not found in dominance but in service. It is found in patience. It is found in humility. It is found in choosing to lead through example rather than simply through words.

Perhaps that is why the role of a father is so important.

Children may not remember every conversation.

They may not remember every rule.

They may not remember every lecture.

But they will remember how they felt around you.

They will remember whether you were present.

They will remember whether they felt valued.

They will remember whether they felt loved.

Years from now, those memories will become part of the foundation upon which they build their own lives.

As Christians, we are also reminded of the example of our Heavenly Father. God's love is patient, faithful, forgiving, and constant. Even when we stumble, He remains present. Even when we lose our way, He continues to call us back. His example reminds us that true fatherhood is rooted not in perfection but in unwavering love.

On this Father's Day, I want to express gratitude for every father, grandfather, stepfather, adoptive father, mentor, coach, teacher, and role model who has invested in the life of another person. Your influence reaches farther than you may ever know. The lessons you teach, the examples you set, and the love you share continue long after the moment has passed.

I also want to acknowledge those who find Father's Day painful. If you are grieving a father, mourning a relationship that never existed, or carrying wounds that still ache today, know that your feelings are valid. Give yourself grace. Allow yourself to remember. Allow yourself to heal. And remember that family is not always defined by blood. Sometimes the people who become family are the ones who choose to stay, support, encourage, and love us when we need it most.

Fatherhood, at its heart, is not about titles.

It is about presence.

It is about sacrifice.

It is about love.

And perhaps the greatest measure of a father is not what he leaves behind for others, but what he leaves within them.

Happy Father's Day.

I want to talk to you. Reach out. Your story matters, and I'd love to hear it.

title: Word Judo
excerpt: Words Matter.
content:
Word Judo

Words have an incredible amount of power.

They can inspire a person to chase a dream they never thought possible. They can comfort someone who is grieving. They can strengthen relationships, heal wounds, and provide hope during life's darkest moments. At the same time, words can discourage, divide, humiliate, and leave scars that remain long after the conversation has ended.

Most of us have experienced both sides of that reality. We can probably remember a compliment that stayed with us for years, just as easily as we can remember a harsh comment that we wish we could forget. Words matter because people matter. The things we say often carry far more weight than we realize.

Recently, I found myself thinking about a concept known as "Verbal Judo," sometimes called "Word Judo." The idea is simple, yet incredibly powerful. Rather than meeting force with force, the goal is to redirect the conversation in a more productive direction. Instead of escalating conflict, you learn to defuse it. Instead of creating another argument, you create an opportunity for understanding.

In traditional martial arts, judo is not about being the strongest person in the room. It is about understanding movement, leverage, and timing. A skilled practitioner learns how to redirect an opponent's energy rather than simply fighting against it. The same principle can be applied to communication.

Think about how many conversations go wrong because people are focused on winning rather than understanding. We see it every day. It happens in families, workplaces, churches, schools, community meetings, and especially on social media. Two people begin with different opinions, but instead of listening to one another, they immediately begin building defenses and preparing counterattacks. The discussion quickly turns into a competition where the objective is no longer finding common ground. The objective becomes proving the other person wrong.

The unfortunate reality is that very few people have ever changed their minds because they were insulted. Very few relationships have been strengthened through sarcasm, ridicule, or personal attacks. When people feel attacked, they naturally become defensive. Once that happens, meaningful communication becomes nearly impossible.

Word Judo encourages us to take a different approach.

Instead of immediately responding with emotion, it encourages us to pause and consider what may be driving the other person's behavior. Often, what we see on the surface is only a small part of a much larger story. The angry customer may have just received devastating news. The impatient driver may be rushing to a hospital. The rude coworker may be carrying burdens that nobody else knows about. The person lashing out online may be struggling with loneliness, fear, or insecurity.

This does not excuse poor behavior, but it can help us understand it.

One of the greatest lessons life has taught me is that people are often fighting battles we cannot see. We walk past individuals every day who are carrying grief, anxiety, disappointment, health challenges, financial stress, family struggles, or emotional pain. Sometimes those burdens spill into their interactions with others. What appears to be anger may actually be hurt. What appears to be arrogance may actually be fear. What appears to be hostility may actually be someone desperately trying to protect themselves from further pain.

When we understand that possibility, our response begins to change.

Instead of immediately becoming defensive, we become curious. Instead of assuming the worst, we begin asking questions. Instead of escalating tension, we start looking for ways to lower it. A simple phrase such as, "Help me understand where you're coming from," can completely change the direction of a conversation. So can saying, "I hear what you're saying," or "Tell me more about that."

People want to feel heard. In fact, I would argue that one of the deepest human needs is the desire to feel understood. Many conflicts continue not because people disagree, but because neither person feels like the other is truly listening.

Listening is a skill that has become increasingly rare in our world. We live in a culture that rewards quick responses, instant reactions, and constant opinions. We are encouraged to speak, post, comment, and react. Yet very little attention is given to the importance of listening carefully before responding.

The truth is that listening requires humility. It requires us to acknowledge that we may not have all the answers. It requires us to recognize that another person's experiences may have led them to conclusions different from our own. It requires patience, empathy, and sometimes the willingness to sit with discomfort.

As a Christian, I often reflect on how Jesus communicated with people. Throughout the Gospels, He encountered individuals from every walk of life. He met people who doubted Him, criticized Him, opposed Him, and even sought to trap Him. Yet His responses were often measured, thoughtful, and purposeful. He listened. He asked questions. He met people where they were. He understood that changing hearts requires far more than winning arguments.

That example remains relevant today.

Our communities do not need more shouting matches. They do not need more insults, more division, or more opportunities to prove who is right. What they need are people willing to listen, people willing to seek understanding, and people willing to communicate with both conviction and compassion.

That does not mean avoiding difficult conversations. Some conversations are necessary. Some issues demand courage and honesty. Word Judo is not about avoiding truth. It is about delivering truth in a way that opens doors rather than slamming them shut. It is about maintaining respect even when disagreements exist.

The older I get, the more convinced I become that relationships are often more important than arguments. At the end of our lives, very few of us will be remembered for the debates we won. We will be remembered for how we treated people. We will be remembered for whether we made others feel valued, respected, and heard.

The next time you find yourself in a difficult conversation, consider taking a different approach. Take a breath before responding. Listen a little longer. Ask one more question. Seek understanding before seeking victory. You may discover that changing the direction of a conversation is far more powerful than winning it.

In a world that often feels divided, Word Judo reminds us that our words can be instruments of peace rather than weapons of conflict. They can build bridges where others build walls. They can create understanding where others create division. They can bring people together rather than push them apart.

And perhaps that is one of the most important skills any of us can learn.

title: What does your favorite color say about you?
excerpt: We all have a favorite color, but what does our favorite color say about us?
content:
What Does Your Favorite Color Say About You?

Have you ever noticed how quickly most people can answer the question, "What's your favorite color?" It is one of the simplest questions we ask each other, and yet the answer often comes without hesitation. Whether it's blue, red, green, yellow, purple, or another color entirely, most of us develop a preference early in life and carry it with us for years. While it may seem like a trivial detail, I have often wondered if our favorite colors reveal something deeper about who we are and how we see the world.

Colors have a unique way of affecting our emotions. They influence the way we decorate our homes, the clothes we choose to wear, the vehicles we drive, and even the products we purchase. Entire industries spend millions of dollars studying how color impacts human behavior because they understand that colors evoke feelings, memories, and reactions. While our favorite color certainly doesn't define us, it may offer clues about what brings us comfort, what inspires us, and what qualities we value most.

My favorite color has always been blue. When I think about blue, I think about calm waters, clear skies, and peaceful moments. Blue often symbolizes trust, stability, loyalty, and dependability. Perhaps that is why so many people are drawn to it. In a world that can sometimes feel chaotic and unpredictable, blue represents steadiness. It reminds us of the comfort that comes from knowing certain things can still be counted on. Maybe those who love blue appreciate peace and consistency because they understand how valuable those things truly are.

People who favor green are often drawn to ideas of growth, renewal, and balance. Green surrounds us in nature. It is the color of new leaves in the spring, healthy forests, and fertile fields. It represents life continuing forward. Individuals who love green may find themselves seeking harmony and appreciating the simple blessings that others sometimes overlook. They often understand that growth takes time and that some of life's most meaningful changes happen gradually rather than all at once.

Red is a color that demands attention. It is associated with passion, determination, courage, and energy. Those who love red are often people who approach life with enthusiasm and conviction. They are not afraid to pursue their goals or stand up for what they believe. Red reminds us that life is meant to be lived fully and that sometimes we must be willing to take risks if we hope to accomplish something meaningful. People drawn to red often bring energy and excitement wherever they go.

Yellow is frequently connected with happiness, optimism, and creativity. It is the color of sunshine, warmth, and hope. Individuals who love yellow often possess a positive outlook and have a way of encouraging others during difficult times. They may not always have perfect lives, but they often choose to focus on the good that remains rather than dwelling entirely on what has gone wrong. Their outlook can serve as a reminder that joy is not found in perfect circumstances but in learning to appreciate the blessings that still exist.

Purple has long been associated with wisdom, creativity, and imagination. Historically connected to royalty, purple often attracts those who think deeply and see possibilities where others see limitations. People who love purple frequently have a creative spirit and enjoy exploring ideas, dreams, and perspectives that stretch beyond the ordinary. They remind us that innovation and imagination are often what move the world forward.

Even colors such as black and white can reveal something meaningful. Black is often associated with strength, sophistication, and independence. While some view it negatively, many who prefer black appreciate simplicity and authenticity. White, on the other hand, often represents clarity, peace, and fresh beginnings. It reminds us that every day provides an opportunity to start over and write a new chapter in our lives.

Of course, no color can tell the entire story of a person. Human beings are far too complex for that. A favorite color cannot reveal the struggles someone has endured, the losses they have experienced, or the dreams they carry in their hearts. It cannot tell us about their faith, their family, or the lessons life has taught them along the way. Yet colors may offer small glimpses into the qualities we admire and the feelings we seek.

The older I get, the more I realize that life is not really about identifying which color describes us best. Instead, it is about appreciating the incredible variety that exists among people. Just as a beautiful painting requires many colors to create its masterpiece, our world needs different personalities, different perspectives, and different gifts. The things that make us different are often the very things that make our communities stronger.

Perhaps that is why favorite colors matter. They remind us that no two people see the world exactly the same way. One person finds peace in blue skies. Another finds hope in green fields. Someone else finds strength in red or joy in yellow. None of those perspectives are wrong. They simply reflect the unique experiences and personalities that God has given each of us.

The next time someone asks about your favorite color, take a moment to think beyond the answer itself. Consider what draws you to that color and what it represents in your life. You may discover that your favorite color says less about what you like to look at and more about what you value most. And that, perhaps, is a matter of perspective.

title: A Broken World.
excerpt: Our World is a mess and broken in many areas.  One true light remains
content:
A Broken World and the One Sure Thing

It doesn't take much these days to recognize that we live in a broken world. Every time we turn on the news, scroll through social media, or simply listen to conversations around us, we are reminded of the struggles people face. We see violence, division, hatred, loneliness, and uncertainty. We watch families become divided over differences. We see communities struggling to come together. We witness people searching desperately for answers while feeling more lost than ever before.

For many people, it can feel overwhelming.

There are moments when it seems as though the world is moving faster than we can keep up with. Technology advances, cultural norms shift, and opinions change almost daily. What was accepted yesterday may be challenged today. What once felt certain may suddenly feel uncertain. In the midst of all this change, many people find themselves asking a simple but important question: What can I truly depend on?

The reality is that much of what we place our trust in is temporary. Careers can disappear. Financial security can change overnight. Relationships can end. Health can decline unexpectedly. Even the strongest institutions are made up of imperfect people who sometimes fail to live up to expectations. Time has a way of reminding us that everything in this earthly life is fragile.

That truth can be frightening if we stop there.

Yet for those of us who have faith, there is another truth that stands above all the uncertainty of the world. While everything around us may change, Jesus Christ does not. His teachings remain the same. His love remains the same. His promises remain the same. The world around us may feel unstable, but Christ remains our foundation.

I often think that one of the greatest challenges facing people today is not a lack of information. In fact, we have more information available to us than at any point in human history. The challenge is that information alone does not bring peace. Knowledge alone does not heal a broken heart. Success alone does not remove loneliness. Wealth alone does not provide lasting joy. We can fill our lives with countless distractions and still feel empty inside.

What people are truly searching for is hope.

They are searching for something greater than themselves. They are searching for purpose when life feels meaningless. They are searching for peace when anxiety overwhelms them. They are searching for love that does not come with conditions and acceptance that does not disappear when mistakes are made.

That search ultimately leads to Jesus.

Throughout His ministry, Jesus met people exactly where they were. He met the broken, the rejected, the fearful, the grieving, and the lost. He didn't wait for people to become perfect before offering His love. He reached out to those whom society often ignored. He brought comfort to those carrying heavy burdens. He offered forgiveness to those who believed they were beyond redemption.

More than two thousand years later, that invitation remains open.

What has always amazed me about the Gospel is that Jesus never promised a life free from hardship. He never said we would avoid disappointment, illness, grief, or heartbreak. In fact, He acknowledged that we would face difficult days. He understood the reality of suffering because He experienced it Himself. Yet even while recognizing the struggles of this world, He continually pointed people toward hope.

That hope is not found in circumstances.

It is found in Him.

When the world feels chaotic, Jesus offers peace. When fear begins to take hold, Jesus offers reassurance. When we feel abandoned, Jesus reminds us that we are never alone. When we feel unworthy, He reminds us that we are loved. When we feel lost, He points us toward a path forward.

I believe that is why faith remains so important in our lives. Faith gives us an anchor when the storms of life threaten to pull us away. It reminds us that our value is not determined by our bank account, our job title, our popularity, or our failures. Our value comes from being children of God. That truth remains constant regardless of what is happening around us.

As Christians, we are called not only to follow Jesus but to reflect Him. In a world filled with anger, we are called to show kindness. In a world filled with division, we are called to build bridges. In a world that often chooses judgment, we are called to offer grace. The brokenness of the world should not cause us to retreat from it. Instead, it should inspire us to bring more light into it.

Sometimes that light comes through simple acts. A kind word. A helping hand. A listening ear. A sincere prayer. A moment of compassion for someone who is struggling. We may never fully realize how much those small actions can mean to another person. What seems insignificant to us may be exactly what someone else needs to make it through another day.

The world has always been broken because humanity is imperfect. Yet God's love has always been present in the midst of that brokenness. Through every generation, every crisis, every tragedy, and every challenge, Christ has remained faithful. Kingdoms have risen and fallen. Leaders have come and gone. Entire civilizations have changed. Yet the message of Jesus continues to transform lives.

That is why, when I look at the world today, I refuse to lose hope.

Yes, there is brokenness. Yes, there is pain. Yes, there is uncertainty about what tomorrow may bring. But there is also a Savior who walks beside us through every challenge. There is a God who continues to love His people. There is a promise that this broken world is not the end of the story.

And that, from my perspective, is the one sure thing we can hold onto when everything else feels uncertain. The world may change. Circumstances may change. People may change. But Jesus Christ remains the same yesterday, today, and forever.

When the world feels broken, keep your eyes on Him.

title: The Journey
excerpt: We are all on a journey and not all journeys look the same, take the same amount of time or require much, whereas there are journey's that are quite the opposite.
content:
The Journey

Life is often described as a journey, and while that phrase may sound simple, it contains a profound truth. Every one of us is traveling a road that is uniquely our own. No two journeys are identical. We may share similar experiences, walk alongside others for a season, or even travel the same roads for a time, but each person's path is shaped by different circumstances, choices, challenges, and blessings.

When we are young, we often imagine life as a carefully planned route. We believe that if we study hard, make good decisions, and follow the rules, life will unfold exactly as we expect. We picture ourselves reaching certain milestones by certain ages. We create timelines for education, careers, relationships, and families. There is comfort in believing that life follows a predictable schedule.

As the years pass, however, most of us discover that life rarely follows our plans.

Unexpected opportunities appear. Doors we thought would open remain closed. Relationships begin and end. Careers take surprising turns. Dreams evolve. Priorities change. Sometimes the very things we thought would define our future become little more than distant memories, replaced by opportunities we never could have imagined.

That is the nature of the journey. It is not a straight highway stretching endlessly toward a visible destination. It is a winding road filled with curves, hills, valleys, and occasional detours. Some stretches are smooth and easy. Others challenge every ounce of strength we possess. Yet it is often those difficult stretches that teach us the lessons we need most.

For many people, the journey begins with a search for purpose. We spend years trying to figure out who we are and where we belong. We look for our place in the world. Some discover their calling early in life. Others spend decades exploring different paths before finding work that truly fulfills them. Neither path is right or wrong. Both are part of the process of becoming who we were created to be.

College is often one of the first major milestones on that journey. While people frequently think of college as a place to earn a degree, it is often much more than that. It is a season of discovery. It is where many people leave behind familiar surroundings and begin making decisions on their own. It is where friendships are formed, perspectives are challenged, and futures begin to take shape.

Some students arrive on campus absolutely certain of their major and career path. Others change direction multiple times before graduation. Looking back, many realize that the most valuable lessons learned during those years had little to do with textbooks and examinations. The real lessons involved resilience, independence, responsibility, and learning how to navigate uncertainty.

The journey continues as we enter the workforce. Careers have a way of teaching us lessons that no classroom can fully prepare us for. We learn how to work with people who think differently than we do. We learn how to handle disappointment, setbacks, and failure. We learn that success is often built upon countless moments of perseverance that nobody else sees.

Many of us spend years chasing promotions, titles, and achievements, believing they will bring lasting satisfaction. There is nothing wrong with ambition, but eventually most people discover that fulfillment comes from something deeper. It comes from knowing that our work matters. It comes from making a difference in the lives of others. It comes from understanding that success is measured by more than a paycheck or a job title.

Along the way, many people encounter another significant journey: the journey of love.

Love has a remarkable ability to transform our lives. It teaches us to think beyond ourselves. It challenges us to become more patient, more understanding, and more compassionate. Love often enters our lives when we least expect it and changes us in ways we never anticipated.

The journey of love is not always easy. Relationships require effort, sacrifice, communication, and forgiveness. There are moments of joy and moments of frustration. There are seasons when everything feels effortless and seasons when love requires intentional work. Yet those challenges often deepen the bonds between people and create relationships that withstand the test of time.

For some, the journey includes marriage and building a life with someone they cherish. For others, the journey may involve heartbreak, loss, or the realization that life has different plans. Regardless of the outcome, every relationship leaves an imprint on our hearts. Every person we love teaches us something about ourselves and about what truly matters.

Perhaps no journey is more personal than the journey of faith.

Faith is not simply about attending church, reciting prayers, or following traditions. Faith is about developing a relationship with God. It is about learning to trust even when we do not understand. It is about finding hope when circumstances seem hopeless and discovering peace when life feels chaotic.

For some people, faith comes naturally. They grow up surrounded by strong spiritual influences and never drift far from their beliefs. For others, faith is a long road filled with questions, doubts, and struggles. They may spend years searching for answers before eventually finding their way back to God.

What I have learned is that God is patient with all of us. He does not abandon us when we struggle. He does not stop loving us when we question. He walks beside us through every season, whether we recognize His presence or not.

Many people discover their deepest faith not during life's victories but during its hardships. When everything is going well, it is easy to feel self-sufficient. When life falls apart, we often find ourselves reaching for something greater than ourselves. It is during those moments that many people encounter Jesus in a way they never have before.

The journey can become especially difficult when serious health challenges enter our lives.

A major diagnosis has a way of changing everything. Suddenly the future feels uncertain. Priorities shift. The things that once seemed important become less significant. Daily routines are replaced with appointments, treatments, tests, and waiting for answers.

Anyone who has faced a life-threatening illness knows that the experience changes you. It forces you to view life differently. You begin appreciating moments that once seemed ordinary. A sunrise becomes more meaningful. A quiet afternoon with family becomes a gift. The simple act of feeling well enough to enjoy a normal day becomes something worth celebrating.

Health struggles teach us that life is fragile. They remind us that tomorrow is never guaranteed. While those lessons can be painful, they also have the power to deepen our gratitude and strengthen our faith. Many people emerge from those battles with a renewed appreciation for life and a clearer understanding of what truly matters.

Then there is the journey that none of us wish to take but all of us eventually face: the journey of grief.

Losing someone you love changes you forever. Whether it is a parent, spouse, sibling, child, friend, or mentor, grief creates a space in our lives that can never be completely filled. The pain may soften over time, but the love remains.

The hardest part about loss is that life continues moving forward while a part of us wishes time would stand still. The world keeps turning. People return to their routines. Yet our hearts are trying to adjust to a reality we never wanted to accept.

Over time, however, we begin to understand that grief is not about letting go of someone we love. It is about learning how to carry their memory with us. We carry their stories. We carry their lessons. We carry the impact they made on our lives. Their physical presence may be gone, but their influence remains woven into who we are.

As I reflect on the journey of life, I have come to believe that the destination is not the most important part. The most important part is who we become along the way. It is the people we meet, the relationships we build, the faith we develop, the obstacles we overcome, and the love we share.

The journey is rarely easy. There will be seasons when the road ahead is clear and beautiful. There will also be seasons when we feel lost, exhausted, or uncertain of what comes next. Yet every season has value. Every chapter contributes to the story God is writing in our lives.

The truth is that none of us know exactly where the road will lead tomorrow. We do not know what opportunities await us, what challenges we may face, or what blessings may be just around the next bend. What we do know is that we are not traveling alone. God walks beside us through every joy, every heartbreak, every victory, and every struggle.

The journey may not always be easy, but it is still a gift. Every day offers another opportunity to learn, grow, love, forgive, and become the person God created us to be. The road may be long, but there is beauty in the miles, wisdom in the detours, and purpose in every step.

So wherever you find yourself today on life's journey, keep moving forward. Trust God with the road ahead. Embrace the lessons of the present moment. And never forget that some of the most meaningful parts of the journey are often found in places we never expected to go.

Because in the end, life is not simply about reaching a destination. It is about the people we love, the faith we discover, the lives we touch, and the story we leave behind.

title: Ten hugs a day
excerpt: Hugs matter.  Daily goal should be 10 hugs a day!
content:
Ten Hugs a Day

Several months ago, I shared a simple piece of advice with Canyon. At first, he looked at me like I had completely lost my mind.

I told him that everyone needs ten hugs a day.

Naturally, he laughed.

Most teenagers probably would. When you're eighteen, you're focused on school, work, friends, future plans, and figuring out where life is taking you. Counting hugs probably doesn't rank very high on the list of priorities. But the more we talked about it, the more he realized I wasn't really talking about hugs alone.

What I was talking about was connection.

I was talking about the need every human being has to feel seen, valued, and loved. I was talking about the reality that no matter how independent we believe we are, we were never created to go through life alone.

The older I get, the more I realize that one of the greatest struggles facing our world today is loneliness. It is a quiet struggle that often goes unnoticed because it rarely announces itself. Many people who are lonely don't look lonely. They smile. They go to work. They attend church. They participate in family gatherings. They post happy photos on social media. From the outside, everything appears perfectly fine.

Yet beneath the surface, many are carrying a tremendous weight.

We live in an age where communication has never been easier. We can send a text message around the world in seconds. We can instantly share photos, videos, thoughts, and opinions with hundreds or even thousands of people. Yet despite all of our technology, genuine human connection often feels harder to find than ever before.

There is a significant difference between being connected and feeling connected.

A person can have five hundred friends on social media and still feel completely alone. They can sit in a crowded room and feel invisible. They can attend church every week and leave wondering if anyone truly knows them. They can spend an entire day interacting with people and still go home feeling isolated.

The truth is that people are hungry for authentic relationships. They are longing for someone to ask how they are doing and genuinely care about the answer. They are searching for people who will stay when life becomes difficult and who will continue to show up long after everyone else has moved on.

That is where the idea of ten hugs a day begins to make sense.

Of course, not every hug involves wrapping your arms around someone. Some of the most meaningful hugs I've ever received didn't involve physical contact at all. They came in the form of a phone call at exactly the right moment. They came through a handwritten note. They came from someone sitting beside me during a difficult season and simply refusing to let me face it alone.

Over the years, I've experienced my share of difficult chapters. Cancer brought uncertainty and fear. Losing Rick brought grief that still surfaces unexpectedly. There have been moments when life felt overwhelming and moments when I wasn't entirely sure what the next chapter would hold.

What I remember most from those seasons is not the advice people gave me. It is not the solutions they offered or the words they spoke. What I remember is who showed up.

I remember the people who called to check on me. I remember those who prayed for me. I remember the friends who sat and listened when there was nothing they could do to fix the situation. I remember the people who stayed present when it would have been easier to move on with their own lives.

Those moments mattered.

In fact, they mattered more than those individuals will probably ever know.

The longer I live, the more convinced I become that some of the greatest gifts we can offer another person are our time, our attention, and our presence. Those gifts cost very little, yet they often have immeasurable value. A few minutes spent listening can change someone's entire day. A simple message saying, "I was thinking about you," can arrive at precisely the moment someone needs it most.

The reality is that every person we encounter is carrying something. Some burdens are obvious. Others are completely hidden. The smiling cashier at the grocery store may be struggling financially. The coworker who seems distant may be caring for an aging parent. The student sitting quietly in class may be wrestling with anxiety. The person sitting alone in a church pew may desperately wish someone would simply acknowledge their presence.

We rarely know the full story.

That is why kindness matters so much.

It is also why I believe we should never underestimate the power of small gestures. A smile, a conversation, a compliment, a note of encouragement, or a simple act of compassion can have a greater impact than we realize. We often assume people know they are appreciated, but many never hear those words. We assume people know they matter, yet countless individuals spend their days wondering if anyone notices them at all.

Imagine what our communities would look like if we intentionally became better at offering those daily "hugs." Imagine if we checked on friends more often. Imagine if we reached out to someone who seemed distant. Imagine if we spent less time arguing and more time listening. Imagine if we made it a point to encourage rather than criticize.

The world certainly offers no shortage of negativity. Every day we encounter division, anger, criticism, and conflict. What people need more of is compassion. They need reminders that they are not alone. They need people willing to extend grace, offer encouragement, and walk alongside them through life's challenges.

Perhaps ten hugs a day is not really about reaching a specific number. Perhaps it is simply a reminder to live intentionally. It is a reminder to notice people, care about people, and invest in relationships. It is a reminder that every person we meet has value and that even the smallest act of kindness can leave a lasting impression.

So today, take a moment to check on someone. Make the phone call you've been meaning to make. Send the text message you've been putting off. Spend a few extra minutes listening. Offer a word of encouragement. Let someone know they matter.

You never know what battle they may be facing. You never know how much they needed that moment of connection. And you never know when your simple act of kindness may become one of the ten hugs that helps someone make it through the day.

— Dr. Nick

title: The Chapters of our Lives
excerpt: Our lives are a book and we are constantly writing chapters...
content:
The Chapters of Our Lives

One of the things I have come to appreciate as I have gotten older is that life is not meant to be understood one day at a time. Most of the time, we can only truly understand where we have been once we have traveled far enough to look back. Life unfolds much like a book. It is written chapter by chapter, page by page, experience by experience. While we often wish we could skip ahead and see how everything turns out, the reality is that each chapter serves a purpose, even the ones we would rather not read.

The challenge is that when we are living through a difficult season, it can feel as though that chapter is the entire story. When life hurts, when disappointment arrives, when relationships change, when health declines, or when uncertainty settles in, it becomes easy to believe that things will always be this way. We begin to view our present circumstances as permanent rather than temporary. We forget that stories continue to unfold and that every chapter eventually gives way to another.

Looking back on my own life, I can clearly identify chapters that I never would have chosen for myself. There were chapters marked by confusion and self-doubt. There were chapters filled with loss and grief. There were chapters when I questioned where I belonged, what direction I was headed, and whether I was strong enough to face what was in front of me. Cancer became one of those chapters. Losing people I loved became another. Career changes, unexpected transitions, setbacks, and moments of uncertainty all became part of the story as well.

At the time, none of those experiences felt like a chapter. They felt like the entire book. They felt endless. When you are sitting in a hospital room, grieving the loss of someone you love, facing a major life decision, or carrying burdens that seem too heavy to bear, it is difficult to imagine that one day those moments will simply become pages in your past. Yet that is exactly what happens. Time moves forward. Healing slowly begins. Perspective develops. What once seemed overwhelming eventually becomes part of the journey that shaped you.

That does not mean painful chapters suddenly become pleasant memories. Some experiences leave scars that never completely disappear. There are people we miss every day. There are losses that continue to ache long after they occur. There are moments we wish we could rewrite. However, even those painful chapters often teach us lessons that comfort and success never could. They teach us empathy. They teach us patience. They teach us how to survive when life doesn't go according to plan.

I have often found that the chapters we resist the most are the ones that produce the greatest growth. Nobody volunteers for hardship. Nobody hopes for heartbreak, disappointment, illness, or failure. Yet those experiences often reveal strengths we never knew we possessed. They force us to lean on our faith, depend on others, and discover what truly matters. They strip away distractions and remind us what is important. Looking back, many of the moments that shaped me the most were not the moments I celebrated. They were the moments I simply endured.

The same is true for many of the people around us. We often meet others in the middle of a chapter without knowing anything about the pages that came before. We see a smile but not the struggle. We see success but not the sacrifices. We see confidence but not the battles that were fought in private. Every person you encounter is carrying a story that you know very little about. That realization has taught me to be slower to judge and quicker to offer grace. Everyone is living through something.

One of the most remarkable things about life is how quickly a new chapter can begin. Sometimes it starts with a phone call. Sometimes it begins with a conversation. Sometimes it arrives through an unexpected opportunity, a new friendship, a change in circumstances, or a door that opens when you least expect it. What once seemed impossible suddenly becomes reality. The chapter that felt dark and discouraging gives way to one filled with hope and possibility.

If someone had told me years ago about some of the experiences, opportunities, and relationships that would become important parts of my life, I probably would not have believed them. Life has taken turns I never anticipated. Some chapters ended sooner than I expected. Others lasted much longer than I wanted. Yet every chapter contributed something valuable to the story. Each one taught a lesson, revealed a truth, or prepared me for something that was still ahead.

As people of faith, we often speak about trusting God, but trust is easiest when everything is going according to plan. The real test comes when we find ourselves in a chapter we do not understand. It is during those moments that faith becomes more than a concept. Faith becomes the confidence that God can see the entire story even when we can only see the page in front of us. He sees how the pieces fit together. He understands how today's struggles may prepare us for tomorrow's blessings.

Perhaps you are currently living through a chapter you would never have chosen. Maybe it is a chapter filled with uncertainty, grief, loneliness, disappointment, or questions about what comes next. If so, I hope you remember that this chapter is not your entire story. It may be an important part of your journey, but it is not the conclusion. There are still pages left to write. There are still lessons to learn, people to meet, memories to create, and blessings to discover.

The older I get, the more convinced I become that life is less about reaching the end of the book and more about appreciating the story as it unfolds. Every chapter matters. The joyful chapters remind us to celebrate. The difficult chapters remind us to persevere. The painful chapters teach compassion. The uncertain chapters strengthen our faith. Together, they create a story that is uniquely ours.

So if you find yourself struggling today, don't assume that the chapter you are living will last forever. Keep turning the page. Keep moving forward. Keep trusting that there is more to your story than what you can currently see. Some of the most meaningful and beautiful chapters of your life may still be waiting to be written.

After all, the story isn't over yet.

Take care of yourself, be kind to others, and remember that every chapter, even the difficult ones, has a purpose.

Read more from Dr. Nick at InspireWithDrNick.com

title: The Glass Half Full or Half Empty
excerpt: The Glass Half Full or Half Empty
content:
Is the Glass Half Empty or Half Full?

Few questions have been asked more often than this one: Is the glass half empty or half full?

Most of us have heard that question countless times throughout our lives. It has become the classic measure of optimism versus pessimism. If you see the glass as half full, you're considered positive and hopeful. If you see it as half empty, you're viewed as negative or discouraged. On the surface, it seems like a simple question with a simple answer.

But life rarely fits into simple categories.

Over the years, I've come to realize that our perspective is shaped by far more than a glass sitting on a table.
