The way we view any situation is often influenced by our experiences, our struggles, our victories, and the circumstances that brought us to that particular moment. What one person sees as a reason for gratitude, another may see as a reminder of loss.

Someone who has recently lost a loved one may naturally see the glass as half empty because grief has a way of coloring everything around us. On the other hand, someone who has just received encouraging news after months of uncertainty may see that same glass as half full. A person battling illness may view life differently than someone celebrating a major milestone. The glass itself remains unchanged, but the perspective through which it is viewed can be dramatically different.

That is one of the most fascinating truths about life. Two people can stand side by side, experience the same event, and walk away with entirely different conclusions. Neither person is necessarily wrong. They are simply interpreting the moment through the lens of their own experiences.

I understand this because I've lived it myself.

There have been seasons in my life when the glass felt nearly empty. Following cancer diagnoses, there were times when uncertainty occupied much of my thinking. There were moments after losing people I loved when it became difficult to see beyond the pain of the present. Like many people, I've experienced periods marked by loneliness, disappointment, grief, and frustration. During those times, it often felt easier to focus on what had been lost than on what remained.

If you've lived long enough, you've likely experienced similar moments. Life has a way of presenting challenges we never anticipated and circumstances we never would have chosen for ourselves. It may come in the form of a lost job, the end of a meaningful relationship, a difficult medical diagnosis, financial hardship, the death of someone we love, or a dream that never became reality. Sometimes it is the prayer that seems unanswered or the opportunity that never arrives.

When we're walking through those seasons, being told to "just stay positive" can feel hollow and dismissive. Real pain deserves to be acknowledged. Real disappointment cannot simply be wished away. There are moments when the glass genuinely feels half empty, and pretending otherwise doesn't help.

What I've learned, however, is that acknowledging hardship is not the same thing as surrendering to it. There is an important difference between recognizing reality and allowing that reality to define our entire outlook on life.

The people I admire most are not those who deny their struggles or pretend everything is perfect. Instead, they are individuals who face their challenges honestly while still choosing gratitude. They recognize the difficulties in front of them, but they refuse to let those difficulties become the whole story.

Throughout my life, I've met people fighting cancer who somehow continue to radiate joy. I've met individuals carrying tremendous grief who still find ways to encourage others. I've known people facing financial hardship who remain remarkably generous, and I've seen those who have experienced rejection, betrayal, and heartbreak continue to choose kindness.

These people are not ignoring reality. They are simply choosing to focus on more than their pain. They understand that perspective matters.

As I've grown older, I've come to believe that the real question isn't whether the glass is half empty or half full. A more meaningful question might be: What are you focusing on?

It's remarkably easy to focus on what we don't have. We think about the promotion we didn't receive, the house we wish we owned, the opportunities we missed, the mistakes we made, the people who disappointed us, and the plans that failed to unfold the way we expected. If we spend enough time concentrating on what is missing, life will always seem incomplete.

There will always be someone with more money, a larger home, a better job, or what appears to be a more comfortable life. Comparison has a way of draining joy from our lives because it shifts our attention away from our blessings and toward our perceived shortcomings. In many ways, comparison is one of the quickest ways to empty an otherwise full glass.

Gratitude, however, has the opposite effect.

Gratitude does not require us to ignore life's difficulties or pretend everything is perfect. Instead, it invites us to recognize what remains despite those difficulties. It encourages us to appreciate the people who stayed rather than dwelling exclusively on those who left. It helps us see the lessons learned through hardship and the strength developed through adversity. Gratitude allows us to appreciate another sunrise, another opportunity, and another day to make a positive difference in the lives of others.

I often think about the story of Jesus feeding the five thousand. Faced with a massive crowd and very limited resources, it would have been easy to focus on what was lacking. Instead, Jesus gave thanks for what was available. That lesson has always resonated deeply with me because it reminds us that God often works through what appears insufficient.

A small act of kindness can change someone's day. A brief conversation can provide encouragement when it is needed most. A simple invitation can create a meaningful connection. A prayer, a smile, or a few words of support may seem insignificant in the moment, yet God frequently uses those small things to accomplish far more than we imagine.

Too often, we underestimate what can happen when we focus on what we have rather than what we lack.

The reality is that every one of us carries a glass containing both blessings and challenges. No one escapes life without experiencing both joy and sorrow. The difference often lies in what we choose to notice and emphasize.

When we focus exclusively on disappointments, life begins to feel darker than it truly is. But when we intentionally recognize our blessings, hope begins to grow. Hope does not erase hardship or eliminate pain. What hope does is remind us that hardship does not have the final word.

I've also come to believe that sometimes the glass is neither half empty nor half full. Sometimes it's simply refillable.

That perspective has become increasingly meaningful to me because it reminds us that no single chapter determines the entire story. One failure does not define a lifetime. One loss does not eliminate the possibility of future joy. One closed door does not mean every opportunity has disappeared.

God has a remarkable way of filling glasses we once believed were permanently empty. He restores what has been broken. He brings healing where there has been pain. He offers comfort during seasons of grief and provides strength when we feel weak. Often, He works in ways we never expected and through circumstances we never would have chosen.

So the next time someone asks whether the glass is half empty or half full, perhaps the most honest answer is that it is both.

Life contains joy and sorrow, success and failure, gain and loss, laughter and tears. Those realities exist side by side. The challenge is not choosing one over the other but learning to recognize God's presence through all of it.

When we do, we discover something important. The glass may not always be full, but it is never truly empty. Not when faith remains. Not when hope survives. Not when God's grace continues to pour into our lives each and every day.

And perhaps that is the perspective that matters most.

Take care of yourself, and be kind to others.

title: The Long Pew
excerpt: Ever felt alone in a room
content:
Dr. Nick's Matter of Perspective
The Long Pew

Recently, I found myself sitting alone in a long church pew.

The church was not empty. Families filled the sanctuary. Couples sat together. Friends greeted one another before Mass. Familiar faces gathered in the same places they often do each week. Yet there I sat with several feet of empty space on either side of me. As people entered, I watched them scan the sanctuary looking for a place to sit. Many chose a different pew rather than sitting next to someone they did not know. Others squeezed into already crowded rows beside friends while entire sections remained nearly empty.

As I sat there, I began to wonder when we became so uncomfortable with one another.

Church should be one of the few places left in the world where nobody has to earn a seat. It should be a place where income, occupation, social status, politics, age, race, or background do not determine where we belong. The church is supposed to be a gathering of believers united by something much larger than ourselves. Yet sometimes it feels as though invisible barriers have made their way into our sanctuaries.

Growing up, I remember churches where people packed into pews shoulder to shoulder. If there was room, you sat there. If someone needed space, you moved over. Nobody thought much about it because everyone understood they were there for the same reason. Today, it sometimes feels as though we arrive carrying our own invisible walls, carefully protecting our personal space while forgetting that we are called to be a community.

What strikes me most is the contrast between what we profess and what we practice. We call the Church a family. We refer to one another as brothers and sisters in Christ. We speak about being part of the Body of Christ. We gather around the same altar, pray the same prayers, and receive the same Eucharist. The language of our faith is built around community, belonging, and relationship.

But if someone were attending our church for the very first time, would they experience any of those things?

Imagine finding the courage to walk into a church alone. Perhaps you have been away from church for years. Perhaps you recently lost a loved one. Maybe your marriage is struggling. Maybe you are battling depression, loneliness, or uncertainty. Perhaps you are simply searching for God and hoping to find answers. Walking through those doors may have taken every ounce of courage you could gather.

Now imagine entering that church and having nobody acknowledge your presence.

No smile.

No greeting.

No handshake.

No welcome.

You sit alone. You worship alone. You leave alone.

And you cannot help but wonder if anyone even noticed you were there.

The reality is that many churches spend significant time discussing outreach, evangelization, and growth. We create committees, programs, strategic plans, and mission statements. Yet one of the most powerful forms of evangelization requires no budget and no planning at all. It simply requires people to notice one another.

I have watched greeters stand near church entrances carrying on conversations with friends while visitors walk right past them unnoticed. I have seen newcomers enter a sanctuary and sit by themselves without a single person introducing themselves. I have watched people leave immediately after Mass without anyone speaking to them. It is difficult to understand because hospitality should be one of the easiest ways we live out our faith.

If someone can attend our parish for weeks, months, or even years without feeling connected, perhaps we need to ask ourselves some difficult questions.

Church should never feel like a private club. It should never feel like a gathering where only familiar faces matter. It should never feel like a place where people feel ignored, shunned, judged, or looked down upon. Yet the unfortunate truth is that some people leave churches carrying exactly those feelings.

Every person who walks through those doors matters. The widow sitting alone matters. The teenager who feels out of place matters. The single parent trying to hold life together matters. The person struggling with addiction matters. The family visiting for the first time matters. The individual whose clothes are worn and faded matters just as much as the person dressed in a tailored suit. Every one of them is a child of God deserving of dignity, respect, and welcome.

Perhaps this disconnect is most visible during the Sign of Peace.

For a brief moment during every Mass, we are invited to offer Christ's peace to one another. It is a simple gesture, yet one filled with meaning. We have spent the previous hour praying together as a community. We have listened to God's Word. We have professed our faith together. Then comes the moment when we are asked to acknowledge one another.

What happens next often says a great deal.

Some people warmly greet everyone around them. Others shake hands only with family members. Some look straight ahead and avoid eye contact altogether. I have watched people reach across aisles to greet friends while never acknowledging the person sitting directly beside them. It is a small thing, perhaps, but it speaks volumes.

How can we speak of unity while avoiding connection?

How can we celebrate community while keeping one another at arm's length?

How can we gather around the same altar and hesitate to offer a simple smile, handshake, or greeting?

The answer may be uncomfortable. Sometimes we become so focused on ourselves that we forget to see those around us. We become creatures of habit. We sit in the same places. We talk to the same people. We interact with the same circles. Without realizing it, we create environments that feel welcoming to those we know and isolating to those we do not.

Yet when we look at the life of Jesus, we see something entirely different.

Jesus consistently moved toward people, not away from them. He sat with tax collectors. He welcomed sinners. He spoke with those society rejected. He touched lepers when others would not come near them. He crossed boundaries that others refused to cross because He saw people not as categories, labels, or inconveniences, but as beloved children of God.

If we truly want to follow Christ, then perhaps we should spend less time worrying about appearances and more time worrying about hospitality.

Every Sunday, people arrive wearing their Sunday's best. They carefully choose their clothes. They make sure everything looks right on the outside. There is nothing wrong with presenting ourselves respectfully before God. However, I sometimes wonder if we spend more time preparing our wardrobe than preparing our hearts.

Because Sunday's best was never supposed to be about a suit, a dress, a tie, or a pair of polished shoes.

Sunday's best is kindness.

Sunday's best is compassion.

Sunday's best is hospitality.

Sunday's best is making room for someone who feels alone.

Sunday's best is introducing yourself to a visitor.

Sunday's best is offering a genuine smile.

Sunday's best is making another person feel like they belong.

The early Christian Church grew because people encountered something they could not find elsewhere. They found belonging. They found acceptance. They found a community that genuinely cared for one another. People were not drawn to Christianity because of impressive buildings or perfect programs. They were drawn because they encountered love.

That same opportunity exists every single Sunday.

The person sitting alone in the long pew may be grieving. They may be carrying burdens nobody can see. They may be questioning their faith. They may have nowhere else to go. They may simply need someone to acknowledge that they matter.

A smile may seem insignificant.

A handshake may seem ordinary.

A simple "Good morning" may feel small.

But to someone who feels invisible, those gestures can mean everything.

As I sat in that long pew, I realized the empty space beside me represented something much larger than a few feet of wood. It represented an opportunity. An opportunity for all of us to become more welcoming, more compassionate, and more intentional in how we treat one another.

The Church is not the building. It never has been. The Church is the people. It is the family of believers gathered together in worship. If we truly believe that, then our churches should be places where every person who enters feels seen, valued, respected, and loved.

At the end of the day, perhaps the greatest testimony a church can offer is not found in its architecture, its music, or even its programs. Perhaps it is found in whether a stranger walks through the doors and leaves feeling like family.

Because if we cannot practice Christian fellowship inside the walls of a church, where exactly do we expect to learn it?

Maybe next Sunday, instead of focusing on Sunday's best, we should focus on bringing our best selves.

I suspect that's the offering God notices most.

title: Raising an 18 year old.
excerpt: "Sometimes the family you never expected becomes one of God's greatest blessings."
content:
Becoming Dad at 47

I never expected to become a dad at 47 years old.

If you had asked me ten years ago, five years ago, or even a couple of years ago what my future looked like, becoming a parent to an 18-year-old would not have been on my list. Like most people, I had a picture in my mind of how life would unfold. There were plans, goals, dreams, and expectations. Yet life has a remarkable way of rewriting our stories when we least expect it. Sometimes the most meaningful chapters are the ones we never planned to write.

For me, that unexpected chapter arrived in the form of a young man named Canyon.

Before anyone imagines some perfect movie script where everything fell neatly into place, let me assure you that it did not. There was no instruction manual waiting on the kitchen table. No online course called "How to Become a Dad Overnight." There was certainly no chapter titled "So You Accidentally Became a Parent to an Almost Adult."

The truth is that I had no idea what I was doing.

And perhaps that is one of the biggest secrets of parenting. Most parents don't have all the answers. They simply love enough to keep showing up. They learn as they go. They make mistakes, second-guess themselves, worry constantly, and somehow continue moving forward because the person they care about is worth every ounce of effort.

What I quickly learned is that stepping into a father role isn't about perfection. It isn't about having all the answers or solving every problem. More often than not, it is about being present. It is about being the person someone can call when life gets messy. It is about listening more than speaking. It is about offering guidance while also understanding that people sometimes have to learn lessons on their own.

At first, I found myself wondering what role I was supposed to play. Was I supposed to be the disciplinarian, the advisor, the encourager, the provider, the chauffeur, the life coach, or the sounding board? As it turns out, the answer was a little bit of everything.

What nobody tells you about loving someone deeply is how much space they begin to occupy in your heart. Suddenly their successes matter to you. Their struggles matter to you. Their dreams become important to you. Their setbacks hurt your heart. Their victories make you proud in ways that are difficult to describe.

Love changes your perspective.

The funny thing is that Canyon probably has no idea how much I worry about him. I worry about his future. I worry about the decisions he will make. I worry when he is unusually quiet. I worry when life gets difficult for him. I worry because caring about someone means carrying a piece of their journey in your own heart.

Looking back, I owe my own parents, grandparents, mentors, and countless other people an apology. I finally understand why they asked so many questions. The questions that once felt annoying now make perfect sense.

Did you eat?

Did you get home safely?

How did your day go?

What are your plans?

Are you okay?

Those questions are not really about information. They are about love. They are the language of people who care.

One of the greatest privileges of becoming a father figure has been having a front-row seat to someone else's growth. I have watched Canyon graduate. I have watched him work hard. I have watched him overcome challenges and continue moving forward. I have watched him mature into a young man with a good heart, strong values, and a desire to build a meaningful future.

There is something incredibly rewarding about witnessing someone discover who they are.

People often think parenting is about shaping a child. While there is certainly some truth in that, I believe it is equally about watching a person become who they were always meant to be. You don't create their identity. You help them discover it. You encourage them when they doubt themselves. You remind them of their strengths when all they can see are their weaknesses.

Sometimes you become the voice that reminds them they are capable when the world is telling them otherwise.

One of the unexpected gifts of bringing Canyon into my life has been the adventure that came with it. Before he entered my world, life had become comfortable and predictable. There is nothing wrong with routine, but routine has a way of making us stop noticing the beauty around us.

Then suddenly, there was someone else's future to invest in, someone else's milestones to celebrate, and someone else's dreams to encourage.

There were graduations, celebrations, church services, dinners, conversations, road trips, and countless ordinary moments that became extraordinary memories. Some of the most meaningful experiences weren't major life events at all. They were the moments in between. The random conversations while driving. The laughter over dinner. The discussions about life, faith, relationships, and dreams. Those moments are often where the strongest bonds are formed.

I've learned that some of life's greatest joys come from celebrating victories that nobody else sees. A difficult semester completed. A goal achieved. A challenge overcome. A door opened. A lesson learned. A moment of growth. These moments may not make headlines, but they matter because they represent progress.

As parents, mentors, and caregivers, we discover that these seemingly small victories often carry the greatest significance because they show that a person is moving forward. Life is not built in giant leaps. It is built through thousands of small steps.

One of the greatest blessings has been watching Canyon strengthen and renew his relationship with God. Faith is not always a straight path. It involves questions, struggles, discoveries, setbacks, and growth. Watching him explore his faith has reminded me of my own journey and reinforced the importance of keeping God at the center of our lives.

Some of our most meaningful conversations have revolved around purpose, faith, forgiveness, grace, and what it means to become the person God created us to be. Those discussions have taught me that being a father figure is not about providing every answer. Sometimes it is about walking alongside someone while both of you continue learning.

The truth is that Canyon has probably taught me as much as I have taught him.

He has reminded me to laugh more often. He has reminded me to appreciate the present moment instead of constantly focusing on the future. He has reminded me that progress matters more than perfection and that people need encouragement far more often than criticism.

Perhaps most importantly, he has reminded me that life is still full of surprises.

There is a common misconception that by the time we reach our forties or fifties, life has largely settled into place. We think the biggest adventures are behind us. We assume the major chapters have already been written.

Yet Canyon reminded me that God is not finished writing our stories.

Sometimes He introduces new people into our lives who change us in ways we never expected. Sometimes He places opportunities before us that challenge us to grow. Sometimes He calls us into roles we never imagined ourselves filling.

Most importantly, Canyon has reminded me that family is not always defined by blood.

Some of the most meaningful relationships in our lives are the ones we never saw coming. They are built through trust, commitment, consistency, and a willingness to show up day after day. They are formed through shared experiences, mutual respect, and the simple decision to care about one another.

When people ask what it is like to become a dad at 47, I find it difficult to answer with a single word. It has been joyful, exhausting, confusing, rewarding, challenging, humbling, and deeply meaningful all at the same time.

It has also been one of the greatest blessings of my life.

There are still plenty of mysteries ahead. There will be career decisions, relationships, opportunities, disappointments, successes, and countless life experiences that neither of us can predict. I do not know exactly what the future holds for Canyon, and I certainly do not know where all of life's roads will lead him.

What I do know is that no matter where those roads go, I will always be in his corner.

I will celebrate his victories. I will encourage him during setbacks. I will remind him of his worth when he forgets it. I will pray for him, support him, and continue believing in him even during moments when he struggles to believe in himself.

And yes, I will probably continue asking whether he remembered to eat.

Because somewhere along the way, without any grand announcement or defining moment, I became Dad.

Not because of biology, paperwork, or a title. I became Dad through love, commitment, faith, trust, and the decision to invest in another person's life.

Looking back, I thought I was helping shape Canyon's future. What I didn't realize was that he was shaping mine as well.

The greatest lesson of all may be this: sometimes the people we help end up helping us just as much. Sometimes the blessings we think we are giving are actually blessings we are receiving. And sometimes the family we never expected becomes one of God's greatest gifts.

For that gift, I will always be grateful.

– Dr. Nick Palisch
"Sometimes the family you never expected becomes one of God's greatest blessings."

title: What is Your Legacy?
excerpt: "Your life may be the chapter in someone else's story that helps them keep going."
content:
What Will You Leave Behind?

One day, whether we like to think about it or not, every one of us will leave this earth. Life, as precious as it is, is temporary. The jobs we worked so hard to obtain will eventually be filled by someone else. The homes we carefully maintained will belong to another family. The cars we drove will eventually sit in someone else's driveway. The titles we spent years earning may fade from memory, and the accomplishments that once seemed so important will slowly become part of history.

The question is not whether we will leave this world. The question is what we will leave behind when we do.

As I have gotten older, I find myself thinking less about what I am accumulating and more about what I am contributing. I think less about what I own and more about who I have helped. I spend less time worrying about achievements and more time wondering about impact. Perhaps that is one of the gifts that comes with age and experience. We begin to understand that life is not measured solely by what we gather for ourselves but by what we give away to others.

Legacy is an interesting thing. Many people assume it is built through wealth, status, power, or recognition. While those things may become part of someone's story, they are rarely the things people remember most.

When we attend a funeral or gather to celebrate someone's life, the conversation almost never revolves around how much money they earned, the size of their home, or the titles printed on their business card. Instead, people share stories. They talk about kindness. They remember laughter. They recall generosity, compassion, faith, encouragement, and love. They tell stories about the difference that person made in their lives.

At the end of the day, people remember how we made them feel.

I often think about my grandparents when reflecting on legacy. There are countless details of my childhood that have faded over time. I cannot remember every gift they bought me, every meal we shared, or every conversation we had. What remains crystal clear, however, is how they made me feel.

I remember riding through the countryside with Papa, looking for deer and wildlife. I remember sitting with him and learning lessons that had little to do with words and everything to do with presence. I remember Mimi spoiling me when she probably shouldn't have, making me laugh, and making me feel like the most important person in the world whenever I walked through the door.

Most of all, I remember feeling loved.

I remember feeling safe.

I remember feeling valued.

I remember feeling like I mattered.

That was their legacy.

Not because they intentionally set out to create one, but because they lived one every day through their actions. Their influence continues to shape my life long after they are gone.

The same can be said for teachers, coaches, clergy members, mentors, neighbors, coworkers, and friends who have crossed our paths. Most never realized the impact they were having. They simply showed up. They listened when someone needed to talk. They offered encouragement during difficult times. They believed in people who struggled to believe in themselves.

Years later, we still carry pieces of them with us.

That is legacy.

The older I get, the more convinced I become that legacy is not built at the end of life. It is built every single day through ordinary decisions and everyday moments. It is built when we choose patience instead of anger. It is built when we offer encouragement to someone who is struggling. It is built when we show up for our family, when we forgive, when we choose integrity even when nobody is watching, and when we invest our time in people rather than possessions.

As many of you know, one of the projects closest to my heart is "Everyone Has a Story." The reason I started that initiative is simple. I believe ordinary people leave extraordinary legacies.

The mother working two jobs to provide for her children is building a legacy.

The grandfather who never misses a ballgame is building a legacy.

The teacher who stays after school to help a struggling student is building a legacy.

The nurse who comforts a frightened patient, the dispatcher who answers a life-changing call, the volunteer who quietly serves without recognition, the neighbor who checks in on someone living alone, and the friend who refuses to give up on someone during their darkest days are all building legacies.

Most of these people will never be famous.

Few will ever appear on television, have buildings named after them, or receive major awards. Yet their impact may outlast many who do.

The world often measures success by visibility. God measures it by faithfulness.

That distinction matters.

Some of the greatest legacies are built in moments that never make headlines and are witnessed by only a handful of people. Yet those moments often change lives in ways we may never fully understand.

As a Christian, I believe there is another layer to this conversation. While I certainly hope people remember my career, my writing, my weather forecasts, and the organizations I have served, I hope those things are not what define me most.

I hope people remember that I tried to love others.

I hope they remember that I believed every person had worth and dignity.

I hope they remember that I tried to offer hope to people who were struggling.

I hope they remember that I pointed others toward kindness, compassion, and faith.

Most importantly, I hope they remember that I loved God and tried to live that faith, imperfectly but sincerely.

Because when everything else is stripped away, faith, hope, and love are what remain.

Recently, I have spent a great deal of time thinking about the people who shaped my life. My grandparents. My stepfather Rick. Friends I have lost. Mentors who believed in me when I doubted myself. Teachers who saw potential where I saw limitations. Each of them left fingerprints on my heart that time cannot erase.

Their stories continue through me.

The lessons they taught, the values they modeled, and the love they shared continue to influence my decisions every day. In many ways, that is what legacy truly is. It is not something we leave behind. It is something we leave within others.

A legacy may be a lesson someone carries for decades. It may be a memory that provides comfort during difficult times. It may be a smile, a value, a belief, or a moment that changes the direction of someone's life forever.

Years from now, someone may not remember where you worked or what kind of car you drove. They may not remember your job title or your professional accomplishments. What they may remember is the encouragement you offered when they were ready to quit. They may remember your compassion when they were hurting. They may remember your faith during a difficult season. They may remember your kindness when the world seemed unkind.

And that memory may be enough to change their life forever.

So today, I leave you with a question worth considering.

If your story ended tomorrow, what would people say you left behind?

Would they talk about what you owned, what you earned, or what you accumulated? Or would they talk about the lives you touched, the people you encouraged, and the love you shared?

Who is better because you were part of their story?

Who feels valued because you took the time to care?

Who has hope because you refused to give up on them?

Because in the end, our greatest legacy will never be measured by what we leave behind. It will be measured by what continues to live on because we were here.

And that, my friends, is a legacy worth building.

– Dr. Nick Palisch

"Your life may be the chapter in someone else's story that helps them keep going."

title: Feeling Like a Stray
excerpt: Ever feel like you don't belong.  You feel Alone?
content:
Feeling Like a Stray

Have you ever felt like a stray?

Not in the literal sense, but in the way a stray animal wanders through life searching for a place to belong. Searching for acceptance. Searching for a home. Searching for someone who sees value beyond appearances, circumstances, or mistakes. Most of us have experienced that feeling at some point in our lives. It may have happened in school when we never quite fit into a particular group, at work when we felt overlooked or forgotten, or even within our own families when everyone else seemed to know exactly where they belonged while we were still trying to find our place.

Feeling like a stray is often less about being alone and more about feeling unseen. You can be surrounded by people and still feel disconnected. You can sit in a crowded room and wonder if anyone truly knows you. You can spend years trying to earn acceptance from people who have already decided who you are without ever taking the time to understand your story.

The truth is that many of us spend far too much of our lives chasing places where we were never meant to belong. We try to fit into circles that require us to change who we are. We hide pieces of ourselves in order to gain approval. We stay in friendships, relationships, organizations, and situations long after we know they are unhealthy because we fear that leaving means ending up alone.

Like a stray standing outside a door that never opens, we wait. We wait for acceptance. We wait for validation. We wait for someone else to tell us we matter. We convince ourselves that if we can just say the right thing, do the right thing, or become the person others expect us to be, then perhaps we will finally be welcomed in.

The problem is that our worth was never meant to be determined by someone else's willingness to let us in.

One of the greatest lessons I have learned is that acceptance and belonging are not the same thing. Acceptance is being allowed into the room. Belonging is being valued once you are there. There is a profound difference between the two. Many people spend years searching for acceptance when what their heart is truly longing for is belonging.

I know what it feels like to be the outsider. I know what it feels like to wonder if you fit in, to question where you belong, and to wonder whether anyone truly understands your journey. Yet I have also learned that there is something remarkable about those seasons of life.

People who have felt like strays often develop strengths that others never have to learn. They become resilient because they have endured rejection. They become compassionate because they know what loneliness feels like. They learn to recognize pain in others because they have carried it themselves. They discover how to keep moving forward even when the path ahead is uncertain.

Most importantly, they learn how to love deeply because they understand what it feels like to be forgotten.

Perhaps that is why some of the kindest people you will ever meet are those who once felt lost themselves. They remember what it was like to stand on the outside looking in. They remember wondering whether anyone cared. They remember feeling invisible. Because they remember, they make a conscious effort to ensure that others never experience those same feelings. They become the welcoming voice, the encouraging friend, the listening ear, and the open door they once needed themselves.

There is something else worth remembering. Even strays eventually find their people.

Sometimes it takes longer than we would like. Sometimes the journey is messy and filled with disappointments. Sometimes there are wrong turns, broken relationships, and seasons of loneliness along the way. Yet eventually, many of us discover the people who love us for who we are rather than who they want us to become.

We find friends who become family. We find mentors who guide us. We find communities that embrace us. We find purpose that anchors us. Often we discover that the home we were searching for was never a physical place at all. It was a collection of people who chose to walk beside us through life's highs and lows.

If you are feeling like a stray today, I want to encourage you not to give up. Do not assume that because one door closed, every door will. Do not assume that because one person failed to see your value, no one else will. Do not assume that because you feel lost today, you will always feel lost.

You matter. Your story matters. Your life has purpose. There is a place for you in this world, even if you have not found it yet. Sometimes the hardest part of the journey is simply continuing long enough to discover where you truly belong.

And perhaps the greatest perspective of all is this: God has never viewed you as a stray.

While the world may sometimes make you feel unwanted, forgotten, or out of place, God never does. He knows your name. He knows your story. He knows your struggles, your fears, your heartbreaks, and your dreams. He sees the parts of your life that others overlook and loves you completely anyway.

You were created intentionally. You were created with purpose. You were created to be loved.

When we begin to understand that truth, we realize something powerful. We were never truly strays at all. We were simply travelers on a journey, searching for the place where we could finally understand our worth.

And perhaps, all along, we were simply finding our way home.

Remember this: The people who feel like strays today often become the very people who create homes for others tomorrow. Your experiences, your struggles, and your journey may one day become the safe place someone else desperately needs. Sometimes the greatest purpose behind feeling lost is learning how to help others find their way.

– Dr. Nick Palisch
"The world may see a stray. God sees one of His own."

title: Precision, Wonder, and Working Together
excerpt: Teamwork and collaboration
content:
Precision, Wonder, and Working Together

Watching the Blue Angels and the Canadian Snowbirds practice this week ahead of the Chesterfield Air Show had me thinking.

Like many people, I found myself stopping what I was doing whenever I heard the familiar roar of jet engines overhead. I'd step outside, look toward the sky, and watch as these incredible teams carved graceful patterns through the clouds. Even from a distance, there was something captivating about it. The speed, the precision, the timing, and the trust required to perform those maneuvers are difficult to fully comprehend until you see them with your own eyes.

As impressive as the aircraft are, what stood out to me most wasn't the planes themselves. It was what they represented.

When we watch an air show, we often focus on the excitement. We marvel at the tight formations, the daring passes, and the seemingly impossible precision. What we don't see are the countless hours of preparation behind every maneuver. We don't see the training, the discipline, the communication, and the commitment required to make those performances appear effortless. Every movement is intentional. Every pilot knows their role. Every member of the team trusts the others completely.

That level of trust is something that extends far beyond aviation.

The Blue Angels and the Canadian Snowbirds remind us that extraordinary things happen when people work together toward a common goal. Neither team is built around a single individual. Success depends on every person doing their part and understanding how their role contributes to the larger mission. One small mistake can affect the entire formation, which is why preparation, communication, and teamwork are so important.

There is a lesson in that for all of us.

Whether it's within our families, workplaces, churches, schools, or communities, we are often part of something bigger than ourselves. We may not always be in the spotlight, but our contributions matter. Just as every pilot in a formation plays a critical role, every person in our lives has the ability to make a difference. The strongest teams are not built on individual accomplishments alone. They are built on trust, respect, and a shared commitment to helping one another succeed.

What also struck me while watching these teams practice was the sense of wonder they create. For a few moments, people stop scrolling on their phones. Neighbors come outside. Children point toward the sky. Complete strangers gather together to watch something remarkable unfold above them. In a world that often feels rushed, divided, and distracted, those moments are refreshing.

The Blue Angels represent the United States, while the Snowbirds represent Canada. Although they come from different countries and fly different aircraft, they share many of the same values: professionalism, service, excellence, and teamwork. Seeing them perform at the same event is a reminder that some things transcend borders. The pursuit of excellence, the spirit of cooperation, and the ability to inspire others are universal.

Perhaps that is why air shows continue to draw crowds generation after generation. They are about much more than airplanes. They remind us what human beings can accomplish through dedication and teamwork. They inspire young people to dream bigger. They reconnect adults with a sense of awe that often gets buried beneath daily responsibilities. They encourage us to look up, both literally and figuratively.

As I watched these teams practice over Chesterfield this week, I couldn't help but think about how much our world needs more examples of people working together. We hear so much about division, disagreement, and conflict. Yet here were two elite demonstration teams showing us what is possible when individuals trust one another, communicate effectively, and pursue a common purpose.

The next time you see the Blue Angels or the Canadian Snowbirds take to the sky, take a moment to appreciate more than just the performance. Appreciate the teamwork behind it. Appreciate the trust. Appreciate the preparation. And appreciate the wonder that still has the power to bring people together, even if only for a few moments.

Sometimes the sky teaches lessons that we can apply right here on the ground.

Take care of yourself and be kind to others.

Dr. Nick Palisch

Read more at www.inspirewithdrnick.com

title: In Remberance: Three years later
excerpt: The face of grief changes, but the pain remains.
content:
Three Years Later

Three years.

It hardly seems possible that three years have passed since we lost Rick in a tragic motorcycle accident. In some ways, it feels like a lifetime ago. In other ways, it feels like yesterday. Grief has a funny way of working like that. Time moves forward, yet certain memories remain frozen in place, as vivid and real as the day they were made.

Rick was the kind of person who rarely sought attention. He didn't need recognition to do the right thing. He simply showed up. If your car was making a strange noise, Rick was there. If a picture needed hanging, a repair needed making, or a project seemed impossible, Rick was usually the first person to offer help. He was one of those people who quietly made life easier for everyone around him.

What I remember most is not the big moments. It is the ordinary ones.

It is the phone calls asking for advice. It is watching him work on something with patience and determination. It is the countless ways he demonstrated love through action rather than words. Rick taught us that caring for others isn't always about grand gestures. Sometimes it is fixing a problem, lending a hand, or simply being there when someone needs you.

His passing left a hole in our family that can never truly be filled. There are still moments when I think about calling him. There are still times when something happens and my first thought is, "Rick would know how to fix this." Those moments remind me that love doesn't disappear when someone dies. It remains woven into the fabric of our lives.

As the years pass, grief changes. The sharp pain softens, but the missing never completely goes away. Instead, it becomes part of who we are. We learn to carry both the loss and the love. We learn to smile at memories that once made us cry. We learn to be grateful for the time we had, even when we wish it could have been longer.

Rick was more than a stepfather. He was family. He was a helper, a fixer, a provider, and a man who cared deeply about the people he loved. His legacy lives on in the lessons he taught, the lives he touched, and the memories we continue to share.

Today, on the third anniversary of his passing, I find myself reflecting not only on what we lost but also on what we were blessed to have. We were blessed to know him. We were blessed to laugh with him, learn from him, and share life with him.

Rick, you are missed more than words can express.

You are remembered more often than you know.

And you are loved still.

Always.

— Dr. Nick Palisch

title: Puzzle Pieces
excerpt: Life can be a puzzle.  The task for us is to make the pieces fit together.
content:
Puzzle Pieces

There is something fascinating about sitting down to work on a puzzle. When you first dump the box onto the table, all you see is a pile of scattered pieces. Some are upside down. Some seem completely unrelated to the others. A few fit together quickly, while many leave you scratching your head and wondering where they belong. If you focus on just one piece, it rarely tells you much about the final image. It is only when the pieces begin connecting that the bigger picture starts to emerge.

Life often unfolds the same way.

Many of us spend our lives trying to understand why certain things happen when they do. We wonder why some doors open while others close. We question why we experience loss, disappointment, setbacks, heartbreak, and unexpected detours. In the moment, those experiences can feel isolated and disconnected, as though they serve no real purpose. We struggle to understand how they fit into the story we are trying to build for ourselves.

The challenge is that we are usually trying to evaluate our lives while we are still living them. We are looking at individual pieces without being able to see the entire puzzle. We want immediate answers and clear explanations. We want to know why a relationship ended, why a career path changed, why a prayer seemed to go unanswered, or why life led us in a direction we never anticipated. Yet life rarely provides those answers in real time.

When I reflect on my own journey, I can see countless puzzle pieces that made little sense when they first appeared. Growing up in a small town, being bullied as a kid, battling cancer, losing loved ones, changing careers, earning degrees, moving away from home, and eventually returning to Missouri after my father's death all felt like separate chapters at the time. There were moments of incredible joy and moments of profound grief. There were seasons filled with confidence and others filled with uncertainty.

Looking back now, I can see how those pieces connect. Experiences that once felt painful taught resilience. Seasons of loss deepened my appreciation for relationships. Challenges I wished I could avoid ultimately helped shape my faith, my perspective, and my understanding of what truly matters. The pieces that seemed out of place were often the ones that contributed most to the overall picture.

I think many of us are carrying a puzzle piece right now that we do not understand. Perhaps it is a health challenge, a job transition, a family struggle, a financial burden, or the loss of someone we love. Maybe it is a season of loneliness or uncertainty. Whatever it may be, it is easy to become frustrated when we cannot immediately see where that piece belongs. We convince ourselves that something has gone wrong because the picture is incomplete.

The reality is that every life contains pieces that are difficult to understand while we are living through them. The mistake we often make is assuming that because we cannot see the purpose today, there is no purpose at all. Time has a remarkable way of revealing connections we never could have imagined. Years later, we often discover that the very experience we questioned most became the catalyst for growth, wisdom, compassion, or opportunity.

Faith plays an important role in this process. Faith is not about having all the answers. It is about trusting that there is a picture being formed even when we cannot see it ourselves. It is believing that God sees the entire puzzle while we are holding only a handful of pieces. It is trusting that even the difficult seasons have value, even when that value is not immediately visible.

The people who enter our lives are puzzle pieces as well. Some remain with us for decades. Others stay for only a brief period. Regardless of how long they are present, they often leave an impact that becomes part of our story. They teach us lessons about love, forgiveness, perseverance, kindness, and sometimes even heartbreak. Every person we encounter contributes something to the picture that is being created.

As I have grown older, I have become less concerned with trying to force every piece into place. I have learned that some answers come quickly while others take years to reveal themselves. Not every chapter makes sense while it is being written. Sometimes the best thing we can do is trust the process, continue moving forward, and allow life to unfold as it will.

If you find yourself staring at a confusing piece of your own puzzle today, remember that you are not looking at the finished picture. You are seeing only one part of a much larger story. The challenges you face today may someday become the very experiences that define your strength, deepen your faith, or allow you to help someone else through a similar struggle.

One day, perhaps years from now, you may look back and realize that what seemed random, painful, or unnecessary was actually connecting everything together. The picture may not be complete yet, but that does not mean it is broken. It simply means the story is still being written.

And sometimes the most beautiful pictures are the ones that take the longest to come together.

— Dr. Nick Palisch

title: Thunderstorms
excerpt: There is something about thunderstorms that brings many emotions.
content:
Thunderstorms

There’s something about a thunderstorm that gets your attention.

Maybe it’s the distant rumble that slowly grows louder. Maybe it’s the dark clouds rolling across the horizon like a freight train in the sky. Maybe it’s the sudden stillness before the first drop of rain falls. Thunderstorms have a way of making people pause. They remind us very quickly that no matter how advanced we become, nature still has the ability to humble us.

I’ve loved storms for as long as I can remember.

As a kid growing up in southeast Missouri, I would sit outside and watch the sky change colors before a storm rolled in. You could smell the rain before it arrived. The wind would pick up. Trees would sway. Birds would disappear. The atmosphere itself felt different. Back then, storms were both exciting and mysterious. Honestly, they still are.

Over the years, thunderstorms became more than just weather to me. They became part of my life story. They led me into meteorology, storm chasing, forecasting, television, and countless nights staring at radar screens while everyone else was asleep. I’ve spent hours watching lightning dance across the sky like nature’s own fireworks show. I’ve also seen the destruction storms can leave behind. Homes damaged. Trees snapped. Lives forever changed.

That’s the thing about thunderstorms. They are beautiful and dangerous at the same time.

Kind of like life.

Some storms move through quickly. Others sit over us for a while. Some storms announce themselves loudly with thunder and lightning. Others quietly build in the distance before we even realize what’s happening. Isn’t that true for life too?

Sometimes our storms are visible to everyone around us. A loss. A diagnosis. Financial struggles. Divorce. Grief. Anxiety. Other times, the storms we battle are completely internal. Nobody sees the lightning inside someone’s mind. Nobody hears the thunder rolling through their heart. Outwardly they smile, work, joke, and show up for life while privately trying to survive the storm within them.

That’s why I think thunderstorms teach us something important.

Storms do not last forever.

It may feel endless when you are in the middle of one. The thunder shakes the windows. The wind howls. The rain pours so hard you can barely see what’s ahead. But eventually, the storm moves on. The clouds break apart. The sky clears. The sun slowly returns.

Life works that way too, even when we forget it.

I think some of the strongest people are the ones who kept going while the storm was raging around them. They kept showing up for their family while their own heart was exhausted. They kept believing even when life gave them every reason to quit. They kept loving people despite being hurt themselves.

There is strength in surviving storms people never even knew you faced.

As a meteorologist, I can tell you thunderstorms are incredibly complex. There are ingredients that have to come together perfectly for storms to form. Heat. Moisture. Instability. Wind shear. Lift. Remove one ingredient and the storm may never fully develop.

People are similar.

The experiences we go through shape us. Pain shapes us. Love shapes us. Loss shapes us. Faith shapes us. Failure shapes us. Sometimes the very things we wish we could avoid end up building the strongest parts of who we become.

Now let me be honest for a moment. Not everyone enjoys thunderstorms the way I do. Some people are terrified of them. Some associate storms with trauma or loss. And that’s understandable. Storms can be frightening.

Life can be too.

But there is also something oddly comforting about sitting safely inside while rain hits the roof and thunder echoes in the distance. It slows the world down for a little while. It reminds us to pause. To reflect. To be still for a moment.

Some of my favorite memories involve thunderstorms. Sitting on the porch with family. Watching lightning bugs before the rain arrived. Listening to the rumble of thunder at night. Even now, my corgis react differently every time storms roll through. Mogwai acts like he’s part of the First Warn Weather Team. Winston looks personally offended that rain exists. Willow keeps watch like she’s protecting the entire house from atmospheric betrayal. Baxter mostly just follows everyone else and tries to look important.

Truthfully, storms have a personality.

And so do people.

Some storms look intimidating but weaken quickly. Others appear harmless at first before exploding with intensity. Some people are loud storms. Others quietly carry hurricanes inside them while pretending everything is fine.

That’s why kindness matters more than we realize.

You never truly know what kind of storm someone is walking through.

Maybe that person smiling at work cried all night before coming in. Maybe the person who seems distant is emotionally exhausted. Maybe someone you know is one bad day away from completely falling apart. We never fully know the battles people are facing internally.

Thunderstorms remind me of another truth too. Sometimes growth requires disruption.

Rain nourishes the ground. Storms break humidity and cool the air. Lightning even helps release nitrogen into the atmosphere, benefiting the soil. What looks destructive in one moment can actually produce growth later on.

The same can happen in life.

The hardest seasons sometimes reshape us into stronger, wiser, more compassionate people. Not because pain is good, but because we learn, adapt, survive, and grow through it.

And maybe that’s the perspective today.

Do not assume your storm means your story is over.

Sometimes the thunder is simply clearing the way for something new.

And sometimes after the darkest clouds pass, you finally notice how beautiful the sky can become.

Stay grounded. Stay hopeful. And if life gets stormy, keep moving forward one step at a time.

The skies eventually clear.

They always do.

title: You are not Junk
excerpt: We are not junk.  We are imperfect people.
content:
God Does Not Make Junk

We live in a world that is very quick to label people. Somewhere along the way, many people begin carrying around the belief that they are somehow less than others because of their mistakes, struggles, failures, appearance, past, or circumstances. Over time, rejection, heartbreak, bullying, criticism, trauma, loss, and disappointment can slowly convince someone that they are not worthy, not valuable, or not important.

But I want to remind you of something today: you are not junk. God does not make junk.

Yes, we are imperfect. Every single one of us is. We all have flaws, insecurities, regrets, fears, and moments where we fall short. We lose patience. We say things we wish we could take back. We struggle emotionally, spiritually, mentally, and physically at times. Some people battle anxiety quietly. Others carry grief they never fully speak about. Some wrestle with loneliness, addiction, self-doubt, or wounds from childhood that still affect them years later. Being imperfect is part of being human. It does not make someone worthless.

Unfortunately, the world often teaches people that their worth must be earned. People begin believing they have to be more attractive, more successful, more accomplished, wealthier, smarter, stronger, or more accepted in order to matter. Social media has only amplified that mindset. We constantly see polished versions of people’s lives while quietly comparing ourselves to unrealistic expectations. Many people are exhausted from trying to prove they are enough in a world that constantly moves the finish line.

The truth is, some people spend their entire lives apologizing for existing. They try to shrink themselves to fit into places where they were never appreciated to begin with. They carry shame for things God has already forgiven. They replay old mistakes over and over in their minds until those failures become part of their identity. Over time, they stop seeing themselves the way God sees them.

I think about the people who have been rejected for being different. The kid who was bullied in school. The person abandoned by someone they loved. The individual quietly battling depression while still smiling in public every day. The person who feels forgotten, overlooked, or invisible. There are people all around us carrying invisible scars while pretending everything is fine because they fear being judged if they admit they are struggling.

What makes this even harder is that society often praises perfection while hiding its own brokenness. People are criticized for their failures while others quietly conceal their own. Sometimes even churches, which are supposed to reflect love and grace, have made hurting people feel unwanted instead of welcomed. That pain can leave lasting scars on someone’s heart and faith.

But throughout Scripture, God consistently used imperfect people. Moses doubted himself. David made devastating mistakes. Peter denied Jesus. Thomas struggled with doubt. Paul persecuted Christians before becoming one of the strongest voices of faith in history. The disciples themselves were ordinary, flawed, emotional people who often misunderstood what God was doing around them. Yet God still called them, loved them, and worked through them.

That matters because it reminds us that God does not wait for perfection before loving people. If perfection were required, none of us would qualify.

The older I get, the more I realize how difficult grace can be for people to accept. Many individuals can forgive others easier than they forgive themselves. They define their entire worth by the worst moment of their life, the worst thing someone ever said to them, or the hardest season they survived. They begin to see themselves through the lens of failure instead of through the lens of grace.

But scars are not proof that someone is junk. Sometimes scars are proof a person survived.

Life leaves marks on people. Grief changes people. Loss changes people. Trauma changes people. But surviving difficult seasons does not diminish someone’s worth. In many ways, it reveals their strength. Some of the strongest people you will ever meet are the ones who quietly kept going after life tried to break them.

I also think we need to stop measuring value based solely on productivity, appearance, status, or accomplishments. Your worth is not tied to a title, paycheck, degree, social media following, relationship status, or the approval of others. The world may place value on performance and outward success, but God looks at the heart.

There have been moments in my own life where I questioned myself, where grief felt overwhelming, where exhaustion took over, or where I wondered what direction life was taking me next. Most people have those moments, even if they never say it out loud. The reality is that being human can sometimes feel heavy. But one thing I continue to learn through every storm in life is that God never abandons imperfect people.

He walks beside us in the middle of the mess, not just after everything is fixed.

That is the beauty of grace. God sees every scar, every flaw, every insecurity, every fear, and every failure, and still says, “You matter.” He sees the parts of ourselves we try to hide from the world and still loves us completely.

So if you are reading this today while carrying pain, shame, self-doubt, loneliness, grief, insecurity, or the feeling that you are somehow not enough, I hope you remember this:

You are not junk because you struggle. You are not junk because you are healing. You are not junk because life knocked you down. You are not junk because someone failed to love you properly. You are not junk because you do not fit neatly into someone else’s expectations.

You are human. You are imperfect. You are growing. You are learning. You are loved.

And God does not make junk.

title: Hustle
excerpt: Hustle.  Hustle. Hustle.  Where do you find your inner peace?
content:
Hustle

We hear the word “hustle” everywhere these days.

Hustle harder.
Sleep less.
Grind nonstop.
Keep pushing.
No days off.

Somewhere along the way, hustle became a badge of honor. People wear exhaustion like a trophy. We live in a world where being busy somehow became proof of worth. If your calendar is packed, your phone is ringing nonstop, and your mind never shuts off, society tells you that you must be successful.

But I’ve learned something over the years.

There’s a difference between purpose and performance.
There’s a difference between ambition and burnout.
And there’s definitely a difference between building a life and simply surviving one.

I know hustle well.

I grew up in a small town in Missouri dreaming big dreams while trying to figure out where I fit in this world. I chased weather. I chased education. I chased opportunities. I chased goals that sometimes felt impossible. Over time, those pursuits turned into degrees, careers, long nights, leadership roles, television appearances, storms, public service, writing, and moments I never imagined would become part of my story.

But hustle also comes with a cost.

Sometimes hustle means missing moments because you are too focused on the next destination. Sometimes it means carrying stress quietly while telling everyone around you that you are “fine.” Sometimes hustle looks productive on the outside while internally you are running on fumes and Pibb.

Truthfully, some of us hustle because we are afraid to sit still.

When life gets quiet, the thoughts get louder.

The grief.
The fear.
The loneliness.
The questions.
The insecurities we try to outrun.

For some people, hustle becomes a distraction from healing.

I think back to my years in higher education, public safety communications, weather coverage, and writing. There were seasons of my life where I was operating at full speed constantly. Work. School. Projects. Papers. Forecasts. Meetings. Responsibilities. Repeat. The calendar stayed full because slowing down felt uncomfortable.

But God has a funny way of eventually forcing us to pause.

Sometimes it happens through exhaustion.
Sometimes through heartbreak.
Sometimes through loss.
Sometimes through illness.
And sometimes through a quiet moment sitting in church where you finally hear the voice you have been drowning out with noise.

One thing I have learned is this: hustle without purpose eventually empties you.

And if we are not careful, hustle can even pull us away from God.

We become so focused on deadlines, goals, promotions, bills, responsibilities, social media, and the next achievement that we stop making time for the One who gave us life in the first place. We say we are “too busy” to pray. Too busy for Mass. Too busy to sit in silence. Too busy to reflect. Too busy to breathe.

Yet somehow we always find time to scroll endlessly through our phones or stress about things we cannot control.

I think one of the greatest tricks of modern life is convincing people that constant noise equals importance.

God often speaks in the quiet.

Not in chaos.
Not in nonstop movement.
Not in the endless hustle.

Some of the most meaningful moments of my life have not happened during the busiest seasons. They happened sitting quietly before Mass. Driving alone during a thunderstorm. Looking at the stars in the middle of nowhere. Sitting with my dogs after a long day. Reflecting on life after losing people I loved. Those quiet moments reminded me that life is bigger than productivity.

We were not created just to produce.
We were created to love, serve, connect, heal, and grow spiritually.

The older I get, the more I realize success means very little if your soul is exhausted.

You can have accomplishments and still feel empty.
You can have titles and still feel lost.
You can appear successful outwardly while spiritually running on empty inside.

That is why time with God matters.

Not because God needs our attention, but because we need Him.

We need grounding.
We need peace.
We need grace.
We need reminders that our worth is not tied to performance.

The world says hustle until you collapse.

God says come to Me and rest.

That does not mean we stop working hard. Hard work is honorable. Dedication matters. Responsibility matters. This world still needs people willing to serve others, show up, sacrifice, and care deeply about what they do.

But there has to be balance.

There has to be moments where the phone gets silenced.
Where the laptop closes.
Where the schedule pauses.
Where the noise stops.

There has to be time for prayer.
Time for reflection.
Time for family.
Time for healing.
Time for laughter.
Time for God.

Because one day, none of us will wish we answered one more email or attended one more pointless meeting.

We will remember the people we loved.
The conversations we had.
The sunsets we watched.
The prayers we whispered.
The moments we sat beside someone hurting.
The memories we made.

I still believe in dreams.
I still believe in hard work.
I still believe in giving your best effort.

But I also believe in sitting on the porch during a thunderstorm.
Laughing until your stomach hurts.
Calling your mom.
Watching your dogs run through the house like tiny fluffy maniacs.
Taking the scenic route home.
Sitting quietly before Mass.
And thanking God for another day you were blessed to wake up.

Because the hustle was never supposed to replace the living.

Life is not just about accomplishments and titles. It is about people. Memories. Purpose. Faith. Love. Healing. Connection. The moments in between the chaos.

So yes, chase the dream. Build something meaningful. Work hard.

But do not lose yourself while trying to build a life.

And do not become so busy chasing the world that you forget to spend time with God.

title: EVERYONE HAS A STORY!
excerpt: Don't be shy! Your story Matters!
content:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE
Dr. Nick Palisch Launches “Everyone Has a Story” Initiative Focused on Sharing the Voices of Everyday People

ST. LOUIS, MO — Author, educator, meteorologist, and motivational storyteller Dr. Nick Palisch has officially launched Everyone Has a Story, a new grassroots initiative dedicated to sharing the journeys, struggles, victories, and life experiences of ordinary people from all walks of life.

The project was created with one mission in mind: to remind people that every life matters and every person carries a story worth hearing.

In a world often divided by noise, negativity, and disconnection, Everyone Has a Story aims to bring people together through authentic human experiences that inspire hope, encourage healing, celebrate perseverance, and remind others they are not alone.

“My goal is to tell as many stories as I can,” said Dr. Nick Palisch, founder of Everyone Has a Story. “Stories that inspire, provide direction, give hope, bring peace, and create connectivity in a broken world. We all walk different paths in life, and sometimes hearing another person’s journey can help someone else find strength to keep going.”

The initiative will feature stories from everyday individuals whose lives may never make national headlines but whose experiences carry powerful meaning and impact. Stories may include overcoming loss, battling illness, caring for family members, surviving addiction, navigating grief, finding faith, raising children, serving others, discovering purpose, or simply enduring life’s hardest seasons with courage and resilience.

“Everyone has faced something,” Dr. Palisch said. “Some people have climbed mountains nobody else ever saw. Some are still climbing them right now. I believe there is power in listening to one another.”

The project is designed to serve as both a platform and a community where people can openly share their life experiences in hopes of encouraging others who may be struggling, questioning life, discerning their purpose, or simply needing reassurance that they are not alone.

“We are all unique,” Dr. Palisch added. “That uniqueness is something to celebrate, not hide. Every person carries wisdom, pain, lessons, memories, and moments that shaped who they became. Sometimes the most ordinary stories become the most extraordinary sources of inspiration.”

Through written features, interviews, blogs, videos, and future publications, Everyone Has a Story seeks to preserve real human experiences while building bridges between people of different backgrounds, generations, beliefs, and walks of life.

Dr. Palisch says the initiative is not about fame or recognition, but rather about humanity, compassion, understanding, and connection.

“At the end of the day, people want to feel heard,” he said. “They want to know their life mattered. If one person reads a story and finds hope, healing, peace, or direction because of it, then this project is already making a difference.”

Community members interested in sharing their story, nominating someone whose journey deserves to be heard, or learning more about the initiative are encouraged to visit the official website and submit their interest online.

To Share Your Story or Nominate Someone:

www.inspirewithdrnick.com

Media Contact:

Dr. Nick Palisch
Everyone Has a Story
Email: nickpalischphd@gmail.com

title: When Someone You Love Takes Their Own Life
excerpt: Mental wellness and kindness are important. Life is precious.  Death is real.
content:
When Someone You Love Takes Their Own Life

There are some kinds of pain that never fully leave you. They change shape over time. They soften around the edges. They stop screaming and start whispering. But they never completely disappear.

The loss of someone to suicide is one of those pains.

It is not just grief. It is confusion. It is anger. It is guilt. It is heartbreak mixed with questions that often never receive answers. It is replaying conversations in your head at two o’clock in the morning wondering if you missed something. Wondering if there was a sentence you should have said differently. Wondering if one more hug, one more phone call, one more “I love you” could have changed the outcome.

And for many people left behind, there is also silence.

People often do not know what to say after a suicide loss. Some become uncomfortable. Some disappear. Some offer quick phrases they think are helpful, but only deepen the hurt. “They’re in a better place.” “Everything happens for a reason.” “God needed another angel.”

The truth is, when your heart is shattered, those words can feel hollow.

What people really need in those moments is presence. A hand to hold. Someone willing to sit in the uncomfortable silence. Someone willing to let tears fall without trying to fix them.

Because grief after suicide is different. It carries layers.

There is the pain of losing the person you loved. Then there is the pain of trying to understand why they felt so hopeless, so tired, so broken that they believed the world would somehow be better without them in it.

That is the tragedy of suicide. The person often cannot see their own worth anymore. Depression, trauma, anxiety, addiction, hopelessness, and emotional exhaustion can distort reality so deeply that even love around them becomes difficult to recognize. The darkness becomes louder than the truth.

The reality is that we truly never know what someone may be carrying internally.

We do not know the battles happening quietly inside their mind. We do not know the trauma they may replay every night when the world becomes quiet. We do not know the fear, shame, loneliness, exhaustion, pressure, heartbreak, or emotional pain they may be trying to survive while still functioning every day.

Some people become experts at hiding pain.

Outwardly, they appear fine. They smile. They laugh. They show up to work. They attend church. They post pictures online. They encourage other people. They engage in conversations. They continue helping others while silently drowning themselves.

And because they still function outwardly, people assume they are okay.

But appearances can be deceiving.

Some of the people struggling the most are the very people everyone describes as “the strong one.” The dependable one. The funny one. The helper. The person who always checks on everyone else.

Meanwhile, nobody realizes they are falling apart internally.

That is why kindness matters so much.

A simple conversation matters. Checking on someone matters. Slowing down enough to genuinely ask, “How are you really doing?” matters. Sometimes people are not looking for someone to solve their problems. They are simply desperate for someone to notice they are hurting.

Mental and emotional pain does not always announce itself loudly.

Sometimes it hides behind humor.

Sometimes it hides behind success.

Sometimes it hides behind exhaustion and silence.

And sometimes it hides behind the sentence, “I’m fine.”

For the families and loved ones left behind, this reality can become incredibly painful. They replay every interaction wondering how they did not see it. But the truth is many people fighting these battles intentionally hide them because they do not want to burden others, appear weak, or admit how much they are struggling.

That is why guilt can become such a dangerous companion after suicide loss.

People blame themselves for things they could not fully see or understand at the time.

The truth is this: loving someone does not always mean you can save them from battles happening deep within their mind and soul.

And in the middle of all of this pain, many people quietly wrestle with another question:

“Does God still love them?”

I believe with my whole heart that the answer is yes.

God’s love is bigger than our worst moments. Bigger than mental illness. Bigger than despair. Bigger than human understanding.

A person experiencing overwhelming emotional suffering is not abandoned by God.

God sees the pain nobody else sees. He sees the battles hidden behind smiles. He sees the exhaustion hidden behind productivity. He sees the tears cried in silence. He sees the moments when someone feels completely trapped and cannot find a way out.

And even in those moments, God’s love does not disappear.

There is a dangerous misconception sometimes placed on suicide that reduces a deeply complex emotional, spiritual, and mental health struggle into a single moment of judgment. But only God fully knows the human heart. Only God fully understands the suffering someone endured internally.

God is merciful.

God is compassionate.

God is loving.

And God does not abandon people in their brokenness.

Sometimes people lose their battle with mental illness the same way others lose battles with physical illness. That does not erase their humanity. That does not erase their worth. And it certainly does not erase the love God has for them.

Faith can still become complicated after a loss like this.

Some people cling tighter to God. Others become angry with Him. Some stop praying altogether because they feel abandoned. Some sit in church physically present while emotionally screaming inside. Some wonder why God did not stop it. Why He did not intervene. Why miracles sometimes seem silent.

Those are real questions.

And I think sometimes we are afraid to admit them because we think faith means never struggling. But faith is not pretending everything is okay. Faith is continuing to search for light even when life feels unbearably dark.

Even Jesus wept.
