There is something deeply human about grief, and there is something deeply spiritual about surviving it.

Over time, many people who experience this kind of loss begin learning difficult lessons they never wanted to learn in the first place.

They learn that people can smile publicly while privately falling apart.

They learn that checking on people matters.

They learn that mental health struggles are real illnesses, not weakness.

They learn that kindness matters more than we realize because we often have no idea what battle another person is fighting behind closed doors.

They learn that life is fragile.

And sometimes, they learn that healing is not about “moving on.” It is about carrying the memory differently.

You never stop loving the person you lost. You never stop wishing they were still here. Holidays still hurt. Birthdays still sting. Certain songs still punch you in the chest without warning.

But eventually, the love begins to outweigh only the pain.

You start remembering the laughter again. Their voice. Their habits. Their stories. The little things that made them uniquely them. You realize grief is proof that love existed deeply and fully.

And somewhere in the middle of surviving the unimaginable, many people begin finding purpose in helping others survive too.

Sometimes the greatest ministry a hurting person can offer is simply saying, “I understand.”

If you are someone carrying this kind of loss today, please hear this clearly: your grief is valid. Your questions are valid. Your tears are valid. Your anger, exhaustion, confusion, and heartbreak are valid.

Healing is not linear.

Some days you will feel okay. Some days you will feel like you are right back at the beginning. That does not mean you are failing. It means you are human.

And if your faith feels shaken, fragile, or exhausted, God is still there in the middle of the mess. Sometimes faith is not standing tall on the mountain. Sometimes faith is barely whispering, “Help me make it through today.”

That still counts.

The pain of suicide leaves scars on the hearts of those left behind. But scars also remind us that we survived wounds we thought might destroy us.

Keep talking about mental health.

Keep checking on people.

Keep saying “I love you.”

Keep choosing compassion over judgment.

And if you are the one struggling silently right now, please do not carry it alone. There are people who want you here. There are people who would rather listen to your pain than attend your funeral.

Your story still matters.

Even here.

Even now.

title: Words.
excerpt: Words matter.  Words have power.  Words can change people.
content:
# Dr. Nick’s Matter of Perspective

## Words

Words are strange things when you really stop and think about them.

They weigh nothing, yet they can carry incredible power. A single sentence can heal a broken heart or completely shatter someone’s confidence. Words can start wars, mend relationships, inspire movements, strengthen faith, destroy trust, comfort the grieving, or leave wounds that people carry for decades.

And the truth is, most of us remember words far longer than we remember events.

Many of us can still recall hurtful things said to us years ago. A comment from childhood. An insult in school. A criticism from a parent, teacher, coach, partner, or stranger. Sometimes those words replay in our minds long after the person who spoke them forgot they even said them.

That is the thing about words. Once released, they cannot truly be taken back.

You can apologize for words. You can regret them. You can try to explain them. But spoken words leave fingerprints on people’s hearts. Some become scars. Others become reminders of love, encouragement, and hope.

I think about this often in today’s world because we live in a time where people speak quickly and think later. Social media especially has created a culture where many people type things they would never say face-to-face. Cruelty has become entertainment for some. Sarcasm has replaced compassion. People tear each other apart over politics, religion, lifestyles, opinions, appearances, and mistakes.

Somewhere along the way, many forgot there is an actual human being on the receiving end of those words.

A person with feelings.
A person with struggles.
A person already fighting battles nobody else sees.

Words can either add weight to someone’s pain or help carry it for a little while.

I have always believed some of the strongest people are not those who can argue the loudest or insult the quickest. Strength is often found in people who know how to speak with wisdom, patience, gentleness, and grace even when emotions are high.

That does not mean avoiding truth. Truth matters. Accountability matters. Honest conversations matter. But there is a difference between speaking truth and weaponizing words to wound people intentionally.

Too often people say, “Well, I’m just being honest,” when what they really mean is, “I have decided kindness no longer matters.”

You can be truthful without being cruel.

I think many of us underestimate how deeply encouraging words can impact someone. A compliment may arrive at the exact moment a person is doubting themselves. A simple “I’m proud of you” may heal a wound someone has carried for years. A conversation may stop someone from giving up entirely.

There are teachers who changed lives simply because they believed in students who felt invisible.
There are parents whose words built confidence that lasted generations.
There are friends whose encouragement helped people survive dark seasons.
There are pastors, counselors, coworkers, dispatchers, nurses, and strangers whose calm words carried people through moments of fear and grief.

Words matter because people matter.

I also think we need to talk about the words we say to ourselves. Sometimes we become our own harshest critics. We replay failures. We focus on flaws. We convince ourselves we are not good enough, smart enough, attractive enough, successful enough, or worthy enough.

Those words become dangerous too.

Eventually the voice in your head can become louder than the voices around you.

That is why we must learn to speak life into ourselves as well. Not arrogance. Not ego. But reminders that we are human beings created with value and purpose. God did not create any of us to spend our lives drowning in self-hatred and shame.

The Bible speaks often about the power of the tongue. Proverbs 18:21 says, “The tongue has the power of life and death.” That verse is powerful because it is true. Words can breathe life into people or slowly destroy them.

Think about how often Jesus used words to restore people. He spoke to those society ignored. He comforted the broken. He defended the rejected. He challenged people without humiliating them. He spoke with truth, but also with love.

That balance matters.

As I have gotten older, I have realized people rarely remember every detail of conversations. What they remember is how those conversations made them feel. They remember who encouraged them. They remember who humiliated them. They remember who spoke peace into chaos and who added more noise to already difficult moments.

In many ways, our words become part of our legacy.

Long after we are gone, people will remember the way we spoke to them. They will remember whether we brought comfort or criticism, hope or negativity, calm or chaos.

That should make all of us pause a little longer before we speak.

Before sending the angry text.
Before making the sarcastic comment.
Before posting something cruel online.
Before speaking out of frustration.

Sometimes silence is wisdom.
Sometimes gentleness is strength.
Sometimes compassion changes more than anger ever could.

And sometimes the right words, spoken at the right moment, can completely change someone’s life.

So choose your words carefully.

Use them to encourage.
Use them to teach.
Use them to comfort.
Use them to inspire.
Use them to defend people instead of tearing them down.

Because this world already has enough noise, anger, and cruelty.

Let your words become something better.

title: Being the Sunshine
excerpt: There is a lot of darkness in our world.  Be the sunshine.
content:
Being the Sunshine

We spend so much time talking about darkness in this world that sometimes we forget how powerful light can truly be. Every day we are surrounded by negativity, division, anger, criticism, stress, and exhaustion. It shows up on the news, across social media, at work, in families, and even within ourselves. People are tired. Emotionally tired. Spiritually tired. Mentally tired. Many are carrying burdens nobody else can see.

That is why being the sunshine matters more than ever.

Not the fake kind of sunshine where people pretend life is perfect. Not forced smiles or empty positivity. Real sunshine is deeper than that. Real sunshine is the person who chooses kindness when life has given them reasons to become bitter. It is the person who still encourages others while quietly fighting battles of their own. It is the person who walks into a room and somehow makes people feel calmer, safer, and less alone.

The truth is, you never fully know what someone else is carrying.

The person standing next to you at the grocery store may have just lost someone they love. The coworker laughing during lunch may be struggling with anxiety or depression. The parent trying to keep their children under control may be emotionally exhausted. The elderly person sitting alone at church may simply miss conversation and companionship. Even the strongest people often carry silent pain.

That is why small acts of kindness matter so much.

A smile matters.
A conversation matters.
Patience matters.
Checking on someone matters.
Simply listening matters.

Sometimes people are not looking for someone to solve their problems. Sometimes they just need to feel seen.

I think we underestimate how much our presence affects other people. There are individuals who walk into a room and immediately drain the energy from it. Negativity follows them everywhere. Criticism pours out of them constantly. Nothing is ever good enough. Everything becomes an argument, a complaint, or a source of anger.

Then there are people who do the opposite. They bring peace. They bring warmth. They make others feel valued. Even during stressful moments, they somehow help people breathe a little easier. Those are the people others remember. Those are the people who quietly change lives every single day without even realizing it.

I have learned over the years that being sunshine does not mean you never struggle. In fact, some of the brightest people I have ever known were individuals who had endured incredible hardship. Some had experienced devastating loss. Some battled illness. Some faced rejection, loneliness, or heartbreak. Yet despite everything life had thrown at them, they still chose compassion. They still chose gentleness. They still chose to care about others.

That takes strength.

Anybody can become angry. Anybody can let pain harden them. Anybody can spread bitterness after being hurt. But it takes real courage to remain kind in a world that often rewards cruelty and outrage.

I think about this often when I sit quietly before Mass begins. You look around the church and realize every person there has a story. Some are carrying grief. Some are worried about their health. Some are praying for their children. Some are lonely. Some are overwhelmed. Some are simply trying to hold themselves together another day while searching for peace.

And sometimes all it takes to brighten someone’s day is acknowledgment. A handshake. A smile. A simple “good morning.” A moment where someone feels noticed instead of invisible.

Sunshine does not always arrive in dramatic ways. Often it appears through ordinary people doing ordinary things with extraordinary kindness.

The world tells us that influence belongs only to famous people, wealthy people, or people with large platforms. I do not believe that at all. Some of the most impactful individuals in life will never appear on television or trend online. They are teachers who encourage struggling students. Nurses who comfort scared patients. Dispatchers who remain calm during emergencies. Parents working endlessly for their families. Friends who answer late-night phone calls. Grandparents who offer wisdom and unconditional love.

Those people are sunshine.

And the beautiful thing about sunshine is that it spreads. Kindness creates more kindness. Compassion inspires compassion. Encouragement gives people hope. One positive interaction can completely change the direction of somebody’s day, and sometimes even their life.

We also have to remember that even sunshine needs rest. There are seasons where life feels heavy. Seasons where stress, grief, fear, or disappointment make it difficult to keep your own light shining. That does not make you weak. It makes you human. Even the strongest hearts need time to heal, recharge, pray, and breathe.

But even during difficult seasons, never underestimate your value.

Your words matter.
Your presence matters.
Your compassion matters.
Your ability to love people matters.

In a world filled with enough storms already, choose to be the person who brings warmth instead of more darkness. Choose to encourage instead of tear down. Choose to forgive instead of hold onto bitterness. Choose to bring calm into chaos.

Because somewhere today, someone is struggling silently. Someone feels forgotten. Someone feels exhausted. Someone is wondering if anybody notices them at all.

And your kindness may be the very thing that reminds them they are not alone.

Be the sunshine.

title: Why I am looking for everday people?
excerpt: I believe everyone has a story. It needs to be told.  I am looking to tell your story to share with others.
content:
Why I Want to Tell Your Story

I think one of the greatest tragedies in life is not that people suffer. It’s that so many people suffer quietly while believing nobody would care enough to listen.

Every single day, people wake up carrying burdens they never talk about. They put on their uniforms, clock into work, smile at customers, answer phones, sit in classrooms, stand in church pews, cheer for their kids, help strangers, and laugh at jokes. Meanwhile, many of them are barely holding themselves together inside. The world keeps moving around them while they grieve someone they lost, battle depression, carry trauma, wrestle with fear, question their worth, or wonder if anybody truly sees them at all.

Most people have become experts at hiding pain.

Maybe that is why this mission has become so important to me. I am tired of living in a world where people think their only value comes from titles, popularity, money, followers, or accomplishments. Somewhere along the way, we stopped honoring ordinary human beings. But after years of working in education, public service, weather communication, and simply talking with people through everyday life, I have learned something important: ordinary people are the ones holding this world together.

Not celebrities. Not influencers. Not politicians.

Everyday people.

The exhausted nurse finishing another twelve-hour shift. The father trying to provide despite financial stress. The widow learning how to exist after losing the love of her life. The dispatcher hearing panic and fear over the phone while remaining calm for someone else. The teacher staying late because one student needed someone to believe in them. The recovering addict fighting every day not to relapse. The caregiver sacrificing years of their own life to care for someone they love.

Those are the people I think about. Those are the stories that deserve to be heard.

Over the last several months, this idea has weighed heavily on my heart in a way I cannot fully explain. It started quietly through prayer, reflection, and moments of silence before Mass. I found myself looking around the church asking the same question over and over:

“What have these people survived?”

I would notice smiles that did not fully reach people’s eyes. I would see exhaustion hidden behind politeness. I would notice the older man sitting alone, the overwhelmed parent trying to hold everything together, the teenager avoiding eye contact, or the person staring quietly into space like they were carrying years of pain nobody else could see.

The more I noticed people, the more I realized something powerful:

Everybody is carrying something.

Some people are carrying grief. Some are carrying shame, regret, fear, loneliness, or memories they wish they could erase. Some are carrying emotional wounds nobody knows about. And despite all of it, most people continue showing up every single day anyway.

That takes real strength.

Not the fake kind social media celebrates. Not carefully edited perfection pretending life is beautiful all the time. I mean real human strength. The kind that gets out of bed despite emotional exhaustion. The kind that keeps loving after heartbreak, keeps believing after disappointment, and keeps surviving even when life feels unbearably heavy.

That strength deserves recognition.

I honestly think we are living in one of the loneliest times in modern history. We are constantly connected through screens and technology, yet emotionally disconnected from each other. We know what someone had for dinner, but we do not know if they cried themselves to sleep the night before. We scroll past pain. We skim past humanity. We react more than we listen.

Somewhere along the way, people began believing their struggles were either too small to matter or too messy to share.

But stories matter because stories remind people they are not alone.

Stories connect people in ways arguments and opinions never will. A story can reach places facts and statistics cannot. A story can soften hearts. A story can save lives. A story can remind somebody that survival is not weakness. Sometimes hearing another person’s story gives language to emotions someone has buried for years.

I cannot tell you how many times someone has quietly messaged me after reading something I wrote and simply said:

“I thought I was the only one who felt that way.”

That matters more than followers, numbers, clicks, or popularity ever will.

Human connection matters.

That is the heartbeat behind “Everyone Has a Story.”

This is not about interviewing perfect people with perfect lives. Honestly, perfection is one of the most isolating lies we ever created. Perfection keeps people hidden. It keeps people silent. It keeps people pretending.

I am interested in authenticity.

I want the stories of people who fought through divorce and rebuilt their lives. The stories of people who lost someone they loved but somehow found the strength to keep going. Stories about addiction recovery, redemption, faith, forgiveness, resilience, heartbreak, survival, healing, and second chances. I want the stories of people who once believed they were broken beyond repair but eventually discovered they were still worthy of love and grace.

Because I think people need hope again.

Real hope.

Not fake positivity. Not motivational clichés. Not “good vibes only.”

Real hope acknowledges pain while still believing healing is possible.

Faith plays a huge role in why I feel so strongly connected to this mission. I truly believe God places people in our lives intentionally. I believe every person carries value whether the world recognizes it or not. I believe some people survive storms not only for themselves, but so they can eventually help someone else survive theirs too.

Sometimes your story becomes someone else’s lifeline.

The pain you survived may someday help another person choose not to give up. The grief you endured may comfort someone else standing at a funeral feeling shattered. The anxiety you battled may help another person finally feel understood. The mistakes you made and learned from may remind another human being they are not beyond redemption.

That is powerful.

Maybe that is part of why I feel so emotionally connected to this project. I know what it feels like to keep moving while carrying invisible weight. I know what it feels like to question purpose, to pour yourself into helping others while privately trying to hold yourself together too.

Most people do.

That is why empathy matters now more than ever. We have become so quick to judge one another without understanding what people have lived through. We see reactions without understanding the wounds behind them. We see behavior without understanding the battles underneath it.

But if we slowed down long enough to hear each other’s stories, I think we would become softer toward one another. More patient. More compassionate. More human.

I want people reading these stories to recognize themselves in them. I want them to think:

“That sounds like me. I’ve felt that too. I’m not weak. I’m not crazy. I’m not alone.”

Because survival itself is often a miracle people never give themselves enough credit for.

Some people survived childhood trauma. Some survived abuse. Some survived addiction. Some survived devastating loss. Some survived years of believing they were not enough. Some survived suicidal thoughts nobody else even knew existed.

And they are still here.

That deserves to be honored.

I also believe there is something sacred about being trusted with another person’s story. When someone opens up about their life, pain, healing, faith, failures, or growth, they are handing you pieces of their soul. That should never be treated lightly.

Stories are not content to me.

They are people.

This mission is not about exploiting pain for attention. It is about creating connection in a disconnected world. It is about reminding people they matter before life convinces them otherwise.

I want people to know they do not need to become world-famous for their life to have meaning. Maybe you raised good kids. Maybe you cared for a sick parent. Maybe you survived addiction. Maybe you kept your family together during impossible circumstances. Maybe you quietly fought depression every single day and nobody even knows it.

That matters.

Your life matters.

And maybe the greatest lie people believe is this:

“My story is nothing special.”

But maybe your story is exactly what another person needs to hear to survive their own chapter. Maybe the storms you survived were not only about you. Maybe your scars became proof that healing is possible. Maybe your life carries more impact than you will ever fully understand this side of Heaven.

That is why I want to tell these stories.

Because behind every face is a battle. Behind every smile is history. Behind every human being is a story worth hearing.

And in a world growing colder, louder, and more divided by the day, maybe one of the most important things we can still do is remind people:

“You matter.”

Your story matters. Your life matters. Your voice matters.

And if you are willing to share your story, I would be honored to listen.

I want to talk to you! Reach out.

title: Stopping the Hate and Letting Go of the Anger
excerpt: We all carry anger and we will often say, I hate this and that, but the word hate is a powerful word. How to let go and live a happier life.
content:
Stopping the Hate and Letting Go of the Anger

We live in a world where anger has become almost second nature. It is everywhere around us. Turn on the television, scroll through social media, sit in traffic for ten minutes, or read the comments section under almost any post online, and you will quickly realize just how much frustration people are carrying these days. Somewhere along the way, outrage became normal. Hate became casual conversation. Anger became a personality trait instead of an emotion we were supposed to process and release.

What is alarming is how often we use the word “hate” without even thinking about it.

“I hate this weather.”
“I hate my job.”
“I hate people.”
“I hate them.”
“I hate what they believe.”
“I hate Mondays.”

The word slips out so naturally now that we rarely stop to consider how heavy that word truly is. Hate is not a small thing. It is not harmless. The more we feed it, the more it changes us from the inside out. Hate has a way of settling into the heart quietly. At first, it feels justified. Maybe someone hurt us deeply. Maybe we were betrayed, ignored, lied to, abandoned, or treated unfairly. Maybe life itself has not been kind to us lately. The anger begins as pain, but if we do not deal with it, pain slowly hardens into bitterness.

And bitterness is exhausting.

There are people walking around every day smiling on the outside while carrying years of anger inside them. Some are angry at family members. Some are angry at former friends. Some are angry at politics, religion, society, coworkers, or even themselves. Some are angry at God because life did not unfold the way they prayed it would. You can often see the heaviness in the way people talk, the way they react, or the way they immediately assume the worst in others.

The truth is, unresolved anger changes how we see the world. It changes our patience. It changes our relationships. It changes our ability to trust. When anger sits inside us too long, it starts coloring everything around us. Suddenly, every inconvenience feels personal. Every disagreement becomes a battle. Every frustration feels larger than it should.

And sometimes the deepest anger comes from loss.

There are people carrying anger because someone they loved died too soon. Anger because cancer took someone they needed. Anger because an accident changed everything in a single moment. Anger because they prayed for healing that never came. Anger because they feel abandoned by life itself.

Grief and anger often walk hand in hand.

People do not talk about that enough.

When we lose someone, we are not only grieving the person. We are grieving the future we imagined with them. The conversations we will never have. The holidays that now feel different. The phone calls that no longer come. The empty chair at the table. Sometimes anger becomes easier than sadness because anger feels powerful while grief feels vulnerable.

Some people become angry at doctors.
Some become angry at family.
Some become angry at themselves.
Some become angry at God.

They replay the “what ifs” over and over in their minds.
“What if I had called sooner?”
“What if I had noticed something?”
“What if I had done more?”
“What if things had gone differently?”

The human heart has a difficult time accepting loss it cannot control.

Then there is the anger that comes from being hurt by other people. Betrayal leaves deep scars. Sometimes the people who damage us most are the ones we trusted the most. A spouse who walked away. A friend who betrayed confidence. A parent who failed to protect. A coworker who spread lies. A relationship that slowly broke pieces of your confidence over time.

Wounds from people often linger because we expect human beings to love us correctly. When they do not, the disappointment cuts deep.

And if we are honest, some of us are still carrying anger from events that happened decades ago.

Moments from childhood.
Words spoken in anger.
Things we were told we would never become.
Humiliation.
Bullying.
Abandonment.
Failure.
Rejection.

Time does not automatically heal everything. Some wounds stay buried quietly beneath the surface until something triggers them years later. A smell. A memory. A conversation. A song. Suddenly emotions we thought we buried come rushing back like they never left.

That is why healing is not about pretending pain never happened.

Healing is about refusing to let pain control the rest of your life.

That does not mean forgetting. Some losses will always ache. Some betrayals permanently change us. Certain chapters of life leave scars that never fully disappear. But scars are proof that healing happened at all. Open wounds bleed forever. Scars remind us we survived.

One of the hardest truths to accept is that closure does not always come. Sometimes the apology never happens. Sometimes the relationship is never repaired. Sometimes the answers never arrive. Sometimes life remains unfair despite our prayers.

And yet somehow, we still have to find a way to move forward.

Not because the hurt was small.
Not because the pain did not matter.
But because staying trapped in anger eventually destroys our own peace more than anything else.

The human body was never designed to stay angry all the time. Stress, resentment, bitterness, and rage eventually wear people down emotionally, mentally, spiritually, and physically. There is a reason angry people often seem tired. Hate drains the life out of a person slowly over time. It steals peace. It steals joy. It steals the ability to appreciate good things happening around us because anger keeps pulling our attention back toward the negative.

One of the hardest lessons in life is realizing that the anger we hold onto often hurts us more than the people we are angry with. Many times, the person who wounded us has already moved on while we continue replaying the hurt over and over in our minds. We relive conversations. We replay betrayals. We imagine arguments we wish we had won. Meanwhile, life keeps moving forward while we stay emotionally chained to moments that already happened.

That is why letting go matters so much.

Letting go does not mean pretending the pain never existed. It does not mean excusing terrible behavior or allowing people to continually hurt you. Forgiveness is not weakness, and it certainly is not permission for abuse. Sometimes boundaries are necessary. Sometimes distance is healthy. Sometimes walking away is the wisest thing a person can do.

But there is a difference between protecting your peace and feeding your hatred.

Too often, people believe that staying angry somehow gives them control. In reality, anger often controls them. It dictates moods, reactions, relationships, and perspectives. It becomes a prison people build around themselves brick by brick until they no longer remember what peace even feels like.

Faith teaches us something radically different from what the world teaches. The world says to get even. The world says to destroy people who hurt you. The world says to hold grudges forever and wear them proudly. But Christ consistently taught something harder and far more transformative: forgiveness, mercy, grace, and love.

And let’s be honest, that is not easy.

Forgiveness sounds beautiful until someone truly wounds your heart. It is easy to preach kindness when life is comfortable. It becomes much harder when trust has been broken, when betrayal cuts deep, or when disappointment leaves scars that linger for years. Yet carrying that anger forever eventually becomes its own kind of suffering.

Sometimes the healing begins when we finally admit we are tired of being angry.

Tired of carrying resentment.
Tired of replaying old wounds.
Tired of allowing bitterness to shape our personality.
Tired of giving painful moments permanent residence in our hearts.

There is freedom in finally saying, “I do not want to live this way anymore.”

Life is far too short to spend most of it angry. One day, all of us will realize that many of the things we fought about, obsessed over, or hated were not nearly as important as we thought they were in the moment. At the end of life, people rarely wish they had spent more time arguing online or holding grudges longer. Most people simply wish they had loved deeper, forgiven sooner, and appreciated life more while they had the chance.

Peace does not happen overnight. Letting go of anger is often a process, not a single moment. But every time we choose compassion over cruelty, grace over rage, and understanding over hatred, we move closer toward becoming the kind of people God intended us to be.

Maybe that is what the world needs more of right now.

Not louder hatred.
Not stronger division.
Not more outrage.

Maybe the world simply needs more people willing to heal instead of harm.

And maybe that healing starts inside our own hearts first.

title: Sitting in Silence before Mass
excerpt: Sitting in Silence before Mass
content:
Sitting in Silence Before Mass

There is something sacred about arriving early to Mass and simply sitting in silence.

Not scrolling.
Not talking.
Not rushing in at the last second carrying the weight of the world on your shoulders like an overloaded grocery bag threatening to explode in the parking lot.

Just silence.

The older I get, the more I realize that some of the most important conversations with God happen before a single word is spoken during Mass. They happen in those quiet moments while the church is still. Before the music starts. Before the readings begin. Before the distractions of life start pulling at our attention again.

There is a peace inside a quiet church that is hard to explain unless you’ve experienced it.

The dim lights.
The soft sounds of people kneeling in prayer.
The flicker of candles.
The echo of footsteps against the floor.
The stillness.

It feels different from the outside world.

For a little while, the noise fades.

And goodness knows we live in a noisy world.

Phones buzz constantly. News alerts scream for our attention. Social media convinces us we should always be angry, busy, productive, worried, or comparing ourselves to somebody else. Life becomes schedules, deadlines, responsibilities, bills, stress, and exhaustion. Even our minds rarely stop talking.

But then comes that moment before Mass.

You sit down in the pew.

And suddenly… silence.

Not empty silence.
Holy silence.

The kind that settles your breathing.
The kind that slows your racing thoughts.
The kind that reminds you that God does not compete with chaos. He waits patiently for us to become still enough to hear Him.

Psalm 46:10 says, “Be still, and know that I am God.”

Not “be busy.”
Not “be louder.”
Not “be perfect.”

Be still.

Sometimes I think the best part of arriving early to Mass is that it gives us permission to pause. We spend so much of life trying to hold everything together that we forget we were never meant to carry everything alone.

That silence before Mass becomes a reset button for the soul.

It becomes the place where anxiety softens.
Where grief breathes.
Where gratitude grows.
Where prayers too deep for words finally rise to the surface.

Sometimes I sit there and think about life. About people I love. About those hurting quietly. About prayers I’ve been carrying for months. Sometimes I don’t even have words. I just sit there in God’s presence and let the silence speak for me.

And honestly? I think those may be some of the most honest prayers we ever offer.

No rehearsed speeches.
No polished words.
No pretending.

Just us and God.

There is also something beautiful about looking around the church before Mass begins and realizing every person sitting there walked in carrying something invisible.

Someone is grieving.
Someone is scared.
Someone is overwhelmed.
Someone is praying for healing.
Someone is thanking God for answered prayers.
Someone is barely holding themselves together.

And yet for that brief moment, everyone is united in the same silence.

There’s something deeply comforting about that.

In a world where everyone is constantly trying to be heard, silence before Mass reminds us that listening matters too.

Listening to God.
Listening to our hearts.
Listening to the quiet nudges we often ignore because life moves too fast.

I truly believe some of the best moments in church happen before Mass even begins.

Not because the Mass itself isn’t powerful, because it absolutely is. But because those quiet moments prepare our hearts to receive what we need.

The silence softens the ground before the seed is planted.

Maybe that’s why it feels so different.

Maybe that’s why some people sit in their pew long after Mass ends too. Because peace is hard to find these days, and when people finally feel it, they are not in a hurry to leave.

So if you ever get the chance, arrive a little early.

Sit down.
Put the phone away.
Take a breath.
Look around.
Be still.

You may discover that the quiet moments before Mass become the loudest reminder that God has been with you the entire time.

title: The Spirit of God.
excerpt: The Spirit of God is always working and around us.
content:
The Spirit of God

There are moments in life that are difficult to explain with logic alone.

Moments where peace somehow enters the room during complete chaos. Moments where strength appears even after we thought we had emotionally, mentally, and spiritually run out of fuel. Moments where something deep within us whispers, “Keep going,” even when every part of our human nature wants to give up.

I believe those moments are often the Spirit of God moving within us.

Not always loudly.
Not always dramatically.
Not always through some giant miracle that stops us in our tracks.

Sometimes the Spirit of God moves in quiet and deeply personal ways that only our heart fully understands.

I think people often expect God to show up in huge displays of power while overlooking the smaller moments where He is constantly present. We look for booming voices and flashing signs while missing the gentle nudges, convictions, and moments of peace that quietly guide our lives every single day.

Sometimes the Spirit of God is felt in silence.

In the stillness of an early morning before the world wakes up. In the tears that unexpectedly fall during prayer. In the overwhelming sense that you are not alone even when life feels isolating. In the calmness that settles into your chest after days or weeks of anxiety. In the moment you suddenly feel led to reach out to someone who was secretly struggling.

The Spirit of God has a way of meeting us exactly where we are emotionally, spiritually, and mentally.

Not where we pretend to be.
Not where social media says we should be.
Not where society expects us to be.

But where we truly are.

Broken. Confused. Angry. Hopeful. Exhausted. Searching. Healing. Waiting. Grieving. Doubting.

Human.

That is one of the most beautiful things about God. He does not wait for us to become perfect before He draws near to us. So many people convince themselves they have to “clean up their life” before they can approach God, but Scripture repeatedly shows us the opposite.

Throughout the Bible, God used flawed people.

People who doubted Him.
People who failed miserably.
People who ran away from their calling.
People who struggled with fear, insecurity, anger, grief, and shame.

Yet God still worked through them.

That gives me hope because it reminds me that God’s love is not reserved for people who have life perfectly together. If that were the requirement, none of us would qualify.

The Spirit of God often works within the messiness of life.

Sometimes it shows up as conviction when we know we are walking down the wrong path. Sometimes it pushes us to apologize when pride wants us to stay silent. Sometimes it softens our heart after pain has made us cold and guarded. Sometimes it calls us toward healing even when bitterness feels more comfortable.

And honestly, healing is hard.

It is hard to forgive people who wounded us. It is hard to let go of anger when we feel justified holding onto it. It is hard to trust again after betrayal. It is hard to remain kind in a world that often rewards cruelty, arrogance, and division.

But the Spirit of God constantly calls us toward something greater than our pain.

Toward compassion.
Toward grace.
Toward purpose.
Toward love.

Not weak love. Real love.

The kind of love that chooses patience when frustration would be easier. The kind of love that listens instead of immediately judging. The kind of love that sees people as human beings instead of political opinions, failures, labels, or mistakes.

I think one of the clearest signs of the Spirit of God is when our heart begins changing in ways that make us more compassionate, more understanding, and more aware of others.

Because the Spirit of God does not simply make people religious. It transforms how they love.

That transformation often happens slowly. Sometimes painfully slowly.

There are seasons where faith feels strong and alive, and there are seasons where people wrestle deeply with doubt, disappointment, and unanswered prayers. I think many Christians are afraid to admit that struggle because they fear it makes them look weak spiritually.

But struggle does not mean God abandoned you.

Some of the strongest faith is developed in seasons where people continue walking with God even while carrying questions they cannot answer.

Sometimes faith simply sounds like:

“God, I do not understand what You are doing right now, but I need You to stay with me.”

And He does.

Not always according to our timeline.
Not always in the ways we expected.
But He does.

I think the Spirit of God often works through people too. Through the friend who checks on you unexpectedly. Through the mentor who speaks encouragement into your life. Through the stranger who shows kindness at exactly the right moment. Through the person who reminds you that your life still has value during seasons where you have forgotten your own worth.

God frequently uses ordinary people to deliver extraordinary reminders.

And sometimes, the Spirit of God redirects us when we lose ourselves chasing things that ultimately leave us empty. The world constantly tells us to achieve more, earn more, prove more, post more, and compare ourselves endlessly to everyone else. We are taught to measure our worth through productivity, status, appearance, titles, followers, and success.

But the Spirit of God reminds us that our identity was never meant to be built on temporary things.

Because eventually careers change.
Titles fade.
Money comes and goes.
Recognition disappears.

But purpose remains.

Love remains.

Faith remains.

I have personally experienced moments where I could feel God stirring something inside of me. Moments during prayer, writing, conversations, Mass, or even complete silence where I realized life is about far more than accomplishments and survival. There are moments where the Spirit of God reminds us to slow down and pay attention to what truly matters.

People matter.
Kindness matters.
Grace matters.
Compassion matters.
Forgiveness matters.

The Spirit of God constantly points us back toward those truths.

And maybe someone reading this today feels spiritually exhausted. Maybe life has hurt you. Maybe disappointment has shaken your faith. Maybe you feel distant from God because of mistakes, guilt, grief, anger, or unanswered prayers.

But hear this clearly:

You are not abandoned.

You are not forgotten.

You are not too broken for God to still work within your life.

The Spirit of God still moves in wounded hearts. In tired souls. In struggling people. In ordinary lives. Sometimes through powerful moments and sometimes through quiet whispers that slowly remind us we are still loved.

And maybe that quiet whisper is enough to keep us moving forward one more day.

title: The Family We Find.
excerpt: Not all family are connected by blood.
content:
he Family We Find Along the Way

As I’ve gotten older, I’ve started to realize something that probably should have been obvious much earlier in life:

Some of the greatest family we will ever have are the people we were never actually related to.

No shared bloodline.
No shared last name.
No ancestry website connecting us together somewhere back in 1842 beside a horse and buggy.

Just connection.

Real connection.

Life has a funny way of bringing people into our path exactly when we need them most. Sometimes they arrive loudly and instantly become part of our lives. Other times they quietly show up and slowly become woven into our everyday routines until one day you realize, “I cannot imagine life without this person.”

And somehow, those relationships often become stronger than the ones biology assigned us.

That truth can be both beautiful and painful at the same time.

Because not everyone grows up feeling accepted, understood, or valued by the people they share blood with. Some people spend years trying to earn love from family members who only know how to criticize. Some walk on eggshells at family gatherings. Some feel invisible in rooms where they should have felt safest. Some become the “different one,” the outcast, the black sheep, the misunderstood one, or the one who simply never quite fit the mold everyone else expected.

And that kind of hurt stays with people.

It shapes how they trust.
How they love.
How they view themselves.

But then something remarkable happens.

Life begins placing people in front of us who see us differently.

Maybe it is a friend who notices your silence when everyone else overlooks it. Maybe it is a coworker who checks on you after a rough shift. Maybe it is someone at church who hugs you a little longer because they somehow know you needed it. Maybe it is a mentor who reminds you that you are capable of more than you believe. Maybe it is a neighbor who becomes family simply because they consistently show up.

Or maybe it is someone who walks into your life unexpectedly and stays.

Those people matter more than they probably realize.

I think one of the greatest human needs is not just to be loved, but to feel chosen. To feel wanted. To know someone voluntarily makes space for you in their life. There is something deeply healing about finding people who accept you without constantly trying to change you.

People who allow you to exhale.

People who make you feel safe enough to laugh loudly, cry honestly, vent openly, and exist fully without fear of rejection.

That kind of connection is rare.

And sacred.

The older I get, the more I believe God often sends people into our lives at exactly the right moments. Sometimes He knows our biological family cannot provide what our hearts or souls need, so He creates family through friendship, mentorship, compassion, and shared experiences instead.

Maybe that is why some friendships feel so immediate and so deep. It is almost as if your soul recognizes something before your mind catches up.

Some of the strongest families are built, not born.

Built through late-night conversations.
Built through surviving hard seasons together.
Built through inside jokes, shared tears, long drives, hospital visits, coffee talks, life advice, random check-ins, and simple moments of “I’m here if you need me.”

Built through consistency.

And honestly, consistency is one of the purest forms of love.

Anyone can show up when life is easy. But the people who sit beside you when everything falls apart? The people who answer the phone at 2 a.m.? The people who help carry you emotionally when you feel too exhausted to carry yourself? Those are the people who become family.

Not because of obligation.
But because of choice.

There is something powerful about being chosen.

I think about all the people throughout my own life who became family without ever officially holding the title. Teachers. Mentors. Friends. Coworkers. People who encouraged me when I felt defeated. People who believed in me when I doubted myself. People who sat beside me in difficult seasons without trying to fix everything. They simply stayed.

And sometimes staying is the greatest act of love a person can give.

In today’s world, relationships can feel temporary. People come and go quickly. Social media gives us thousands of connections but often very little depth. We “like” posts, react to stories, and scroll endlessly, yet many people are still deeply lonely.

That is why genuine connection matters now more than ever.

Not surface-level conversations.
Not transactional relationships.
Not friendships built only around convenience.

Real connection.

The kind where someone notices your absence.
The kind where people celebrate your growth instead of competing with it.
The kind where you do not have to shrink yourself to belong.

Those are your people.

Those are your family.

And maybe some of you reading this have spent years mourning the family you wished you had while overlooking the incredible people God already placed around you. The friend who calls to check in. The person who always saves you a seat. The coworker who notices when your spirit seems heavy. The person who never lets you fight alone.

That is family too.

Maybe not by blood.
But definitely by heart.

At the end of our lives, I do not think we will remember who shared our DNA nearly as much as we will remember who shared our burdens, our laughter, our struggles, and our journey.

The people who showed up.
The people who stayed.
The people who helped us survive life.

Those are the people who become part of our soul story.

And maybe one of the greatest blessings in life is realizing that family is sometimes less about where we came from and more about who walks beside us while we are here.

title: Getting out of the Muck
excerpt: We have all been there--in the muck of life.  The lesson is how to rise above it.
content:
Making It Out of the Muck

There is something about getting stuck in the muck that changes a person. Muck is heavy. It slows you down. It drains your energy, clouds your thinking, and makes even simple things feel difficult. Sometimes life places us in seasons where we feel emotionally buried beneath stress, grief, fear, heartbreak, disappointment, or exhaustion. We try to keep moving forward, but it feels like every step takes twice the effort.

Most people know exactly what that kind of season feels like.

For some, the muck comes through loss. The loss of a loved one, a relationship, a dream, or even the loss of the person they once were. For others, it may be depression, anxiety, addiction, rejection, financial hardship, loneliness, burnout, or simply carrying years of emotional wounds nobody else can see. The hard truth is that many people are silently fighting battles every single day while pretending everything is fine.

One of the hardest parts about being in the muck is how isolating it can feel. You begin looking around at everyone else and convincing yourself they have life figured out while you are barely holding yourself together. Social media certainly does not help. We see carefully filtered moments of happiness while quietly carrying struggles we are afraid to talk about openly. What we do not see are the sleepless nights, the tears behind closed doors, the fear about the future, or the emotional exhaustion people carry privately.

Life has a way of humbling every person eventually. None of us escape hardship forever. There will always be moments that test our strength, challenge our faith, and force us to confront parts of ourselves we would rather avoid. Those moments can either harden us or deepen us. They can make us bitter, angry, and closed off, or they can slowly shape us into more compassionate and understanding human beings.

I think some of the strongest people in this world are people who kept going even when life became messy. Not the people who had perfect lives, but the people who fought through imperfect ones. Strength is not always loud or dramatic. Sometimes strength is simply showing up another day when your heart feels tired. Sometimes strength is going to work while carrying grief. Sometimes it is taking care of others while quietly struggling yourself. Sometimes it is simply refusing to give up.

Nature itself teaches us important lessons about muddy seasons. After a hard rain, fields become soaked and messy, but that same rain is also what produces growth. Seeds do not grow in comfort. They grow buried beneath dirt, pressure, darkness, and storms. The mud that appears ugly on the surface often becomes the very thing that nourishes new life underneath.

People are not much different.

Some of the kindest, wisest, most compassionate people you will ever meet are individuals who once walked through deep pain themselves. They know what loneliness feels like. They understand rejection. They recognize grief in another person’s eyes because they have lived through it too. Pain has a way of making people more aware of the struggles others carry.

That does not mean the muck is easy. Some seasons in life feel incredibly unfair. There are moments when we question God, question ourselves, and question whether things will ever improve. We pray for answers and sometimes feel like all we hear is silence. We wonder why certain doors closed, why certain people walked away, or why healing seems to take so much time.

Yet even in those moments, growth is quietly happening.

Faith often grows strongest in difficult seasons. Not perfect faith, but honest faith. The kind of faith that simply says, “God, I do not understand this, but please help me keep moving forward.” Some of the most sincere prayers people ever pray come from moments where they feel emotionally exhausted and spiritually worn down. And somehow, even then, God continues walking beside us through the mud.

One of the most beautiful things about surviving hard seasons is that eventually you begin realizing the muck did not destroy you. In many ways, it strengthened you. It taught you resilience. It taught you empathy. It taught you to appreciate peace, kindness, and genuine connection in ways you may never have understood otherwise.

You also begin to realize that healing does not happen all at once. It happens gradually. Quietly. One good day at a time. One deep breath at a time. One small step forward at a time. Eventually you notice yourself laughing again. Smiling again. Hoping again. Slowly, the weight becomes easier to carry.

And maybe that is part of the purpose behind difficult seasons. Maybe surviving the muck is not only about our own healing, but also about helping others someday. People who have walked through pain often become the very people capable of offering the most encouragement, compassion, and hope to someone else struggling nearby.

So if life feels heavy right now, if you feel emotionally exhausted, discouraged, or stuck in the mud, remember this: difficult seasons do not last forever. The muck may slow you down, but it does not define your future. You are still growing even in seasons where you feel buried.

One day you will look back and realize that what once felt like it might destroy you actually helped shape the person you were becoming all along.

title: Knowing Your Worth and Knowing When to Leave the Table
excerpt: We all have worth.  It is important to recognize it and value it.
content:
Knowing Your Worth and Knowing When to Leave the Table

There’s a quote from Brené Brown that has stuck with a lot of people for a reason:

“The hardest part about understanding our worth and value is accepting that the people who can’t see it aren’t the problem. The problem is staying at the table too long hoping they’ll finally notice.”

That quote hits differently when you’ve spent years trying to earn a seat somewhere that was never meant for you.

Sometimes we stay too long in places that drain us. Relationships. Friendships. Jobs. Social circles. Even family dynamics. We keep showing up hoping this time they’ll appreciate us. This time they’ll acknowledge our effort. This time they’ll stop overlooking us, dismissing us, criticizing us, or making us feel like we have to prove we belong.

And if we’re honest, many of us have done this far longer than we should have.

We sit at tables where our voice is ignored. Tables where we are tolerated instead of valued. Tables where we give everything we have emotionally, mentally, spiritually, and physically, while receiving crumbs in return. We stay because we believe loyalty means enduring mistreatment. We stay because we fear disappointing people. We stay because maybe, just maybe, if we work harder, love harder, achieve more, sacrifice more, then someone will finally look at us and say, “You matter.”

But here’s the difficult truth: people who are committed to misunderstanding your worth will often never see it no matter how brightly you shine.

That is not a reflection of your value.

A diamond does not stop being valuable because someone mistakes it for glass.

Too many people spend their lives trying to convince others of something God already declared when He created them: that they have purpose, dignity, value, and worth. You were not designed to beg for a seat at every table. Some tables are unhealthy. Some tables are built on ego, control, jealousy, manipulation, or convenience. Some tables only want you there as long as you stay small enough to make others comfortable.

And eventually, there comes a moment where standing up becomes an act of healing.

Not revenge.
Not bitterness.
Not arrogance.

Healing.

There is strength in quietly realizing, “I deserve better than this.” There is courage in walking away from environments that constantly make you question your worth. There is wisdom in understanding that protecting your peace is not selfish.

Jesus Himself walked away from places where hearts were hardened. Think about that for a moment. Even Christ did not force Himself into spaces where people refused truth, grace, or love. Sometimes the most faithful thing you can do is stop exhausting yourself trying to win over people who are determined not to see you clearly.

And leaving the table does not always mean physically leaving. Sometimes it means emotionally detaching from the need for approval. It means no longer allowing someone else’s opinion to determine your identity. It means realizing your worth was never up for public debate in the first place.

The older I get, the more I realize peace is expensive. Not financially, but emotionally. Peace often costs you toxic relationships, unhealthy attachments, constant people-pleasing, and the exhausting habit of shrinking yourself to fit into rooms where you were never celebrated.

But the beautiful thing is this: when you leave the wrong table, you make room to find the right one.

The table where you are heard.
The table where your presence matters.
The table where authenticity is welcomed.
The table where love is not transactional.
The table where you no longer have to audition to belong.

And sometimes? The most important table you’ll ever sit at is the one where you finally learn to sit comfortably with yourself.

No apology.
No explanation.
No begging for acceptance.

Just peace.

So if you’ve been questioning your value lately because of how someone has treated you, hear this clearly: your worth does not decrease because someone failed to recognize it.

Know your worth.

And when necessary, have the courage to get up from the table.

title: Leftovers
excerpt: We all have leftovers in our life.
content:
## Dr. Nick’s Matter of Perspective

### “Leftovers”

There’s something oddly personal about leftovers.

You open the refrigerator at midnight, standing there in the glow of the fridge light like it’s some holy revelation, staring at containers of mashed potatoes, half a casserole, cold pizza, or that mysterious bowl nobody wants to claim. And somehow, leftovers become a strange reflection of life itself.

Some leftovers are exciting. You know the kind. Chili that somehow tastes better the next day. Lasagna that settled into perfection overnight. Thanksgiving leftovers that become a full-contact sport in the kitchen. There’s anticipation there. Comfort. Familiarity.

And then there are the leftovers nobody touches.

The forgotten containers shoved to the back of the refrigerator. The ones we avoid making eye contact with because we already know the outcome is not going to be good. Every refrigerator has that one container that has crossed over from “leftover” to “science experiment.”

Honestly, some of us have emotional leftovers like that too.

Old hurt. Old conversations. Old regrets. Old failures. Old heartbreaks. Things we tucked away in the back corners of our minds hoping we wouldn’t have to deal with them again. We keep carrying them around long after they’ve stopped nourishing us.

The truth is, life leaves leftovers behind all the time.

Leftover grief from people we miss.

Leftover anger from words we can’t forget.

Leftover fear from moments that changed us.

Leftover guilt from choices we wish we could redo.

Sometimes we carry yesterday into today so heavily that we forget God already gave us fresh mercies this morning.

That’s one of the beautiful things about faith. God doesn’t define us by what’s leftover. He specializes in renewal.

We tend to look at ourselves and see the scraps. The mistakes. The pieces that didn’t work out. Meanwhile, God looks at us and still sees purpose.

Think about that.

Jesus fed thousands with leftovers.

Not only did He provide enough, but Scripture tells us there were baskets left over afterward. What people saw as “extra,” God saw as abundance. What seemed insignificant became evidence of provision.

Maybe the leftovers in your life are not proof that you’re broken. Maybe they’re reminders that you survived.

Survived loss.

Survived disappointment.

Survived seasons you thought would destroy you.

And somehow, by the grace of God, you’re still here reheating hope one day at a time.

Life has a way of convincing us that if something is leftover, it somehow has less value. We do this with people too. Society loves what is shiny, new, popular, trending, and polished. But there are so many people walking around feeling like emotional leftovers. Divorced. Rejected. Forgotten. Overlooked. Aging. Hurt. Feeling like everyone else got picked first while they were pushed to the side.

But some of the most beautiful people you will ever meet are the ones life has already broken open a little.

The people who know pain often become the people who show the most compassion.

The people who have struggled financially are often the first to help someone else in need.

The people who have battled loneliness are usually the ones checking on others.

Brokenness has a way of deepening the soul if we allow God to work through it instead of giving up inside it.

And maybe that’s why leftovers matter.

They remind us something was shared.

Something was experienced.

Something was enough.

There’s also something comforting about leftovers because they remind us of home. A container of your grandmother’s soup recipe. A leftover holiday meal. The smell of reheated roast or spaghetti. Food has memory attached to it. Sometimes leftovers feed more than hunger. Sometimes they feed the heart.

The older I get, the more I realize life itself is made of moments we try to hold onto because they mattered.

Leftover laughter from people no longer here.

Leftover traditions passed down through generations.

Leftover lessons from parents and grandparents who shaped us.

Leftover wisdom from seasons that nearly broke us.

Those things stay with us.

And truthfully, not everything leftover is bad.

Some things are worth revisiting.

Old dreams sometimes deserve another chance.

Old friendships sometimes deserve reconciliation.

Old passions sometimes deserve revival.

Sometimes we bury parts of ourselves because life became busy, painful, or exhausting. But deep down, pieces of who we once were still remain.

God can still use those pieces.

One of the hardest lessons in life is realizing not everything is meant to be carried forever. Some leftovers need discarded. Some bitterness needs released. Some guilt needs handed to God instead of reheated every single day in our minds.

Because if we keep consuming emotional leftovers that have spoiled, eventually they poison our spirit.

That’s why forgiveness matters.

That’s why grace matters.

That’s why healing matters.

Not because what happened was acceptable, but because you deserve peace moving forward.

Now let’s be honest for a moment. Leftovers also teach us humility. Especially when you confidently open a container thinking it’s chocolate pudding and discover it’s actually beef gravy. That kind of emotional damage stays with a person.

Life can feel like that too sometimes.

We expect sweetness and get struggle.

We expect answers and get silence.

We expect certainty and get change.

But not every season is meant to feel comfortable. Some seasons are meant to grow us.

There’s also something deeply human about sharing leftovers. Sending someone home with a plate after dinner. Wrapping up extra food for a neighbor. Feeding someone who needs help. Long before fancy meal delivery apps existed, people showed love through leftovers.

That’s because leftovers represent more than food.

They represent care.

Provision.

Enough to share.

Maybe that’s part of the perspective we need more often. Instead of focusing on what’s missing, maybe we should notice what remains.

Because even after the storm, even after the heartbreak, even after life takes bites out of us, there is usually still something left worth giving.

A little hope left.

A little faith left.

A little strength left.

A little kindness left.

And sometimes, that’s enough to help someone else keep going too.

So tonight, when you open the refrigerator and stare into the kingdom of plastic containers and aluminum foil, maybe remember this:

Life is full of leftovers.

Some things need thrown away.

Some things need warmed back up.

And some things, with time, somehow become even better than they were the first day.

title: “When the Lights Go Out and the Waters Rise”
excerpt: We are all handed things in life that throw us for a loop, how we respond to them makes the difference.
content:
There’s something humbling about losing power. One minute, life is moving along normally. The television is on, the microwave clock is glowing, the air conditioner is humming, and then... darkness. Silence. The kind of silence that almost feels loud.

And if you’ve ever walked downstairs after heavy rain only to hear the squish of carpet beneath your feet, then you know the special kind of heartbreak that comes with a flooded basement. That moment where you just stand there staring at the water like somehow glaring at it will make it disappear. Spoiler alert: it won’t. Trust me. The water always wins that argument.

Life has a funny way of reminding us how quickly things can change.

A storm rolls through. The lights go out. The sump pump quits because there’s no electricity to run it. Water starts creeping where it doesn’t belong. Suddenly, the things we take for granted become very noticeable. Electricity. Dry floors. Air conditioning. Wi-Fi. And yes, even that old basement box full of random cords we swore we’d clean out someday.

What’s interesting is how quickly our perspective changes in those moments. We stop worrying about emails, social media notifications, or what someone said online. We focus on the basics. Safety. Family. Cleanup. Survival. Getting through the next few hours.

Sometimes God has a way of stripping life down to its essentials so we can see clearly again.

Storms do that. Not just weather storms, but life storms too.

There are moments in life where everything feels flooded. Grief floods the heart. Anxiety floods the mind. Stress floods relationships. Fear floods our spirit. And sometimes we feel like we’re standing ankle-deep in emotional water holding a flashlight with dying batteries wondering how in the world we’re going to clean up the mess.

But here’s the thing about storms: eventually the water recedes.

The cleanup may take time. You may lose things. You may have to rebuild parts of your life. You may even sit in frustration asking, “Why did this happen?” while throwing soaked cardboard boxes into the trash pile.

But storms also reveal something important. They show us what truly matters.

Nobody stands in a flooded basement and says, “You know what I really wish I had done? Argued more on Facebook.” No. In those moments, people check on neighbors. They share generators. They offer towels, fans, food, and encouragement. Humanity tends to show up strongest when life gets messy.

Maybe that’s part of the lesson.

Maybe the loss of power reminds us where true power actually comes from.

Not from outlets in the wall, but from faith. From resilience. From community. From the people who show up with shop vacs and pizza. From the quiet strength God gives us when we feel overwhelmed.

And let’s be honest, if you’ve ever dealt with a flooded basement, you also discover mysterious things floating around that make you question every life decision you’ve ever made. “Why do I still own tax paperwork from 1997?” “Whose extension cord is this?” “Why do I have seventeen empty flower pots?”

A flooded basement is basically an archaeological dig of bad decisions and forgotten hobbies.

But maybe life is like that too. Sometimes storms uncover things we’ve ignored for years. Hurt. Pride. Neglected relationships. Forgotten dreams. Old wounds tucked away in the basement corners of our hearts.

And maybe God allows us to notice those things not to destroy us, but to help us clean house.

The truth is, the lights eventually come back on.

The basement dries out.

The fans stop humming.

Normal life slowly returns.

But if we’re paying attention, we come out of the storm with a different perspective. A little more grateful. A little more grounded. A little more aware that material things can disappear quickly, but faith, love, and perseverance hold stronger than any foundation poured in concrete.

Storms are temporary. Even the messy ones.

And sometimes the greatest growth happens after the floodwaters go down.

title: Some Assembly Required
excerpt: Who has built something?
content:
Some Assembly Required

There is something both hopeful and frustrating about opening a box labeled: “Some Assembly Required.”

You open the package thinking it will be simple. Then suddenly there are 437 pieces scattered across the floor, instructions written in what appears to be ancient hieroglyphics, and one tiny screw that somehow disappeared into another dimension the moment you opened the bag.

And somehow, life feels a lot like that sometimes.

As children, many of us believed adulthood would arrive fully assembled. We thought confidence, purpose, peace, faith, relationships, careers, and identity would just magically come together one day like a finished display model sitting perfectly in the showroom.

Instead, we discover that life comes in pieces.

Some assembly required.

A little heartbreak here.
A little healing there.
A few broken relationships.
Some failures we never saw coming.
Dreams that changed.
Doors that closed.
Unexpected storms.
Moments where we questioned everything.

And somewhere in the middle of all that chaos, God quietly keeps building.

That is the part we often forget.

We focus on the scattered pieces on the floor while God sees the finished design.

Spiritually, many of us become frustrated because we are still “under construction.” We compare ourselves to others who appear polished, successful, confident, faithful, or put together. Meanwhile, we are sitting in the middle of our emotional living room floor staring at missing parts and wondering why our lives do not look complete yet.

But faith reminds us that God has never expected perfection overnight.

Even Scripture is filled with people who required assembly.

Moses doubted himself.
David failed repeatedly.
Jonah ran away.
Peter sank in the water and denied Jesus.
Paul carried scars from his past.

Yet God still used them.

Why?

Because God specializes in unfinished people.

The truth is, spiritual growth is rarely instant. It happens piece by piece. Prayer by prayer. Lesson by lesson. Sometimes loss teaches us compassion. Sometimes failure teaches humility. Sometimes loneliness teaches us how to lean on God instead of our own understanding.

And sometimes the assembly process hurts.

That’s the honest part nobody likes talking about.

Growth can feel uncomfortable because God often has to remove things that no longer fit who we are becoming. Pride. Fear. Anger. Bitterness. Ego. Control. Sometimes we cling tightly to pieces that were never meant to remain attached to our story.

Meanwhile, God keeps working with patience we do not even give ourselves.

There is also something humbling about instruction manuals. Most of us avoid them at first. We assume we know what we are doing. Twenty minutes later we are holding a backwards shelf, three extra bolts, and enough frustration to question every life decision that led us to this moment.

Spiritually… we do the same thing.

We often try to build our lives without guidance. We ignore wisdom. We avoid prayer. We rush ahead of God. Then when life feels unstable, we wonder why everything keeps wobbling.

Faith reminds us that God gave us direction for a reason.

Not to control us.
Not to punish us.
But to help hold us together.

And maybe one of the most important lessons is this:

Assembly takes time.

We live in a world obsessed with instant results. Instant success. Instant healing. Instant happiness. Instant transformation. But most meaningful things in life are built slowly. Character is built slowly. Trust is built slowly. Faith is built slowly.

Even healing happens in layers.

God is not mass producing people in a factory. He is shaping souls with intention.

So if your life feels unfinished right now… if parts of your story feel scattered… if you feel like you are rebuilding after loss, heartbreak, disappointment, or uncertainty… maybe that does not mean you are broken.

Maybe it simply means:

Some assembly is still required.

And that is okay.

Because God is not finished with you yet.

title: The First Gray Hair You Find
excerpt: I found a gray hair.
content:
The First Gray Hair You Find

There comes a moment in life that catches you completely off guard. You’re standing in the bathroom mirror under lighting that should honestly be considered criminal, minding your own business, when suddenly… there it is.

A gray hair.

Just sitting there boldly. Confidently. Almost disrespectfully.

You stare at it for a moment like you’ve discovered evidence at a crime scene. You lean closer. You pull your glasses down. You move your head around trying to convince yourself it’s just bad lighting. Maybe it’s blonde. Maybe it’s dust. Maybe the bathroom light is broken.

But deep down, you know.

The gray hair has arrived.

And what’s funny is that the first gray hair never asks permission. It doesn’t politely knock on the door and say, “Excuse me, Nick, I’d like to inform you that adulthood has now entered its deluxe edition.” No. It just shows up uninvited like a relative at Thanksgiving carrying emotional baggage and potato salad.

When we’re younger, we associate gray hair with getting old. We fear it. We resist it. Society tells us youth is the goal, wrinkles are enemies, and aging is something to hide from. Entire industries were built on convincing us that time itself is somehow a flaw.

But maybe gray hair is not a symbol of losing life.

Maybe it’s proof that we lived it.

That little silver strand represents years of surviving things you once thought would break you. It represents late nights, early mornings, stress, laughter, grief, healing, heartbreak, responsibility, growth, and perseverance. Gray hair doesn’t appear because life was easy. It appears because you kept going.

Every person carrying gray hair is carrying a story.

Some earned theirs raising children. Some earned theirs caring for aging parents. Some earned theirs through loss, anxiety, careers, sleepless nights, broken hearts, or battles nobody else ever saw. Others earned theirs simply by showing up every single day in a world that can sometimes feel exhausting.

And honestly? There is something honorable about that.

The Bible speaks often about aging not as weakness, but as wisdom. Proverbs 16:31 says, “Gray hair is a crown of splendor; it is attained in the way of righteousness.” That changes the perspective a little, doesn’t it?

A crown.

Not a punishment. Not a failure. A crown.

Maybe we spend too much time mourning what youth looked like instead of appreciating what experience has taught us. Because somewhere along the way, the things that mattered at 20 start looking very different at 40, 50, 60, and beyond. You begin valuing peace more than popularity. Quiet more than chaos. Authenticity more than approval.

And let’s be honest, some of us earned our gray hair faster than others. Dispatch work alone can age a person approximately 14 years during a single shift. Add in weather forecasting, life stress, bills, relationships, corgis screaming because it started raining outside, and suddenly your hair starts looking like a winter weather advisory map.

I truly think one of the greatest gifts that comes with aging is perspective. When you’re younger, you think life is about speed. As you grow older, you realize life is more about meaning.

You stop chasing everything.

You start appreciating things.

The smell of rain.
A quiet evening.
A conversation that matters.
The sound of laughter in another room.
A dog laying beside you.
A peaceful moment with God.
The ability to wake up and try again.

Gray hair reminds us that time moves quickly, but it also reminds us that every season has beauty. Spring may bring new beginnings, but winter carries wisdom. Both matter. Both have purpose.

So the next time you find that first gray hair, maybe don’t panic.

Maybe smile a little.

Because that tiny silver strand is evidence that you have lived, learned, survived, adapted, grown, and continued forward through every storm life placed in your path.

And honestly?

That’s something worth wearing proudly.

title: The Keyboard of Life
excerpt: Think about life and our computer keyboards.
content:
The Keyboard of Life

I was sitting at my computer the other day staring at my keyboard when something hit me. Life is a lot like the keys we press every single day. Some keys get used constantly. Some we ignore until we desperately need them. And some keys end up carrying far more meaning than we ever realized.

It is funny how ordinary objects can quietly teach extraordinary lessons if we slow down long enough to pay attention.

Most of us spend our lives typing away through responsibilities, obligations, deadlines, conversations, mistakes, dreams, and disappointments without ever thinking about the tools right in front of us. Yet every key on a keyboard reflects something about the human experience, our spiritual walk, and the way we navigate life itself.

Take the Escape key for example.

If we are honest, most people are trying to escape something. Some people are escaping stress. Others are escaping pain, loneliness, grief, trauma, insecurity, or fear. Some escape through work. Others through distractions, social media, unhealthy relationships, or endless busyness. Sometimes people stay so busy because silence forces them to confront emotions they are not ready to face.

And honestly, life can become overwhelming enough that escaping sounds appealing.

There are days when people want to escape the pressure of expectations, the uncertainty of the future, or simply the exhaustion of carrying emotional burdens nobody else can see. Sometimes even faith-filled people become tired. Even Jesus stepped away from crowds to pray, rest, and recharge. There is nothing wrong with needing moments of stillness and recovery.

But there is a difference between resting and running.
