Spiritually, many people spend years trying to avoid hard conversations, personal growth, forgiveness, healing, or accountability. Yet God often does His deepest work in the very places we try hardest to avoid. Some storms in life are not meant to destroy us. Some are meant to develop us.

Then there is the Space Bar.

Honestly, this might be the most underappreciated key on the keyboard.

Without space, everything becomes cluttered, difficult to read, overwhelming, and chaotic. The same thing happens in life. Many of us have filled every inch of our lives with noise, responsibilities, stress, opinions, schedules, notifications, and emotional clutter. We move from one obligation to another without ever giving ourselves room to breathe.

The world has become addicted to constant stimulation.

People wake up and immediately grab their phones before they even say good morning to God. We scroll while eating, scroll while resting, scroll while sitting in silence because somehow silence itself has become uncomfortable for people. We fear stillness because stillness forces us to think.

But spiritually, space matters.

Space allows healing.
Space allows reflection.
Space allows peace to enter places anxiety once occupied.

Some of the most important moments in life happen in quiet spaces. Moses encountered God in solitude. Jesus often withdrew to lonely places to pray. Sometimes God speaks the loudest when the distractions finally quiet down.

And maybe some of us need to create more space not only in our schedules, but also in our hearts.

The Tab key reminds me how easily distracted we become.

You start out paying one bill online and suddenly forty-five minutes later you are somehow watching videos about raccoons stealing cat food while researching whether Bigfoot could survive in Missouri. The human attention span is hanging on by a thread these days.

Life is full of tabs competing for our attention.

Work tabs.
Stress tabs.
Relationship tabs.
Financial tabs.
Political tabs.
Emotional tabs.

At some point, our minds become overloaded from having too many things open at once. Spiritually, distraction can quietly pull people away from purpose. Not because they stopped believing, but because they became mentally exhausted from trying to manage too much at one time.

Sometimes we need to close unnecessary tabs in our life.

Not every argument deserves your energy.
Not every opinion deserves your response.
Not every burden belongs on your shoulders.

Peace often returns when we simplify what has become unnecessarily complicated.

Then there is Caps Lock.

We all know people who live in permanent Caps Lock mode.

Everything is loud.
Everything is dramatic.
Everything feels like a crisis.

And social media certainly has not helped. Entire platforms are filled with people emotionally screaming at strangers they have never met. Everybody is offended. Everybody is angry. Everybody thinks typing in all capital letters somehow transforms them into a theologian, political analyst, life coach, and relationship expert simultaneously.

Meanwhile, half of us cannot even figure out why the printer stopped working.

Spiritually though, God often works differently than the chaos around us. Scripture says Elijah encountered God not through the wind, earthquake, or fire, but through a still small voice. There is tremendous strength in calmness. There is wisdom in restraint. Not every battle requires noise.

Sometimes maturity is learning when not to react.

The Delete key carries powerful meaning too.

There are things in life we need to intentionally remove.

Bitterness.
Constant comparison.
Unforgiveness.
Toxic relationships.
Self-hatred.
Shame over past mistakes.

Many people keep replaying old failures long after God has already offered grace. They continue punishing themselves for chapters God already redeemed. Spiritually, that becomes exhausting.

Delete does not mean pretending pain never existed. It means refusing to allow pain to permanently control your identity.

Some people need to delete the voices that constantly tell them they are not good enough. Some need to delete environments that drain their peace. Others need to delete the unrealistic expectation that they must hold everything together perfectly all the time.

And thank God for the Backspace key.

Because humans make mistakes constantly.

We say things we regret.
We misunderstand people.
We overreact.
We trust the wrong individuals.
We accidentally send text messages to the wrong person and immediately consider disappearing into witness protection.

Life is messy because people are messy.

The beautiful thing about grace is that God understands imperfection better than anyone. Backspace reminds us that mistakes do not have to define our future. We can apologize. Learn. Grow. Change. Mature. Healing and wisdom often come from moments we wish we could erase.

Then comes the Enter key.

At some point, every person reaches a moment where they must decide whether they are going to move forward or remain frozen in fear.

Pressing enter requires courage.

It means applying for the opportunity.
Starting over after heartbreak.
Trusting again after disappointment.
Stepping into purpose even when uncertainty exists.

Many people wait their entire lives for perfect clarity before taking action. But faith rarely works that way. Sometimes God asks us to move before we fully understand where the road leads.

That is what makes faith difficult and beautiful at the same time.

The Shift key may be one of the most spiritual keys on the keyboard.

Because life requires shifts constantly.

There are seasons where God shifts our priorities. Sometimes He shifts our mindset, our perspective, our relationships, or our direction entirely. And while shifts can feel uncomfortable, growth almost always requires movement.

Nobody grows by staying exactly the same forever.

Sometimes the hardest seasons in life are actually preparing us for the next chapter. Looking back, many of the moments we once resented became the very moments that shaped our strength, compassion, wisdom, and faith.

And finally, there is the Control key.

This one probably speaks to all of us.

Most people are exhausted from trying to control everything. We try controlling outcomes, timelines, relationships, careers, emotions, and situations that were never fully within our control to begin with. We grip tightly to plans while life keeps reminding us how unpredictable it can be.

Control creates anxiety because deep down we know we were never designed to carry the weight of the entire world.

Spiritually, surrender becomes one of the hardest lessons people learn.

Not surrender as weakness.
Not surrender as giving up.
But surrender as trust.

Trusting that God sees what we cannot. Trusting that unanswered prayers still have purpose. Trusting that delays are not always denials. Trusting that even when life feels chaotic, God is still writing something meaningful through the mess.

And maybe that is the real perspective hidden within the keyboard.

Every key serves a purpose. Every function matters. Even the keys we overlook still play a role in creating something bigger.

The same is true for people.

Your life has purpose.
Your story matters.
Your struggles are not wasted.
Your mistakes are not final.

Even during the hard seasons… God is still typing.

title: The Lighter Side of Live
excerpt: Life can be serious.  We all need to feel the lighter side.
content:
# Dr. Nick’s Matter of Perspective

## The Lighter Side of Life

Life gets heavy sometimes. There is really no other way to say it.

Between work, responsibilities, stress, bills, relationships, the news, and trying to remember why you walked into a room in the first place, adulthood can feel like one long group project nobody wanted to participate in. Somewhere between paying insurance premiums and discovering your back can go out simply from sleeping incorrectly, many of us slowly lose touch with the lighter side of life.

And that is a problem.

Because life was never meant to be lived entirely under pressure.

Now don’t get me wrong. I understand seriousness. I work in public safety. I understand stress, difficult days, emotional exhaustion, and moments where the weight of life sits on your shoulders like a sack of concrete. But I have also learned something important over the years: if you do not intentionally make room for laughter, joy, humor, and lightness in your life, the world will gladly consume every ounce of your peace.

Sometimes we become so focused on surviving life that we forget to actually live it.

The older I get, the more I appreciate the ridiculous little moments in life. Honestly, they are often the best part. Like when one of my corgis takes off running through the house at full speed because someone opened a cheese bag three rooms away. Or when they bark at absolutely nothing with the confidence of trained security professionals protecting the Pentagon. Meanwhile, I am investigating the “threat” only to discover it was a leaf blowing across the patio.

A leaf.

A dangerous criminal leaf.

And yet somehow those moments matter. They break the tension. They remind us not to take every second of life so seriously.

Humor has a way of healing people in ways we do not always recognize.

There is something deeply human about laughing so hard you cannot breathe correctly for a moment. The kind of laughter where tears form in your eyes and you replay the moment three hours later and start laughing all over again. Those moments are medicine for the soul.

I think God understood that too.

I do not believe faith was ever intended to make people miserable, angry, judgmental, or emotionally stiff. Somewhere along the line, some people confused holiness with permanent irritation. But when you really look at life, there is humor woven throughout creation itself.

Think about it.

God created goats.
And corgis.
And the fact that humans can sneeze so violently we temporarily lose control of our entire body.

There is humor in life.

There is also humility in it.

Nothing keeps you grounded quite like waving at someone who was not waving at you. Suddenly you are standing there awkwardly pretending you were actually stretching your arm all along. We have all been there. If you say you have not, you are lying and probably need to repent.

Life gives us these little moments constantly. Moments that remind us we are human.

And honestly, the world needs more people who can laugh at themselves.

We live in a culture where everyone is exhausted, offended, angry, or trying to prove something all the time. Social media especially has made people forget how to simply enjoy life. Everybody is arguing. Everybody is performing. Everybody is trying to look perfect while secretly eating shredded cheese over the sink at midnight wondering what happened to their metabolism.

The lighter side of life reminds us that perfection was never the goal.

Connection is.

Joy is.

Peace is.

Some of the happiest people I have ever met were not the richest, most famous, or most successful people in the room. They were simply people who learned to appreciate life’s ordinary moments. They laughed often. They found joy in small things. They did not let bitterness consume them.

That takes effort.

Because bitterness is easy. Joy takes intentionality.

It takes choosing to still smile after hard days. Choosing to still find beauty after disappointment. Choosing to still laugh even when life feels uncertain.

And maybe that is one of the greatest acts of faith there is.

To still believe there is goodness in the world.
To still believe joy matters.
To still believe light can exist even during dark seasons.

I think many people today are desperate for permission to breathe again. To stop carrying the emotional weight of the world every second of every day.

So here is your reminder:

Watch the funny movie.
Call the friend who makes you laugh.
Sit on the porch.
Take the vacation day.
Pet the dog.
Eat the dessert.
Laugh until your stomach hurts.

And if life feels overwhelming, remember this important truth: sometimes surviving adulthood is simply finding humor in the fact that you walked into the kitchen for one thing and walked out holding a flashlight, a spoon, and absolutely no memory of your original mission.

That is not failure.

That is character development.

title: Don't Laugh at Me
excerpt: We never know the inner battles. Be mindful
content:
“Don’t Laugh At Me”

There is a song by Mark Wills called “Don’t Laugh At Me.” It came out years ago, but the message still punches just as hard today as it did back then. Maybe even harder.

Because if we are honest, a lot of people know exactly what it feels like to be laughed at.

Some people were laughed at because of their weight.
Some because of the clothes they wore.
Some because they struggled in school.
Some because they talked differently, looked differently, loved differently, or simply never seemed to “fit” into the mold everyone else expected.

And some people carry those moments for decades.

The truth is, people often remember the pain of rejection longer than they remember the victories in their life. A careless comment in middle school can echo louder in adulthood than a hundred compliments later on. Funny how that works, isn’t it? One cruel sentence can move into someone’s mind and sign a long-term lease without paying rent.

I remember what it felt like to be the one standing there hoping not to be picked last. Hoping someone would see value in me. Hoping someone would look beyond the outside and realize there was a human being standing there with feelings, dreams, insecurities, and purpose.

The world can be incredibly harsh to people who are different.

But here is the thing I have learned over the years: the people who are mocked, overlooked, rejected, and underestimated are often the very people who develop the deepest compassion for others. Pain has a strange way of teaching empathy. The ones who know heartbreak usually become the ones most careful with someone else’s heart.

That song reminds us of something simple but powerful: every single person is fighting a battle we may never fully understand.

The loud kid in class may be covering pain at home.
The quiet coworker may feel invisible.
The person who always jokes around may actually be carrying anxiety, depression, or loneliness.
The person sitting alone may not want to be alone at all.

And yet we live in a culture where people still laugh first and understand later.

Social media made it worse in many ways. We now have front-row seats to public humiliation. People hide behind screens and throw stones at others they have never even met. Sarcasm has become entertainment. Cruelty gets clicks. Compassion sometimes gets ignored.

But kindness still matters.

Actually, kindness matters more now than ever.

One encouraging word can stop someone from giving up. One moment of inclusion can change the trajectory of someone’s life. One conversation can remind someone they are seen. Never underestimate the power of making another human being feel valued.

Jesus understood this better than anyone.

Throughout Scripture, Christ consistently moved toward the people society mocked, rejected, judged, and pushed aside. The outcasts. The broken. The forgotten. The imperfect. While others pointed fingers, He offered compassion. While others built walls, He built connection.

That matters.

Because if we claim to follow God while tearing people apart, humiliating them, gossiping about them, or mocking them, then we completely miss the heart of the Gospel. Faith was never supposed to be used as a weapon against hurting people. It was supposed to be a light guiding people toward hope, grace, and love.

And maybe that is why this song still resonates after all these years. Deep down, every person wants the same thing: to be accepted, respected, and loved for who they are.

Not laughed at.
Not dismissed.
Not made to feel small.

Just seen.

Life has a way of humbling all of us eventually. The popular crowd gets older. Titles fade. Status changes. Wrinkles show up uninvited like relatives during the holidays. Hair disappears and knees start making microwave noises when you stand up. Time reminds us pretty quickly that appearances and popularity were never the things that mattered most anyway.

Character matters.
Compassion matters.
How we treat people matters.

So maybe today is a good reminder to pause before judging someone. To speak softer. To listen longer. To love deeper.

Because the person everyone else is laughing at may be carrying more strength than anyone realizes.

And sometimes the people who were laughed at the most become the people who spend their lives making sure others never feel that same pain again.

title: The Importance of Bees
excerpt: The Power of Bees
content:
The Importance of Bees

You ever notice how something so small can make such a massive impact? Bees are a perfect example of that. Tiny little creatures, buzzing around flower to flower, looking like nature’s overly caffeinated workers. Most people see them and either ignore them or immediately start waving their arms around like they’re directing air traffic at Lambert Airport. Meanwhile, the bee is probably just trying to mind its business and do its job.

Truth is, bees are some of the hardest workers on this planet. Quietly. Consistently. Without applause. Without recognition. Without a social media post announcing their productivity for the day.

And honestly? There’s a lesson in that.

Bees are responsible for pollinating a huge portion of the food we eat. Fruits, vegetables, flowers, crops, and ecosystems all depend on them. Without bees, the world changes dramatically. Gardens become empty. Harvests decline. Beauty fades. Life becomes harder. Yet most people never stop to think about the importance of the little things happening around them every single day.

That sounds a lot like people too.

Some of the most important people in our lives are often the quiet workers. The ones who show up every day. The ones who hold families together. The friend who checks in on everyone else. The dispatcher answering the phone in someone’s worst moment. The teacher who stays late. The nurse running on empty. The parent working double shifts. The person battling their own storms while still helping others survive theirs.

Not all heroes are loud.

Some simply keep the world pollinated.

Bees also remind us that life was designed with connection in mind. A bee cannot fulfill its purpose separated from creation around it. Flowers need bees. Bees need flowers. Nature itself works together in a balance that God designed intentionally. Scripture constantly reminds us that we are not meant to walk through life alone. We are connected to one another in ways we often fail to appreciate.

Sometimes we become so focused on ourselves that we forget how deeply our actions affect others. A kind word can pollinate hope. Encouragement can pollinate healing. Grace can pollinate growth. Even a smile can plant something meaningful in someone carrying invisible burdens.

The opposite is true too. Negativity spreads. Cruelty spreads. Bitterness spreads. We have enough hornets in the world already. We don’t need to volunteer for the position.

And let’s be honest for a moment. Bees don’t have easy lives. Wind knocks them around. Rain beats them down. Storms interrupt their path. Yet somehow they continue moving forward doing the work they were created to do.

That hits differently when you think about your own life.

How many storms have you survived already?

How many times did life try to knock you off course? How many moments did exhaustion, heartbreak, rejection, grief, anxiety, or disappointment make you wonder if you could keep going?

Yet here you are.

Still flying.

Still building.

Still trying.

Still becoming.

There’s something beautiful about persistence, even when your wings are tired.

I also think bees teach us something important about purpose. A bee does not wake up wondering if its existence matters. It simply fulfills the role it was created for. Humans, on the other hand, spend so much time doubting our value, comparing ourselves to others, or believing we have to become famous, wealthy, or powerful to matter.

But sometimes purpose looks ordinary.

Sometimes purpose is simply being faithful where you are planted.

Sometimes changing one life matters more than impressing a thousand strangers.

And maybe that’s the real perspective here.

Never underestimate the impact of small things done with consistency, kindness, and purpose. A bee may seem insignificant on its own, but together they sustain life itself.

That means your kindness matters.

Your work matters.

Your presence matters.

Your encouragement matters.

And your life matters more than you probably realize.

So the next time you hear the buzz of a bee drifting through the air, maybe pause before swatting at it. Maybe let it remind you that even the smallest creations can carry enormous purpose.

And maybe, just maybe, God designed you that way too.

title: The Silence Left Behind
excerpt: Suicide and Healing
content:
The Silence Left Behind

Suicide is one of the hardest conversations we can have as human beings.

It leaves behind questions that often never receive complete answers. It leaves families, friends, coworkers, classmates, and entire communities trying to understand something that feels impossible to fully process. It leaves silence where laughter once existed. Empty chairs at dinner tables. Unanswered text messages. Birthdays that suddenly feel different forever.

And perhaps one of the most painful realities is this:

Many people never truly saw the depth of the struggle happening inside the person who was suffering.

That is what makes suicide so complicated and heartbreaking. Sometimes the people who seem the strongest are carrying unbearable emotional weight in silence. Some smile publicly while privately fighting battles so dark and exhausting that they no longer know how to continue.

People often ask, “Why would someone do this?”

The honest answer is that suicide is rarely about one single moment, one single event, or one single weakness. It is often the result of emotional pain becoming so overwhelming that the person no longer sees a path forward. Depression, trauma, hopelessness, anxiety, loneliness, shame, addiction, exhaustion, grief, or mental illness can distort a person’s thinking in ways others cannot always see from the outside.

When someone is in that level of pain, they are often not trying to end life because they truly want to die. Many times, they simply want the pain to stop.

That does not make it easier for those left behind.

The aftermath of suicide leaves survivors carrying emotions that are incredibly heavy and deeply complicated.

Shock.
Anger.
Confusion.
Guilt.
Sadness.
Regret.
Questions that replay endlessly at 2 a.m.

“Did I miss the signs?”
“Could I have done more?”
“Why didn’t they call me?”
“Why didn’t they know they were loved?”

The human mind desperately searches for logic after tragedy because we want pain to make sense. But grief does not always give us clean answers.

And one of the cruelest burdens survivors often carry is guilt.

People replay conversations. They revisit memories. They overanalyze moments they once thought were ordinary. They wonder if one more hug, one more conversation, one more phone call could have changed the outcome.

Sometimes they even become angry at the person they lost, and then immediately feel guilty for feeling angry.

That emotional tug-of-war is real.

Because suicide leaves behind both grief and trauma.

Spiritually and emotionally, it can shake people deeply. Some struggle with faith afterward. Some question God. Some question themselves. Others become emotionally numb because the weight of the loss feels too large to hold all at once.

And if we are being honest, many people wrestle with painful spiritual questions after suicide.

“Where was God?”
“Why didn’t God stop this?”
“Did they know they were loved?”
“Did they find peace?”

Those questions come from broken hearts trying to understand unbearable pain.

I do not believe God is frightened by our questions, our tears, or even our anger. Throughout Scripture, we see people crying out to God in confusion, grief, exhaustion, and despair. David did it. Job did it. Elijah did it. Even faithful people reached moments where emotional suffering overwhelmed them.

Faith does not mean pretending pain does not exist.

Faith means bringing that pain honestly before God.

Sometimes people imagine faith as always being strong, confident, smiling, and unwavering. But some of the deepest faith ever demonstrated happens in moments when someone can barely whisper a prayer through tears.

“God, I do not understand.”
“God, this hurts.”
“God, help me survive this.”

That is still faith.

And for the people left behind, survival after suicide often becomes its own silent battle.

Because life keeps moving even when your heart feels frozen in time.

People still go to work.
Bills still arrive.
Children still need care.
The world keeps spinning while internally everything feels shattered.

Some survivors throw themselves into busyness because silence hurts too much. Others isolate because they no longer know how to explain their pain. Some cry openly. Others stop crying altogether because they become emotionally exhausted.

Many become afraid.

Afraid of losing someone else.
Afraid of not seeing warning signs again.
Afraid to trust happiness because they now know how quickly life can change.

Some survivors cope by talking constantly about the person they lost. Others avoid speaking the name entirely because it hurts too much. Grief does not follow a straight line, and there is no universal “correct” way to survive something this painful.

And then comes the blame.

Sometimes survivors blame themselves.
Sometimes they blame doctors, family members, friends, workplaces, systems, or even God.
Sometimes families quietly blame one another without ever saying the words out loud.

Pain has a way of looking for somewhere to land.

That is why grace becomes so important after tragedy.

Because most people did the best they could with what they understood at the time.

The reality is that mental illness and emotional suffering can become incredibly complex and hidden. Many people who die by suicide convinced others they were okay. Some had moments of hope right before they died. Some made future plans. Some laughed hours before. That confusion alone can haunt survivors because they keep trying to connect dots that may never fully connect.

And perhaps one of the hardest parts for survivors is learning how to carry both love and unanswered questions at the same time.

There may never be complete closure.

That is difficult for human beings because we crave resolution. We want the final chapter to make sense. But grief after suicide often leaves pages unfinished.

Faith itself may ebb and flow after loss.

One day someone may feel close to God. The next day they may feel abandoned. One moment they may find comfort in prayer, and the next they may feel anger while sitting in church wondering why tragedy touched their family.

That does not make them bad people.
That makes them grieving people.

Sometimes surviving grief means learning that faith is not always emotionally steady. Sometimes faith limps. Sometimes faith questions. Sometimes faith sits silently in confusion. Sometimes faith is simply choosing not to completely let go of God even while wrestling with heartbreak.

And maybe that is where many people misunderstand faith.

Faith is not the absence of doubt.
Faith is continuing the conversation with God even when your heart is broken.

There are people reading this right now who carry invisible grief from losing someone to suicide years ago, maybe even decades ago. And despite the passing of time, certain days still reopen the wound.

Birthdays.
Holidays.
Anniversaries.
Songs on the radio.
A familiar scent.
A random memory in the grocery store aisle.

Grief has no expiration date.

But slowly, over time, many survivors begin learning something important:

Healing does not mean forgetting.
Healing means learning how to live again while carrying the memory with you.

Some people honor loved ones through advocacy. Some through kindness. Some through helping others struggling with mental health. Some simply by surviving one more difficult day themselves.

And perhaps one of the greatest lessons for all of us is this:

We never fully know what someone else is carrying internally.

That stranger smiling at work.
That friend always joking around.
That strong person helping everyone else.
That family member who says “I’m fine.”

Some people are fighting silent battles every single day.

So check on people.
Listen more carefully.
Love more intentionally.
Judge less harshly.
Speak kindly.
Show up for others.

Because sometimes the smallest act of compassion becomes the very thing that helps someone hold on.

And to those left behind carrying grief today:

May you give yourself permission to heal slowly.
May you release the burden of carrying blame for things beyond your control.
May you find moments of peace again without guilt.
And may you remember that even in seasons where your faith feels weak, shaken, or exhausted, God’s presence does not disappear simply because your heart is struggling to feel it.

If you or someone you know is struggling with thoughts of suicide or emotional crisis, help is available. In the United States and Canada, you can call or text 988 to reach the Suicide & Crisis Lifeline. There are people ready to listen, support, and help carry the weight.

You do not have to fight alone.

Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.

title: The Old Towel
excerpt: How old is your oldest towel in your linen closet?
content:
The “Old Towel” in the Linen Closet

Every house seems to have one.

You know exactly which towel I am talking about too. The old towel sitting somewhere in the linen closet that has survived what feels like several presidential administrations, three washing machines, family chaos, life transitions, and enough detergent to sanitize an entire hospital wing.

It is faded. Slightly rough. Probably stained in ways nobody fully understands anymore. Maybe one corner is frayed. Maybe it no longer matches anything in the bathroom because the color has transformed from “ocean blue” into something resembling “emotional support gray.”

And despite owning newer, fluffier, more expensive towels… nobody throws that towel away.

Why?

Because the old towel has earned its place.

It became the towel you grab during emergencies. The towel for muddy shoes, spilled drinks, unexpected messes, dog baths, kitchen disasters, rainy days, or the corgi stampede coming through the house after they ignored every warning to stay out of the mud.

The old towel may not look impressive anymore, but it is dependable.

And honestly, the older I get, the more I think people are a lot like that towel.

We live in a world obsessed with “new.” New trends. New technology. New appearances. New upgrades. New versions of ourselves. Somewhere along the way, society started equating value with perfection, polish, and presentation.

But life does not work that way.

Some of the most valuable people in our lives are the ones who have been through wear and tear. The people with scars, heartbreaks, disappointments, setbacks, grief, failures, wrinkles, and stories. The people who may not appear shiny and perfect anymore but have become dependable through experience.

There is something sacred about people who continue showing up after life has tried to unravel them.

The “old towel” people are often the ones holding everyone else together.

They are the friend who answers the phone at 2 a.m.
The parent who sacrifices quietly.
The grandparent whose wisdom came from struggle.
The teacher who keeps encouraging students despite burnout.
The first responder carrying invisible emotional weight.
The person battling their own storms while still helping others survive theirs.

Life has worn them down in places, but it has also strengthened them.

Spiritually, I think sometimes we misunderstand how God works in our lives. We often think God only uses the polished, perfect, successful versions of people. But throughout history and Scripture alike, brokenness has never disqualified someone from purpose.

In fact, many times it becomes part of the purpose.

The worn places in our lives often create compassion.
The painful places often create empathy.
The difficult seasons often create wisdom.

An unused towel folded perfectly in the closet may look beautiful, but it has never actually served its purpose.

The old towel has.

And maybe that is true for us too.

Maybe some of the moments we are most ashamed of are actually the places where we became softer toward others, more understanding, more patient, more grounded, and more human.

Now let’s be honest for a second. The old towel in most households also becomes the “universal towel.” Nobody knows exactly whose towel it originally was anymore. It simply exists. It has transcended ownership. It belongs to the family now. It may have survived moves, renovations, breakups, children, pets, and probably at least one plumbing emergency.

That towel has seen things.

Frankly, if old towels could testify, some households would need legal representation immediately.

But humor aside, there is something comforting about familiar things that remain through changing seasons of life. In a world where so much changes so quickly, the old towel quietly reminds us that usefulness is not determined by appearance.

Sometimes the most reliable things in life are not the prettiest. They are simply faithful.

And maybe some people reading this today feel like the old towel.

Worn out.
Overused.
Tired.
Overlooked.
Feeling less valuable because life has been hard.

But do not confuse wear with worthlessness.

You may have endured difficult seasons, but that does not lessen your value. If anything, your endurance reveals your strength. The struggles you survived did not ruin you. They shaped you.

There is dignity in surviving.
There is beauty in perseverance.
There is wisdom in weathered souls.

So maybe the old towel in the linen closet is more than just a towel. Maybe it is a reminder that some things become more meaningful not because they stayed untouched, but because they remained useful despite everything they endured.

And honestly, the world needs more dependable people than decorative ones.

Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.

title: Fearing Fear
excerpt: What do you fear?
content:
Fearing Fear

Fear is one of the most powerful emotions we carry as human beings.

It can stop us before we even begin. It can convince us to stay quiet when we should speak, stay still when we should move, and stay hidden when we were meant to grow. Fear has a way of sitting in the corner of our minds like an unwanted houseguest, whispering every possible thing that could go wrong.

What if I fail?
What if I embarrass myself?
What if they reject me?
What if I am not good enough?
What if things never get better?

The truth is, many of us are not just afraid of situations themselves. We are afraid of the feeling fear creates inside of us. We fear the uncertainty. We fear losing control. We fear discomfort. Sometimes we fear fear itself so much that we avoid opportunities, relationships, dreams, healing, and change altogether.

And that is where fear becomes dangerous.

Fear was never meant to become a permanent residence in our lives. Fear can serve a purpose. It can warn us. Protect us. Make us cautious in dangerous situations. But fear was never supposed to become the steering wheel of our entire existence.

Yet so many people spend years imprisoned by it.

Some fear failure.
Some fear success.
Some fear being alone.
Some fear being seen.
Some fear loving others because they know what heartbreak feels like.
Some fear taking the first step because they have fallen before.

Fear often builds walls long before life ever builds them.

I think about storms often. Naturally, as a meteorologist, I respect storms. I know their power. I know their unpredictability. But I also know something important: radar may show a storm approaching, but that does not mean the storm will destroy everything in its path.

Sometimes fear operates like emotional radar. We see trouble in the distance and immediately assume catastrophe. Our minds begin forecasting disaster before a single drop of rain even falls.

We create worst-case scenarios.
We rehearse heartbreak.
We anticipate rejection.
We expect failure before we even try.

And in doing so, we often suffer twice. Once in our imagination and again in reality.

Fear also has a strange way of making us underestimate ourselves. We forget the storms we already survived. We forget the battles God already carried us through. We forget the countless moments we thought would break us but somehow did not.

Fear says, “You cannot handle this.”
Experience quietly whispers, “You already have.”

Spiritually, fear can become incredibly heavy because it often pulls us away from trust. Fear tells us we must control every outcome. Faith reminds us that not everything was ours to control in the first place.

That does not mean faith removes fear entirely. Even faithful people become afraid. Even strong people shake sometimes. Courage is not the absence of fear. Courage is choosing to move forward despite it.

Sometimes courage looks dramatic.
Sometimes courage simply looks like getting out of bed.
Making the phone call.
Applying for the job.
Setting boundaries.
Walking away from toxicity.
Going back to school.
Starting over.
Trusting again.
Loving again.
Living again.

Fear also thrives in isolation. The more alone we feel, the louder fear becomes. That is why connection matters so deeply. Honest conversations matter. Compassion matters. Community matters. Sometimes the strongest thing a person can say is, “I am struggling.”

There is no shame in fear. We are human. But there is danger in allowing fear to define who we are.

Some of the greatest moments in life exist on the other side of fear.

The friendship you almost avoided.
The dream you almost abandoned.
The opportunity you almost talked yourself out of.
The healing you almost refused to begin.

Fear loves closed doors. Growth usually begins when we open one anyway.

And let’s be honest, sometimes fear itself becomes exhausting. We become tired from carrying anxiety about things that may never even happen. It is like mentally paying interest on a debt we do not yet owe.

That kind of fear steals joy from the present moment.

So maybe today is not about pretending fear does not exist. Maybe it is about recognizing that fear does not deserve authority over your life.

You are allowed to feel uncertain and still move forward.
You are allowed to feel nervous and still try.
You are allowed to feel afraid and still believe better days are ahead.

Because strength is not found in never being afraid. Strength is found in refusing to surrender to it.

And sometimes the most powerful words we can say are simple:

“I was afraid… but I kept going.”

Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.

title: The Struggles of a Monday
excerpt: Monday's are one of those days...
content:
The Struggles of a Monday

There is just something about Monday.

The alarm clock sounds a little louder. The bed feels a little warmer. The coffee never seems strong enough, and somehow the weekend already feels like it happened three months ago instead of two days ago. Monday walks into our lives like an uninvited relative carrying responsibilities, deadlines, bills, emails, meetings, laundry, and that strange feeling of emotional exhaustion before the week even begins.

For many people, Monday represents pressure. It is the restart button whether we are ready or not. The work resumes. The routines return. The expectations show back up at the front door carrying a clipboard.

And truthfully? Sometimes we walk into Monday already tired.

Not just physically tired, but emotionally tired. Spiritually tired. Mentally drained from carrying burdens nobody else can see. Some people are walking into this Monday carrying grief. Others are carrying anxiety. Some are worried about finances, relationships, health, loneliness, work stress, or simply trying to hold themselves together while pretending everything is fine.

The thing about Mondays is they expose where we are emotionally. After the distractions of the weekend settle down, we often come face-to-face with reality again.

But maybe that is also where the beauty of Monday quietly exists.

Monday is not just the start of another work week. It is proof that we were given another opportunity.

Another sunrise.
Another chance to begin again.
Another opportunity to heal something broken.
Another day to grow, forgive, improve, reconnect, or simply survive.

Not every Monday has to be productive and perfect. Sometimes success on a Monday simply means getting out of bed when life feels heavy. Sometimes the victory is showing up. Sometimes the accomplishment is making it through the day without giving up on yourself.

We live in a culture that glorifies nonstop hustle and perfection. Every Monday people post motivational quotes about “grinding harder” and “dominating the week.” Meanwhile, there are people quietly fighting battles that make simply existing feel exhausting.

Maybe this Monday does not require perfection from you. Maybe it just requires persistence.

There is also something humbling about Mondays because they remind us that life moves forward whether we are fully prepared or not. Seasons change. Time moves. Responsibilities continue. But so does growth.

The hard Mondays teach resilience.
The exhausting Mondays teach endurance.
The uncertain Mondays teach faith.

Faith is not always standing on a mountain shouting confidence into the sky. Sometimes faith is dragging yourself through a difficult Monday while whispering, “God, help me get through today.”

And there is nothing weak about that.

Even nature struggles with transitions. Storms do not instantly become sunshine. Winter does not abruptly become spring. There is always a process. A gradual shift. A movement from darkness toward light.

We are not much different.

Some Mondays feel sunny and full of possibility. Others feel cloudy with scattered emotional breakdowns by noon. Trust me, as someone with corgis, I can confirm that sometimes Monday also includes muddy paw prints across the floor, dog hair floating through the air like tumbleweeds, and one of the dogs staring at me like I personally scheduled the work week myself.

But life is still happening in those moments too.

Sometimes we spend so much time dreading Monday that we forget it holds opportunities we prayed for years ago. The job we complain about may once have been a prayer for stability. The education stressing us out may have once been a dream opportunity. The responsibilities overwhelming us may exist because we are trusted, capable, and needed.

Perspective changes everything.

Monday may not always feel exciting, but it is still meaningful.

So if today feels heavy, take a breath. Give yourself grace. Stop measuring your worth by how productive you are before 9 a.m. You do not have to conquer the entire week today. You simply have to take the next step forward.

One hour at a time.
One conversation at a time.
One prayer at a time.
One moment at a time.

And maybe that is the true lesson of Monday. Not perfection. Not speed. Not pressure.

Just movement forward.

Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.

title: A Mother’s Love Leaves Fingerprints on the Heart
excerpt: Happy Mother's Day
content:
A Mother’s Love Leaves Fingerprints on the Heart

Mother’s Day is often filled with flowers, brunch reservations, greeting cards, and social media posts. And while all of those things are wonderful, Mother’s Day is really about something much deeper. It is about sacrifice. It is about unconditional love. It is about the women who carried burdens quietly so others could stand a little taller in life.

Some mothers gave birth to us. Some adopted us. Some stepped into our lives when they did not have to. Some became “mom” simply because they chose to love and protect someone else. Motherhood is not always defined by biology. Sometimes it is defined by presence, patience, forgiveness, and showing up over and over again even when life is exhausting.

As I have gotten older, I have realized something. Moms often carry the emotional weather of the household. They celebrate victories loudly while quietly carrying worries nobody else sees. They remember birthdays, appointments, milestones, and little details most people forget. They pray over their families in silence. They stay awake when everyone else is asleep. They worry when they do not hear from you. And somehow, they still find the energy to ask, “Did you eat today?”

That kind of love is sacred.

For many people, Mother’s Day is joyful. For others, it can be painful. Some are celebrating without their mother for the first time. Some long to become mothers. Some have complicated relationships with their moms. Some are grieving children they lost too soon. Days like today can hold both gratitude and grief at the same time. Life often works that way. Two emotions can exist together. Joy and sorrow frequently sit at the same table.

I think one of the greatest lessons mothers teach us is resilience. They teach us how to keep going when life gets messy. They teach us how to love imperfect people. They teach us how to survive hard seasons while still finding reasons to laugh around the kitchen table. Mothers have this remarkable ability to hold families together with faith, duct tape, casseroles, and determination. Honestly, if moms ran the world completely, we would probably all be better hydrated and carrying snacks.

Spiritually, motherhood reflects something powerful about God’s love. Scripture constantly reminds us of compassion, nurturing, patience, and mercy. Those qualities are often modeled first through the women who raise us. Long before many people fully understand grace, they experience it through a mother’s forgiveness, encouragement, and care.

And maybe that is the perspective today.

Mother’s Day is not just about celebrating perfection. It is about honoring the countless unseen moments that helped shape who we are becoming. The rides to school. The late-night talks. The worried prayers. The hugs. The discipline. The sacrifices nobody applauded. The moments that seemed ordinary at the time but became foundational pieces of our lives.

So today, call your mom if you can. Hug her if you are able. Remember her if she is gone. Thank the women who stepped into your life and loved you when they did not have to. And if today is difficult for you, give yourself grace too.

Because love, especially a mother’s love, leaves fingerprints on our hearts long after the moment passes.

Happy Mother’s Day to all the moms, grandmothers, stepmothers, foster mothers, dog moms, spiritual mothers, and every woman who has helped nurture and guide others along the way.

And as always…

Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.

title: The Tassel Was Worth the Hassle
excerpt: May is graduation month!
content:
The Tassel Was Worth the Hassle

May carries a different kind of energy.

Across towns, cities, colleges, universities, and schools, gyms and auditoriums begin transforming into places of celebration. Folding chairs are lined up in perfect rows. Proud parents walk in carrying flowers and balloons. Grandparents search for the best seat possible. Cameras flash constantly. Teachers smile with pride. Students laugh nervously while adjusting caps and gowns that somehow never fit quite right.

For a few brief hours, those gyms and auditoriums become sacred places.

Not because of the decorations.

Not because of the music.

But because they become gathering places for stories of perseverance, sacrifice, growth, and survival.

Graduation is never just about receiving a diploma.

It is about everything it took to get there.

Every graduate who walks across that stage carries an invisible story. Some stories are filled with triumph. Others are filled with struggle. Some graduates reached this milestone while balancing jobs, financial hardship, caregiving responsibilities, military service, mental exhaustion, personal loss, heartbreak, or self-doubt.

Yet despite every obstacle, they kept going.

That matters.

We often celebrate the final moment without fully understanding the journey that created it. We applaud the achievement but do not always see the countless nights of stress, the tears after failed exams, the fear of not being good enough, or the moments where students quietly sat alone wondering if they should simply quit.

But they didn’t quit.

They kept climbing.

And every single step toward that stage means something.

The walk across the stage may only last a few seconds, but those few seconds represent years of dedication. Each step symbolizes early mornings, late nights, deadlines, sacrifices, discipline, growth, and resilience. Every step represents the determination to continue moving forward even when life became difficult.

There is something symbolic about stairs leading to a graduation stage.

You step upward.

One foot at a time.

Just like life itself.

Nobody jumps from the beginning of a journey directly to success overnight. Great accomplishments are built slowly through consistency, sacrifice, patience, and endurance. Sometimes graduates do not even realize how much they have grown until they pause long enough to look back at where they started.

For some graduates, this chapter marks the completion of 8th grade and the beginning of young adulthood. For others, it is high school graduation, where the safety of familiarity begins to give way to uncertainty and independence. College graduates celebrate years of academic rigor and personal transformation. Graduate and professional students often carry an even heavier weight of sacrifice.

Obtaining a graduate degree, professional degree, medical degree, law degree, or doctorate is an extraordinary accomplishment.

The reality is that only a relatively small percentage of the population ever achieves these levels of education. Earning a doctorate or professional degree places individuals among a select group of people who committed themselves to years of advanced learning, training, research, clinical work, writing, testing, internships, residencies, dissertations, or endless hours reviewing case law and preparing for exams.

Those degrees are not earned casually.

They are earned through exhaustion.

Through persistence.

Through sacrifice.

Through continuing to move forward when everything inside of you wants to stop.

Many graduates pursuing advanced degrees sacrifice parts of themselves along the way. They miss birthdays, holidays, vacations, and ordinary moments because they are chasing something bigger than temporary comfort. They spend nights studying while others sleep peacefully. They read textbooks until their eyes blur. They question themselves repeatedly while carrying pressure that most people around them never fully understand.

There are moments during those journeys where students feel completely defeated.

Moments where anxiety creeps in quietly.

Moments where imposter syndrome whispers that maybe they are not smart enough.

Moments where financial stress, emotional exhaustion, or personal struggles collide all at once.

Yet somehow, they continue climbing.

That is what makes graduation beautiful.

It is not perfection that got them there.

It is perseverance.

And while these accomplishments deserve celebration, they also bring something else: responsibility.

A doctor walking across that stage is not simply receiving a title. They are stepping into the responsibility of healing, comforting, listening, and caring for human life during some of people’s most vulnerable moments. Patients will someday place trust in their hands.

An attorney walking across the stage is stepping into the responsibility of defending justice ethically and honestly. Words, arguments, and decisions now carry tremendous weight.

A doctoral graduate enters a world where leadership, mentorship, research, and scholarship become part of their mission. Their knowledge now has the ability to influence future generations, policies, organizations, and communities.

Educators carry the responsibility of shaping minds.

Counselors carry the responsibility of guiding hurting hearts.

First responders carry the responsibility of protecting lives during crisis.

Leaders carry the responsibility of influencing people beyond themselves.

Titles matter.

But what we do with those titles matters even more.

The world does not simply need educated people. It needs compassionate people. It needs ethical people. It needs humble people who remember where they came from and who choose to help others climb rather than pulling the ladder up behind them.

Because education should never create arrogance.

It should create awareness.

The more we learn, the more we realize how much we still do not know.

True wisdom creates humility.

Life itself unfolds in chapters.

Graduation marks the ending of one chapter, but it also marks the uncertain beginning of another. Some graduates know exactly what comes next. Others feel terrified because the future feels unclear. Some are moving across the country. Some are beginning careers. Some are continuing their education. Some are quietly trying to figure out who they even are outside of school.

That uncertainty can feel overwhelming.

But uncertainty is often where growth begins.

Faith often works that way too.

God rarely hands us the entire roadmap all at once. Instead, life unfolds chapter by chapter, lesson by lesson, season by season. Sometimes we want immediate answers about our future, but growth often happens in the waiting, the uncertainty, and the uncomfortable moments where we are forced to trust even when we cannot fully see what comes next.

Graduation reminds us that growth always requires movement.

No one reaches the stage accidentally.

Every assignment mattered.

Every difficult conversation mattered.

Every failure mattered.

Every lesson mattered.

Even the setbacks mattered.

Sometimes the classes that challenged us the most became the moments that shaped us the deepest. Sometimes the failures we feared the most became the lessons we needed most in life. Sometimes the closed doors redirected us toward opportunities we never would have discovered otherwise.

And perhaps that is one of the greatest truths about education and life itself: growth often happens during the hardest seasons.

To the Graduating Class of 2026, understand something important:

You are stepping into a world desperate for people with integrity, compassion, wisdom, patience, humility, and courage.

The world needs doctors who see patients as human beings, not numbers.

It needs attorneys who pursue justice honestly.

It needs educators who inspire instead of discourage.

It needs leaders who serve rather than control.

It needs people willing to bring light into places filled with negativity.

Your diploma represents accomplishment, but your character will define your legacy.

Years from now, people may not remember your GPA, your honors cords, or the exact degree you earned. But they will remember how you treated people. They will remember whether your success made you compassionate or arrogant. They will remember whether you encouraged others or only focused on yourself.

Success without kindness becomes hollow.

Achievement without humility becomes dangerous.

Knowledge without compassion loses its purpose.

As you enter these new chapters of life, embrace both the opportunities and the responsibilities ahead of you. Continue learning. Continue growing. Continue listening. Continue showing kindness in a world that often forgets how important simple kindness truly is.

Do not be afraid of beginning again if life changes direction.

Do not be afraid of failure.

Do not be afraid of uncertainty.

Some of the greatest stories are written during the chapters we never planned for.

So as graduates walk across stages in gyms and auditoriums this May, surrounded by applause, cheers, tears, and celebration, remember that the moment represents far more than a ceremony.

It represents growth.

It represents sacrifice.

It represents resilience.

It represents chapters ending and new ones beginning.

It represents opportunity.

It represents responsibility.

And perhaps most importantly, it represents hope.

Hope for a future still waiting to be written.

Hope for lives that will impact others.

Hope that education, perseverance, faith, and compassion still matter in this world.

So graduates, walk proudly into your next chapter.

Take the lessons with you.

Carry the humility with you.

Carry the compassion with you.

Carry the memories with you.

And never forget how hard you worked to arrive at this moment.

The tassel truly was worth the hassle.

Congratulations Class of 2026.

-Dr. Nick

title: What we Wear?
excerpt: What are you wearing and what is it saying about you?
content:
Dr. Nick’s Matter of Perspective: What We Wear

Clothes are funny things when you really stop and think about them. They are pieces of fabric stitched together, hung in closets, tossed on chairs, piled in laundry baskets, and yet somehow… they tell stories. Before we ever say a word, our clothes often speak for us. Sometimes loudly. Sometimes quietly. Sometimes with confidence. Sometimes with exhaustion.

There are days we wear bright colors because life feels hopeful. There are days we throw on sweatpants and oversized hoodies because honestly, we are just trying to survive the day without falling apart in the cereal aisle at the grocery store. We all have those outfits. The “don’t talk to me” outfit. The “I need confidence today” outfit. The “I’m pretending I have my life together” outfit.

And let’s be honest. Some of us have clothes we refuse to throw away because they represent a memory, a season of life, or a version of ourselves we are not quite ready to let go of yet. That old concert shirt. The hoodie from college. The jacket that somehow survived every storm of life with us. Those clothes become more than fabric. They become emotional landmarks.

Clothes can reflect our personality and identity. Some people dress professionally because structure makes them feel grounded. Others dress casually because comfort feels safe. Some express themselves through bold colors and patterns, while others prefer neutral tones that quietly blend into the background. Neither is wrong. It is simply expression. It is human nature trying to show the world who we are, or maybe even who we hope to become.

Sometimes clothes are armor.

A suit can hide insecurity. A uniform can carry responsibility. Makeup and fashion can mask pain. We can look perfectly put together while silently carrying heartbreak, anxiety, grief, or loneliness underneath it all. The world often compliments appearances without ever realizing the battle happening inside the person wearing them.

That perspective matters.

Because maybe the person wearing wrinkled clothes is overwhelmed. Maybe the teenager dressing differently is trying to figure out where they belong. Maybe the elderly person wearing the same outfit repeatedly is struggling more than anyone realizes. Maybe the person overly dressed for an occasion simply needed to feel good about themselves for one day.

We judge people quickly by appearance. Humanity has always done it. But faith reminds us that God sees far deeper than fabric and outward presentation.

Scripture reminds us in 1 Samuel 16:7 that “People look at the outward appearance, but the Lord looks at the heart.”

That verse humbles me every single time.

We spend so much energy trying to look acceptable to others while God is focused on the condition of our spirit. The kindness in our hearts matters more than the logo on our shirt. The compassion we show matters more than the shoes we wear. Character will always outlast style trends.

Now don’t get me wrong, I still appreciate good clothes. I mean, if I find a shirt that fits right, hides stress-eating evidence, and survives corgi hair, it practically deserves an award. And if you own dogs, especially corgis, you already know your wardrobe eventually becomes less about fashion and more about asking yourself, “Will the fur show on this color?”

Black clothing owners understand this struggle deeply.

But maybe clothing also teaches us another important lesson: we are allowed to change.

Styles evolve. Seasons change. We outgrow certain clothes physically and emotionally. The outfit that represented who you were five years ago may not fit the person you are becoming today. And that is okay. Growth often requires us to step into something new.

The same is true spiritually and emotionally.

Sometimes we carry old labels, old shame, old insecurities, and old versions of ourselves far too long. We wear them daily without realizing they no longer fit who God created us to be. Fear. Rejection. Self-doubt. Hurt. We become comfortable carrying things that were never meant to define us forever.

Maybe it is time to take some of those things off.

Maybe it is time to stop dressing your spirit in criticism and disappointment.

Maybe it is time to wear grace again.

Confidence again.

Hope again.

Love again.

Because what you wear on the inside eventually matters far more than what you wear on the outside.

And the beautiful thing is this: every day gives us another opportunity to choose how we present ourselves to the world, not just through fashion, but through kindness, humility, compassion, and authenticity.

The best dressed people are not always the ones wearing expensive clothes.

Sometimes they are simply the people wearing kindness well.

Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.

title: The Rollercoaster We Never Asked to Ride
excerpt: Life can have up and downs.
content:
The Rollercoaster We Never Asked to Ride

I have never been much of a rollercoaster person. Truthfully, the idea of being strapped into a giant metal contraption while someone confidently says, “Enjoy the ride!” has never really brought me peace. I prefer keeping my feet firmly planted on the ground where Jesus and common sense intended them to be.

But even though I avoid rollercoasters at amusement parks, life still seems determined to put all of us on one anyway.

There are unexpected climbs, sudden drops, sharp turns, moments of fear, and seasons where everything feels completely out of control. One minute life feels calm and manageable, and the next minute you are hanging on emotionally wondering how things shifted so fast.

You know the kind of ride life can become. The slow climb upward where you can hear every click of uncertainty beneath you. The anticipation builds. Your stomach tightens. You look around wondering if maybe getting off would have been the smarter choice. But once life starts moving, there is no stopping the momentum completely.

There are seasons where we slowly climb toward goals, dreams, healing, relationships, purpose, or simply trying to find peace within ourselves. Those climbs can feel exhausting. Sometimes we wonder why progress feels so slow. Why the answers are not immediate. Why God feels quiet in the middle of the climb.

But often the climb is preparing us for the view.

The difficult part is that life does not allow us to stay at the top forever. Eventually come the drops. The unexpected diagnosis. The heartbreak. The rejection. The grief. The anxiety. The loneliness. The moments where life feels completely out of control and we are gripping onto faith with trembling hands just trying to hold ourselves together.

And if we are honest, some waves in life hit harder than others.

I think many of us spend so much time trying to avoid the hard moments that we forget the purpose of the ride itself. We pray for smooth roads with no bumps, no fear, no uncertainty, and no pain. But growth rarely happens in the calm stretches of life. Strength is often built in the moments where we feel shaken the most.

Even the ocean teaches us this lesson.

Waves rise.
Waves crash.
Waves retreat.
Then they rise again.

Life moves in seasons the same way. Nobody stays on the mountaintop forever, and thankfully, nobody stays in the valley forever either.

There have been moments in my own life where I felt emotionally upside down. Feeling disconnected. Questioning my worth. Wondering why certain people walked away so easily or why some doors closed without explanation. There are moments where life feels unfair. Moments where you silently ask God, “Are you sure this thing is still on the tracks?”

Yet somehow, we keep moving forward.

Maybe that is what resilience really is. Not pretending life is easy. Not forcing fake smiles. But continuing to hold on through the chaos while trusting that God still sees the full track ahead even when we cannot.

And maybe the people around us are riding waves too.

The cashier who seems frustrated.
The friend who suddenly becomes distant.
The coworker who looks exhausted.
The stranger sitting quietly in their car before walking into work.

We never fully know what kind of emotional ride someone else is surviving.

That is why kindness matters so much.

Sometimes people are not difficult.
Sometimes they are simply tired from the ride.

As I have gotten older, I have realized something important about waves and rollercoasters: they are temporary experiences. The fear passes. The storm settles. The sharp turns eventually straighten out.

And often when we finally catch our breath, we realize we survived moments we once believed would completely break us.

Maybe today you feel stuck in the middle of a steep drop.
Maybe you are climbing toward something uncertain.
Maybe life feels loud, shaky, and completely overwhelming.

But keep holding on.

The ride is not over yet.

There is still purpose in your story.
There is still beauty in your journey.
There is still growth happening in the middle of your uncertainty.

And sometimes the greatest testimony is simply this:

“I didn’t quit.”

Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.

title: When Life Starts Looking Like a Road Sign
excerpt: A sign we all need to see and understand.
content:
When Life Starts Looking Like a Road Sign

Most of us don’t think twice about street signs. They’re part of the routine. You drive, you see them, you respond, and you move on. Stop. Yield. No right turn. Merge. Sharp curve. One lane road. Speed limit. Exit. 

They guide us, warn us, and keep us on track.

But what if they’re doing more than that?

What if those same signs are quietly reflecting the way we’re supposed to move through life?

What if they’re not just directions for the road… but reminders for the soul?

The stop sign is usually the one we try to rush through. Even on the road, people barely come to a full stop anymore. And in life, it’s no different. We keep going, keep pushing, keep filling our schedules and our minds until there’s no space left to breathe. But God calls us to something different. “Be still, and know that I am God.” There are moments when stopping isn’t optional… it’s necessary. Necessary to hear Him. 

Necessary to reset. Necessary to avoid running straight into something we were never meant to face at full speed. Stopping isn’t falling behind. It’s realigning.

Then comes the yield sign, and this is where things get personal. Yielding means stepping back. Letting someone else go first. Trusting timing that isn’t your own. And that goes against everything in us. We want control. We want answers. We want movement. But faith often requires waiting. It requires trusting that God sees the full picture when we only see what’s in front of us. Yielding is not losing your place… it’s trusting that your place is being prepared.

The no right turn sign doesn’t get talked about much, but it might be one of the clearest messages. It’s a boundary. A firm “not this way.” And if we’re honest, those are the signs we struggle with the most. We see them in life. We feel them. We even know deep down that a certain direction isn’t right for us… but we try anyway. We convince ourselves we can make it work. But sometimes God puts up a clear sign that says, “Not this path.” Not to punish you… but to protect you. Not every open road is meant for you to take.
The merge sign speaks to transitions, and transitions are rarely comfortable. Merging requires awareness, patience, and timing.

 You can’t force your way in without causing disruption. Life is full of these moments… new jobs, new environments, new relationships, new seasons. You don’t always feel ready, and you don’t always know where you fit. But God doesn’t call you to force your place. He calls you to trust the process. To move with intention. To find your place in what He is already setting into motion.

The sharp curve ahead is where life can feel overwhelming. These are the moments that catch you off guard. The sudden change. The unexpected loss. The situation you never planned for. And your instinct is to keep going the same speed, hoping things will straighten out. But curves don’t work that way. They force you to slow down. To focus. To hold steady. And in those moments, faith reminds us that even when the road turns sharply, God is still guiding every step. You may not see what’s around the bend… but He does.

The one lane road represents those seasons where life narrows. Where distractions are stripped away. Where options feel limited. And at first, that can feel frustrating. Confining, even. But sometimes, less is exactly what we need. Less noise. Less confusion. Less pulling us in every direction. Just one path forward.

 Clear. Focused. Intentional. Jesus even spoke of the narrow road… not because it’s easy, but because it leads somewhere meaningful. 

Those seasons refine you. 

They teach you discipline. They strengthen your faith.

The speed limit is one we all struggle with. We want things faster. We want answers now. We want growth without waiting, healing without process, success without time. But God’s timing doesn’t operate on our urgency. It operates on purpose. 

There’s a pace designed for your life, and when you try to outrun it, you don’t just miss moments… you risk breaking something along the way. Slowing down isn’t a setback. It’s alignment. It’s trusting that what’s meant for you will come at the right time, not just the fastest time.

And then there’s the exit sign.

This is where faith really gets tested.

Exits require movement. They require leaving something behind. Sometimes something comfortable. Sometimes something familiar. Sometimes something you thought would last longer than it did. And we resist it. 

We stay too long. We hold on too tightly. But sometimes, the exit is exactly where God is leading you. Not away from something good… but toward something better. Toward growth. Toward purpose. 

Toward a path you couldn’t see until you were willing to let go of the one you were on.

When you begin to see these signs through a different lens, they stop being ordinary.

They become meaningful.

They become reminders that God is always guiding, always speaking, always present… even in the simplest parts of your day.

So the next time you’re driving and you see one of those signs, don’t just glance at it and move on.

Let it sit with you for a moment.

Ask yourself what it might be saying beyond the surface.

Because sometimes, the clearest messages in life aren’t the loudest ones.

They’re the quiet reminders placed right in front of you… over and over again… until you finally see them for what they are.

Guidance.
Direction.
Grace.

And a reminder that you are never navigating this road alone.

Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.

title: Using Our Time, Treasures, and Talent to Inspire Others
excerpt: What are you doing with your time, your talent, and your treasures?
content:
Using Our Time, Treasures, and Talent to Inspire Others
We’ve all been given something in this life.

Not the same things. Not in the same amounts. But enough.

Enough time.

Enough treasure.

Enough talent.

And the question isn’t whether we have them… it’s what we’re doing with them.

Time is the easiest to waste and the hardest to replace. It slips through our hands quietly, in busy days and distracted moments. But when we slow down long enough to really see it, we realize something important… the time we give to others is never wasted. A conversation, a visit, a few extra minutes to listen or encourage… those are the moments people carry with them. That’s where connection is built. That’s where impact begins.

Treasure isn’t just about money. It’s about what you’ve been blessed with. It’s your ability to give, to help, to meet a need. Sometimes that looks like something big. Most of the time, it doesn’t. It’s the quiet acts. The thoughtful gestures. The willingness to say, “I have something… and I’m going to use it to help someone else.” That kind of generosity has a ripple effect far beyond what we see.

And then there’s talent.

Every single person has something they bring to the table. Something that can encourage, teach, lead, or simply make someone’s day a little better. Too often, we downplay it. 

We think it’s not enough or not important. But what comes naturally to you might be exactly what someone else needs. When you use your gifts with intention, you create space for others to grow, to feel seen, to believe they can do the same.

Here’s the part that matters most…

You don’t have to have it all figured out to make a difference.
You don’t have to wait until you have more time, more money, or more confidence.
You start where you are.
You give what you can.
You use what you’ve been given.

Because when time, treasure, and talent come together with purpose… they don’t just change your life.
They inspire someone else’s.

And the truth is, you may never fully see the impact you’ve made. You may never hear the full story of how your kindness, your presence, or your effort helped someone through a moment they were struggling with.

But that doesn’t mean it didn’t matter.

It means it mattered more than you know.

So today, maybe the perspective is simple…
Be present with your time.
Be generous with your treasure.
Be intentional with your talent.
Because a life well-lived isn’t measured by what we kept…
It’s measured by what we gave…
and who we inspired along the way.
-Dr. Nick

title: BINGO Chaos
excerpt: B-I-N-G-O.
content:
Earlier this week, I took my son to play BINGO for the first time. Now, I did my best to prepare him. I gave him the rundown… the pace, the passion, the personalities. I told him, “This is not just a quiet little game.”
And let me tell you, I wasn’t wrong.

First off, let’s clear something up. BINGO is not just an “old person game.” Not even close. The room was filled with people of all ages. Young adults, families, seasoned pros who clearly treat this like a competitive sport. It was a full house of energy, anticipation, and just the right amount of chaos.

And the rules? Oh, they matter.

If the caller goes too slow, people get restless. Too fast? You’ll hear about it. Loudly. If a number is called that doesn’t belong, like slipping an “N” into a B/O-only game, the room reacts like a referee just blew a bad call in the final seconds of a championship game.

There are no quiet corrections. Only passionate ones.

Then there are the setups. Everyone has their system. Their lucky trinkets. Multiple daubers lined up like tools on a workbench. Cards spread out with precision. It’s not just playing… it’s a process. A ritual.
And the chatter.

If you’ve never sat and just listened to a BINGO hall, you’re missing out on one of life’s great shows. Conversations about grandkids, work, life struggles, dinner plans, and everything in between. Some stories are hilarious. Some are heartfelt. Some are just plain random. But all of them matter in that moment.
Sitting there, watching it all unfold, I found myself thinking…

What is it about something this simple that brings people together?

Because at its core, BINGO is just numbers on a card. That’s it. No complex rules. No fancy technology required. Just numbers being called and people paying attention.

And yet, it creates something bigger.

It creates connection.

It creates a space where people show up, sit side by side, and share a moment. They laugh. They complain.

 They celebrate a win like it’s the biggest victory of the week. And for some, maybe it is.

In a world that feels increasingly complicated, where everything moves fast and conversations are often reduced to quick messages and notifications, BINGO feels… refreshingly simple.
It reminds us that we don’t need much to feel connected.

We don’t need perfect conditions or elaborate plans. Sometimes all it takes is a shared experience, a little structure, and a willingness to be present.

There’s something beautiful about that.

It made me think about how often we overlook the simple things in life. The moments that don’t seem big enough to matter. The everyday interactions that quietly shape who we are and how we connect with others.

We chase big milestones, big moments, big successes. And those are important. But somewhere along the way, we forget that meaning is often found in the ordinary.

In a room full of people, a stack of cards, and a voice calling out numbers.

BINGO, of all things, reminded me that life doesn’t always need to be complicated to be meaningful.
Sometimes, it’s about showing up. Paying attention. Sharing a laugh. Getting a little competitive. Maybe even getting a little loud when the caller messes up.

And walking away with more than you came in with, even if you didn’t win a single game.

Because in the end, it was never just about the numbers.

It was about the people.

And maybe that’s the real win.

Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.

title: Finding JOY—The Order That Changes Everything
excerpt: Do you know JOY?
content:
Finding JOY—The Order That Changes Everything

Think about the word joy for a moment. Not the kind that comes and goes with a good day or a lucky break, but the kind that sticks with you even when life doesn’t go according to plan. 

We all want it. We chase it in different ways, hoping the next achievement, the next moment, or even the next quiet day will finally bring it. But what if joy isn’t something you stumble into?

 What if it’s something you build—intentionally, steadily, and with purpose?

There’s a simple way to look at it: J-O-Y. Not just a word, but a way of living that, when you get it right, seems to bring everything else back into focus.

J is for Jesus.

Now stay with me here. This isn’t about checking a box on Sunday morning or posting a verse and calling it a day. This is about foundation. When your life is rooted in something bigger than yourself, something steady and unchanging, it shifts how you see everything else. Storms still come. Hard days still show up uninvited. But there’s a peace underneath it all that doesn’t move. That’s where real joy starts. Not in perfection, but in presence. Not in control, but in trust.
